Together with high-gradient pixel screening, DDBD forms a dual-cue edge screening by assessing directional disparity, effectively discriminating true boundaries from noise. OAWA then accentuates these valid boundaries by weighting the neighborhoods along their dominant gradient directions, guiding the watershed to focus on salient ridges. Extensive experiments on the CytoDArk0 dataset demonstrate state-of-the-art performance across multiple magnifications, improving the accuracy and robustness of adherent cell instance segmentation.

title: A Method to Balance Thermal Distribution of Paralleled Press-Pack IGBTs Based on Gradient Copper-Molybdenum Alloy Ratio Layout
authors: Suhang Wei, Jiaxin Liu, Weishan Lv, Yuan Zhang, Siqi Dai, Xuchen Sun, Cai Chen, Yong Kang, Wei Jiang, Pengyang Wang, Bin Lai
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Press-pack IGBTs (PP-IGBTs) are widely used due to their high power, double-sided cooling, and easy series connection. However, the external pressure on the large-area boss is uneven due to the non-uniform strain caused by the mismatched thermal expansion coefficients (CTE) between different materials. This can lead to problems such as edge warping, temperature concentration, and chip cracking during operation. This paper proposes a method to replace the traditional pure molybdenum plates with a gradient copper-molybdenum alloy ratio layout. The results show that increasing the copper proportion in the central region appropriately can reduce the maximum junction temperature by 1 °C, improve the temperature and pressure uniformity of the module chip by up to 7.47% and 14.12% respectively, along a single path, and by 7% and 2% across the entire module. However, a large gradient can impair the stress transfer mechanism, leading to an excessive level of internal stress. This simple and effective method is applicable for high-power applications of PP IGBTs and scenarios with good heat dissipation conditions.

title: Fault-tolerant Operation of 5L-ANPC Inverter with High Performance Modulation and Control
authors: Pengwei Li, Yi Luo, Ali Bazzi
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Intelligent Design and Control of Automation and Drive Systems (IDCD)
abstract: Multilevel inverters have been adopted in medium power level industry applications due to their merits, including lower dv/dt, topology redundancy, and low total harmonics distortion. Among classical multilevel inverters, a hybrid 5L-ANPC topology embedding a flying capacitor has attracted attention recently. Motivated by applications in single-phase PV system inverters, this paper presents a robust modulation strategy that considers switch-fault conditions and harmonic compensation. The switching-state analysis and phase-shifted (PS) modulation are detailed. Considering open-circuit (OC) and short-circuit (SC) faults in semiconductor devices, a real-time fault-tolerant operation method is proposed and discussed. Its fault-tolerance capability is evaluated and compared with the other three counterparts’ topologies. In the meantime, to improve the quality of the output voltage and reduce AC THD, a neutral-point voltage (NPV) observer is proposed to compensate for fluctuations and reduce AC output distortion. The proposed high-performance modulation scheme is validated in the experimental setup. Comparison results verified the advanced fault-tolerance capability and improved output power quality.

title: Weakly Supervised Segmentation and Classification of Alpha-Synuclein Aggregates in Brightfield Midbrain Images
authors: Erwan Dereure, Robin Louiset, Laura Parkkinen, David A Menassa, David Holcman
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Parkinson's disease (PD) is a neurodegenerative disorder associated with the accumulation of misfolded alpha-synuclein aggregates, forming Lewy bodies and neuritic shape used for pathology diagnostics. Automatic analysis of immunohistochemistry histopathological images with Deep Learning provides a promising tool for better understanding the spatial organization of these aggregates. In this study, we develop an automated image processing pipeline to segment and classify these aggregates in whole-slide images (WSIs) of midbrain tissue from PD and incidental Lewy Body Disease (iLBD) cases based on weakly supervised segmentation, robust to immunohistochemical labelling variability, with a ResNet50 classifier. Our approach allows to differentiate between major aggregate morphologies, including Lewy bodies and neurites with a balanced accuracy of 80 %. This framework paves the way for large-scale characterization of the spatial distribution and heterogeneity of alpha-synuclein aggregates in brightfield immunohistochemical tissue, and for investigating their poorly understood relationships with surrounding cells such as microglia and astrocytes.

title: Calibration-Aware Real-Time 6-DoF Pose Estimation Using ArUco Fiducials with Kalman Filtering Enhancement
authors: Abdullah R. Radam, Ahmed J. Abid, Adel A. Obed, Abbas A. Kareem
publish: 2026 22nd IEEE International Colloquium on Signal Processing &amp; Its Applications (CSPA)
abstract: Accurate pose estimation in real time is of vital importance in the field of computer vision, robotics and automatic navigation. Vision based approaches are in widespread use because of their compact cost and flexibility. ArUco fiducial markers allow efficient six degrees freedom (6DoF) pose estimation on monocular cameras with PnP algorithms. However, practical implementations have a problem with frame-to-frame variations due to image noise, lighting variation and imperfect feature detections, within longer distances and non-zero viewing angles. Limited experimental studies have been made on how the orientation and distance errors depend on range and angular displacement. This work introduces a monocular real-time framework based on a combination of ArUco based pose estimation, Kalman filtering, calibration and correction of the camera and distortion effects. Experimental evaluations on different distances (56.5$150 \mathrm{~cm})$ and angles (0° and 30°) prove the stability improvement by filtering and errors increased with the distance and angular deviation which gives an idea to reliable low-cost vision systems. A demonstration video of the proposed system is available online [14].

title: Enhanced Diagnostic Performance Via Large-Resolution Inference Optimization for Pathology Foundation Models
authors: Mengxuan Hu, Zihan Guan, John Kang, Sheng Li, Zhongliang Zhou
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Despite their prominent performance on tasks such as ROI classification and segmentation, many pathology foundation models remain constrained by a specific input size e.g. <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$224 \times 224$</tex>, creating substantial inefficiencies when applied to whole-slide images (WSIs), which span thousands of resolutions. A naïve strategy is to either enlarge inputs or downsample the WSIs. However, enlarging inputs results in prohibitive GPU memory consumption, while downsampling alters the microns-per-pixel resolution and obscures critical morphological details. To overcome these limitations, we propose an space- and time- efficient inference strategy that sparsifies attention using spatially aware neighboring blocks and filters out non-informative tokens through global attention scores. This design substantially reduces GPU memory and runtime during high-resolution WSI inference while preserving and even improving the downstream performance, enabling inference at higher resolutions under the same GPU budget. The experimental results show that our method can achieves up to an 7.67 % improvement in the ROI classification and compatible results in segmentation.

title: Optimization Methodology for a GaN-based Single-Phase Online UPS Using Combined Variable On-Time and Fixed-Frequency Control
authors: Xuancen Wu, Syed Saeed Rashid, Abdullah Saboor, Khurram K. Afridi
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This paper presents an optimization methodology for a novel GaN-based online Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS). This UPS features a new multi-mode transformerless topology with a single dc bus. Both the rectification and inversion stages of the multi-mode UPS operate in buck, boost and buck-boost modes depending on the line-voltage magnitudes and polarities, enabling universal input/output voltage capability while using 650-V GaN transistors. A control strategy that combines variable on-time and fixed-frequency control within a single line cycle is applied to improve efficiency. The proposed optimization methodology generates multi-mode UPS designs within an expanded design space that includes the transition angle in the combined control between variable on-time and fixed-frequency control within a line cycle, along with other design parameters. Pareto-optimal designs are identified in terms of loss, volume, and cost. The proposed approach yields more efficient and compact designs compared with approaches that assume only variable on- time control or only fixed-frequency control. A 1.2-kVA prototype of the UPS is designed, built and tested. The power density of the main power stage is designed to be 46.3W/in<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>. The combined control is experimentally validated up to 800 W, achieving a peak efficiency of 97.4%, outperforming fixed-frequency control (96.5% efficiency) with a 24.9% experimental loss reduction.

title: DINO-MambaSeg: A Foundation-Guided Network for Patchy Atrophy Segmentation
authors: Siqi Wang, Juexue Wang, Zhuo Deng, Wenbin Wei, Lan Ma
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Pathological myopia (PM) is a leading cause of irreversible vision loss, where patchy chorioretinal atrophy (PA) serves as a crucial biomarker of disease progression. Accurate segmentation of PA lesions remains challenging due to low contrast, irregular morphology, and limited annotations. To address these issues, we propose DINO-MambaSeg, a novel segmentation framework driven by foundation models. Our method integrates a frozen DINOv3 encoder with a hybrid Mamba-ASPP module for joint global-local feature refinement. Furthermore, we incorporate an optic disc exclusion prior into the loss function to suppress false positives and enhance anatomical plausibility. We also construct PA-Fundus, a new pixel-level annotated dataset dedicated to PA lesions. Extensive experiments on PA-Fundus demonstrate that DINO-MambaSeg achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and robustness, validating the effectiveness of combining foundation priors with lightweight structural refinement.

title: Switching Loss Characterization with Uncertainty Quantification for 3.3 kV Monolithic Versus Discrete SiC Bidirectional FETs
authors: Pengyu Fu, Zhining Zhang, Shuwei He, Jin Wang, Anant Agarwal, Stephen A. Mancini, Woongje Sung, Adam J. Morgan
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This work presents a systematic investigation into the switching losses of 3.3 kV SiC bidirectional field-effect transistors (BiDFETs) in common-drain configuration. Hard-switching characterization is performed at 2 kV bus voltage, 5–20 A load currents, and 30–150°C case temperatures. The first quantitative comparison between monolithic and discrete (two-chip) architectures reveals that monolithic integration exhibits higher switching losses due to tighter capacitive coupling. Gate drive configuration impact is also quantified: turn-on energy exhibits negligible sensitivity to single- versus dual-gate driving, while turn-off energy shows measurable reduction under dual-gate operation due to reduced body diode reverse recovery. These findings are enabled by a power-based integration framework with uncertainty quantification, which maintains measurement confidence in the presence of post-switching oscillations extending beyond standard IEC 60747-8 windows. The results provide foundational switching characteristics and architecture selection guidelines for medium-voltage SiC BiDFET implementation in circuits.

title: Retrieving the Design Parameters of Silicon Photonic Devices from Optical Responses Using an Inverse Neural Network Model
authors: Amir Masominia, Daniele Melati, Dan-Xia Xu, Yuri Grinberg, Muhammad Al-Digeil
publish: 2026 IEEE Silicon Photonics Conference (SiPhotonics)
abstract: We present a bidirectional framework for silicon photonic grating couplers, enabling both forward prediction of optical spectra from geometry and inverse retrieval of design parameters from target responses, achieving high accuracy and efficiency across extensive simulated datasets.

title: Federated Learning: A Novel Framework for Parkinson’s Disease Detection
authors: Kausar Ali, Maaz Shamim, Mohd Adnan, Aditya Narendra
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Emerging Computational Intelligence (ICECI)
abstract: Inadequate dopamine production from brain dopamine-producing cells leads to Parkinson’s disease which causes dysfunction of body movements. The main disability factors of this disorder include impaired mobility together with impaired speech and vision difficulties while excretory function disruptions emerge as well. The detection algorithms for Parkinson’s disease based on machine learning and deep learning show noteworthy progress yet they depend mainly on concentrated data collection systems that present major difficulties. The combination of data security risks and medical information exposure to hackers together with excessive data transfer expenses towards centralized repositories results in major patient privacy and data protection implications. The purpose of this work involves implementing federated learning techniques for Parkinson’s disease diagnosis because traditional machine learning methods lack robust data security mechanisms and substantial privacy protections and centralized data computational limitations. The research uses different classification methods in a Federated Learning context to accurately identify Parkinson’s disease through proper resolution of detected limitations. Model implementation and result analysis constitute the final parts of the methodology alongside data collection, preprocessing and exploration stages. The research utilized and evaluated Artificial Neural Network (ANN) together with Ridge Classifier and Logistic Regression. The Ridge Classifier executed as the most effective model by reaching 87.17% overall classification accuracy. The assessment shows that federated learning delivers superior performance when compared to conventional machine learning with minor benefits while overcoming issues of classic machine learning approaches.

title: Explainable AI in Rare Cancers: Lessons from Neuroendocrine Tumors for Disease Modeling
authors: Nowsheena, Shaima Qureshi
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Emerging Computational Intelligence (ICECI)
abstract: Neuroendocrine tumors (NETs), classified as rare tumor variants, are constrained by limited data and complex clinical variables, although their incidence is increasing. The diagnosis and prognosis require interpretable models to support reliable clinical decision-making and explainability. Explainable AI (XAI) addressing the black-box nature of AI provides interpretable insights into model predictions for such rare diseases. The research investigates XAI techniques for identifying key biomarkers, implicated genes, and therapeutic strategies to improve the reliability and clinical applicability of AI-driven decision support. We propose an XAI-driven modelling framework and demonstrate it on the GEO GSE119063 gene-expression microarray dataset by training an SVM classifier with leave-one- out cross-validation and interpreting feature attributions using SHAP and LIME. The model achieves 0.8889 accuracy (recall 0.8; F1-score 0.8889) in tumor vs normal classification, and the explanations highlight the gene features that most strongly drive predictions for transparent decision support. Additionally, the research reveals linked patterns and their correlations with disease prognosis, thereby elucidating that interpretable models function as dependable and practical tools for improving precision medicine in oncology settings with limited data, promoting transparent and credible decision support.

title: Fabrication Tolerant Topology Optimized Multi-Layer Foundry Grating Couplers
authors: Michael J. Probst, Jacob M. Hiesener, Arjun Khurana, Stephen E. Ralph
publish: 2026 IEEE Silicon Photonics Conference (SiPhotonics)
abstract: We experimentally demonstrate a layer restriction method for topology optimization to improve performance in multi-layer structures with unwanted conformal layering. We design grating couplers with and without the layer restriction and demonstrate 0.5 dB closer alignment to simulation in the variant with the restriction technique.

title: An Empirical Evaluation of Open-Source LLMs for Philippine Labor Law Question Answering Using Retrieval-Augmented Generation
authors: Cedric Isaiah D. Bautista, Alisandro N. Domingo, John Paul Benedict R. Monteon, Lysa V. Comia
publish: 2026 22nd IEEE International Colloquium on Signal Processing &amp; Its Applications (CSPA)
abstract: This study evaluates the effectiveness of opensource large language models (LLMs) for answering questions about the Philippine Labor Code using a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework. Three instruction-tuned models-LLaMA 3.1 8B, Qwen2.5 7B, and Gemma 2 9B-were deployed within an identical hybrid retrieval pipeline combining BM25, dense embeddings, reciprocal rank fusion, and cross-encoder reranking. Evaluation was conducted on 30 benchmark queries (27 in-scope and 3 out-of-scope) using metrics including semantic similarity, faithfulness, answer relevancy, citation accuracy, LLM-as-a-Judge scoring, and response latency. Retrieval performance was identical across models (Recall $\approx 0.99$; Precision $\approx 0.31$), while differences emerged in generation quality. Gemma 29 B achieved the highest semantic similarity (0.859), citation accuracy (0.935), and judge score (0.733), indicating stronger legal grounding. Qwen2.5 7B showed the fastest responses (8.89 s) and highest faithfulness, while LLaMA 3.1 8B achieved slightly higher answer relevancy. All models correctly refused 100% of out-ofscope queries. The results demonstrate that RAG-enhanced open-source LLMs can provide reliable and accessible legal information systems for Philippine labor law assistance.

title: Cross-Language Performance and Usability Comparison of JSON, XML and Protocol Buffers in Java, JavaScript and Python
authors: Khaoula Ait Bel Mehdi, Nisrine Lachguer, Mohamed Lachgar, Driss Essabbar
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Global Aeronautical Engineering and Satellite Technology (GAST)
abstract: Selecting an appropriate data serialization method is critical for managing latency, network bandwidth, and system maintainability in modern distributed architectures. In this study, a detailed, cross-platform performance and usability analysis comparing JSON, XML, and Protocol Buffers is provided across three prominent language ecosystems: Java (JVM), JavaScript (Node.js), and Python (CPython). The methodology relies on a consistent e-commerce data schema and standardized workloads with varying payload sizes to evaluate key metrics, including message footprint, (de)serialization latency, operational throughput, and peak memory usage. Beyond raw performance, qualitative usability dimensions such as schema management, code generation requirements, and debugging ease are assessed. It is indicated that Protocol Buffers excels in compactness and speed within Java and Python environments. Conversely, JSON offers an optimal compromise between interoperability and ease of use, showing exceptional performance in Node.js thanks to engine-specific optimizations. XML remains the most resource-intensive format, making it mostly relevant for legacy integrations. These results culminate in a set of practical recommendations designed to help engineers select the most suitable format based on specific performance goals and runtime constraints.

title: Vesselfusion: Diffusion Models for Vessel Centerline Extraction From 3D Ct Images
authors: Soichi Mita, Shumpei Takezaki, Ryoma Bise
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Vessel centerline extraction from 3D CT images is an important task because it reduces annotation effort to build a model that estimates a vessel structure. It is challenging to estimate natural vessel structures since conventional approaches are deterministic models, which cannot capture a complex human structure. In this study, we propose VesselFusion, which is a diffusion model to extract the vessel centerline from 3D CT image. The proposed method uses a coarse-to-fine representation of the centerline and a voting-based aggregation for a natural and stable extraction. VesselFusion was evaluated on a publicly available CT image dataset and achieved higher extraction accuracy and a more natural result than conventional approaches.

title: Vision Guided Bin Picking of Fabric Parts using Deep Learning: A case study
authors: Inês Empadinhas, Mihail Fontul, Hugo Costelha, Carlos Neves, Paulo Jorge Coelho
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Autonomous Robot Systems and Competitions (ICARSC)
abstract: This paper presents a vision-guided bin picking system for stiff black fabric parts used in overmolded polymer injection parts for the automotive industry. The proposed approach combines deep learning-based object detection with classical vision methods to address low contrast and cluttered scenes. A single RGBD camera mounted on the robot end effector is used for picking point detection and verification, supported by a mirror-based inspection stage that avoids additional sensors. Experimental results under varying backgrounds and part densities demonstrate reliable placement and cycle times compatible with industrial requirements. The system's performance was assessed through a "single-fill test" and trials with varying part densities in the bin.

title: Desaturation Protection Design for SiC MOSFETs With a Low (1.5 µs) Short-Circuit Clearance Time
authors: Ravi Teja Pogulaguntla, Arun Karuppaswamy B
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Smart Grid, and Renewable Energy (PESGRE)
abstract: Short-circuit protection is vital for ensuring the safe operation of high-power-density power converters. While desaturation (desat) protection is widely used for short-circuit protection of Si IGBTs, the lower short-circuit withstand time (< 3 µs) requirement makes its design challenging for wide band gap SiC MOSFETs. The desat protection can be adapted for SiC MOSFETs with certain modifications to achieve a lower short-circuit clearance time. This paper provides a comprehensive discussion on adjusting the short-circuit clearance time in the desat protection circuit specifically for SiC MOSFETs. It also presents various commercially available gate driver integrated circuits (ICs) that are compatible with the modified desat circuit. The behavior of SiC MOSFETs under short-circuit conditions, including that at elevated case temperatures, is thoroughly examined, and significant observations are reported. The effectiveness of the designed protection circuit is validated through experiments conducted on a double pulse test fixture.

title: SQL vs NoSQL Database Models: Performance, Scalability, and Application Suitability
authors: Sathishkumar Veerappamplayam Easwaramoorthy, Javid Iqbal, R. Geetha Rajakumari, Shaik Shabana Anjum, Usha Moorthy
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: With the growth of the volume and the variability of the data in this modern world, organizations are challenged by the decision over which database management system (DBMS) to implement for effectively managing and processing data. This paper compares SQL and NoSQL databases, highlighting their distinct approaches to data management. SQL databases, based on the relational model, enforce data integrity and support complex queries, making them ideal for scenarios where data accuracy and transaction integrity are crucial. NoSQL databases, on the other hand, are designed to handle unstructured data and scale horizontally, offering flexibility and speed for big data applications and real-time web services. The paper evaluates key aspects like data creation, manipulation, retrieval, and access control, concluding that the choice of DBMS should align with an application's data type, volume, scalability, and performance needs.

title: Clinalignnet: A Clinician-Interactive Framework for Faithful and Aligned Explainability in MRI
authors: Dost Muhammad, Pinar Akdemir Ozisik, Ayse Keles, Iftikhar Ahmed, Malika Bendechache
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Deep learning models achieve significant diagnostic effectiveness in medical imaging but remain hindered by their lack of explainability. This study presents ClinAlignNet, a clinician-interactive and fidelity-driven explainability framework for brain MRI analysis. The method combines a residual counterfactual mechanism with alignment-based regularisation and uncertainty estimation to produce explanations that are anatomically coherent and clinically faithful. Evaluated on fifty patient cases from the Children's Brain Tumour Network, ClinAlignNet demonstrates superior performance to Grad-CAM and LIME with higher Dice (0.8874), Jaccard (0.8973), XAlign (0.9285), and Explanation Fidelity Score (0.896), while achieving a lower Hausdorff distance (23.96). Qualitative assessment by clinicians and a brain surgeon confirmed that the generated explanations align strongly with tumour boundaries and expert annotations. The framework bridges the gap between algorithmic transparency and clinical reasoning, offering a reliable pathway for the adoption of explainable AI in neuro-oncological diagnosis.

title: Evaluation of Neurocombat and Deep Learning Harmonization for Multi-Site Magnetic Resonance Neuroimaging in Youth with Prenatal Alcohol Exposure
authors: Chloe Scholten, Elyssa M. McMaster, Adam M. Saunders, Michael E. Kim, Gaurav Rudravaram, Elias Levy, Bryce Geeraert, Lianrui Zuo, Simon Vandekar, Catherine Lebel, Bennett A. Landman
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: In cases of prevalent diseases and disorders, such as Prenatal Alcohol Exposure (PAE), multi-site data collection allows for increased study samples. However, multi-site studies introduce additional variability through heterogeneous collection materials, such as scanner and acquisition protocols, which confound with biologically relevant signals. Neuroscientists often utilize statistical methods on imagederived metrics, such as volume of regions of interest, after all image processing to minimize site-related variance. HACA3, a deep learning harmonization method, offers an opportunity to harmonize image signals prior to metric quantification; however, HACA3 has not yet been validated in a pediatric cohort. In this work, we investigate HACA3's ability to remove site-related variance and preserve biologically relevant signal compared to a statistical method, neuroCombat, and pair HACA3 processing with neuroCombat to evaluate the efficacy of multiple harmonization methods in a pediatric (age 7 to 21) population across three unique scanners with controls and cases of PAE with downstream MaCRUISE volume metrics. We find that HACA3 qualitatively improves inter-site contrast variations, but statistical methods reduce greater site-related variance within the MaCRUISE volume metrics following an ANCOVA test, and HACA3 relies on follow-up statistical methods to approach maximal biological preservation in this context.

title: Detecting AI-Generated Text Using Multilingual DistilBERT Fine-Tuning on the Kaggle AI vs Human Dataset
authors: Ahlam Migdady, Riyad AlShalabi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Multidomain Integrated Learning Environments (ICSMILE)
abstract: With the recent developments in the field of Generative Artificial Intelligence, it has become possible for the system to produce text that is nearly indistinguishable from the text written by humans, and this has led to several serious concerns with regards to academic integrity and the authenticity of digital content. This has, therefore, led to the growing need for the development of systems that can detect the difference between text written by humans and text written by AI. This paper proposes a solution to this problem by using an intelligent model for classifying text using the Multilingual DistilBERT model, a lean transformer model that has been designed to retain high language understanding capabilities while still maintaining efficiency and fast inference times. The approach takes a balanced dataset of 20,000 pieces of text, half of which are human-generated while the other half are AI-generated. Experimental evaluation on an unseen set of 4,000 samples showed that the model performed outstandingly. The overall accuracy of the model is 98.22%, and the AUC value obtained from the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve is 0.9980, which is outstanding in distinguishing human-written texts from AI-generated texts.

title: TopoGate: Quality-Aware Topology-Stabilized Gated Fusion for Longitudinal Low-Dose CT New-Lesion Prediction
authors: Seungik Cho
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Longitudinal low-dose CT follow-ups vary in noise, reconstruction kernels, and registration quality. These differences destabilize subtraction images and can trigger false “newlesion” alarms. We present TopoGate, a lightweight model that combines the follow-up appearance view with the subtraction view and controls their influence through a learned, quality-aware gate. The gate is driven by three case-specific signals: CT appearance quality, registration consistency, and stability of anatomical topology measured with topological metrics. On the NLST-New-Lesion-LongCT cohort comprising 152 pairs from 122 patients, TopoGate improves discrimination and calibration over single-view baselines, achieving an area under the ROC curve of 0.65 with a standard deviation of 0.05 and a Brier score of 0.14. Removing corrupted or low-quality pairs, identified by the quality scores, further increases the area under the ROC curve from 0.62 to 0.68 and reduces the Brier score from 0.14 to 0.12. The gate responds predictably to degradation, placing more weight on appearance when noise grows, which mirrors radiologist practice. The approach is simple, interpretable, and practical for reliable longitudinal LDCT triage.

title: Dual-Domain Mapping: Sensor Fusion of UAV-LiDAR and Ground Penetrating Radar for High-Fidelity 3D Utility Mapping
authors: Nurul Izzati Abd Karim, Amer Hamzah Taib, Wan Anom Wan Aris
publish: 2026 22nd IEEE International Colloquium on Signal Processing &amp; Its Applications (CSPA)
abstract: Accurate mapping of underground utilities is essential for safe urban development but remains challenging due to the separation between surface observations and subsurface geophysical measurements. This study presents a sensor-fusion framework integrating Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR) with Ground Penetrating Radar (GPR) to generate a unified threedimensional geospatial representation of surface and subsurface infrastructure. The workflow establishes a semantic linkage between surface utility indicators such as manholes and valve covers extracted from LiDAR data and subsurface pipe or cable reflections detected from radargrams. Field data were collected at Lingkaran Ilmu, Universiti Teknologi Malaysia using a RIEGL VUX-1UAV LiDAR system and an IDS GeoRadar platform. Ground Control Points (GCPs) measured using GNSS were used to georeference both datasets and establish spatial correspondence between the aerial and ground surveys. The integrated processing workflow combines LiDAR-derived terrain modelling with GPR interpretation to enable accurate spatial registration of subsurface utilities. The resulting fused model achieved a Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of 0.08 m, demonstrating sub-decimetre positional accuracy suitable for high-quality utility mapping. The integration also reduced interpretation ambiguity by linking radar anomalies with contextual surface features. Although the test site provided adequate GNSS coverage, the framework can be adapted for GNSS-limited environments through the integration of inertial navigation and SLAM-based positioning techniques. The results demonstrate that combining UAV-LiDAR surface modelling with GPR subsurface detection provides a reliable approach for high-fidelity three-dimensional underground mapping.

title: Integrated Planar Transformer with Embedded Zero-Sequence Inductance for Active Power Decoupling in Three-Phase Dual Active Bridge Converters
authors: Yi Han, Satyam Sa, Olivier Trescases
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: The power density of battery chargers is constrained by the large dc-link capacitors required to buffer double-line-frequency (DLF) ripple power, which limit achievable volumetric power density and lifetime. Active power decoupling (APD) can reduce the capacitor volume requirement, but it typically relies on an additional inductor. The zero-sequence inductance (ZSI) of the three-phase (TP) transformer is shown to serve as the APD inductor in a TP dual-active-bridge (DAB) converter, enabling up to 40% reduction in capacitor volume when buffering half of the ripple power, thereby motivating a practical implementation. A planar TP transformer with integrated ZSI is presented for a 300-kHz GaN-based TP-DAB converter. A five-limb gapped core structure and a low-capacitance PCB winding layout were developed to achieve the required ZSI and support high-frequency switching. The flux distribution was analyzed via finite-element simulation, and the analytical ZSI model was validated using hardware measurements from 10-turn and 14-turn prototypes. Experimental results from a 2-kW dc-dc prototype demonstrate effective ripple-power buffering through the transformer neutral port, with the higher-ZSI 14-turn design halving the APD current ripple and incurring only a 3% efficiency penalty relative to the 96.4% baseline. These results verify that the transformer consolidates power-transfer and decoupling functions, enabling APD without a separate inductor.

title: RL-Driven Proactive Self-Healing in 5G Core Networks Using Semantic Log Anomaly Detection
authors: M. Ayyadurai, Nandha Kumar P, Varun Kumar V, Sujith S S
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: The level of complexity and volume of data of 5G networks make the conventional reactive fault management approach unsustainable. To solve this, we propose a proactive, closed-loop self-healing system which is operated by means of Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) and semantic readings of logs. Our system generates semantic embeddings out of unstructured logs so that we can perform unsupervised detection of anomalies of subtle abnormalities. When a DRL agent notices it, it automatically runs an optimal solution to the problem of remediation that could be component restarts or redirecting the traffic to ensure that service disruption is minimal. Moreover, an integrated Explainable AI(XAI) system offers clear, non-mathematic reasonings explaining to the operator the rationale behind these automated processes. The experimental results prove that the framework is superior and it has a high rate of remediation about 90 percent and a considerable low resolution time compared to the standard baselines.

title: Lesiontabe: Equitable AI for Skin Lesion Detection
authors: Rocio Mexia Diaz, Yasmin Greenway, Petru Manescu
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Bias remains a major barrier to the clinical adoption of AI in dermatology, as diagnostic models underperform on darker skin tones. We present LesionTABE, a fairness-centric framework that couples adversarial debiasing with dermatologyspecific foundation model embeddings. Evaluated across multiple datasets covering both malignant and inflammatory conditions, LesionTABE achieves over a 25% improvement in fairness metrics compared to a ResNet-152 baseline, outperforming existing debiasing methods while simultaneously enhancing overall diagnostic accuracy. These results highlight the potential of foundation model debiasing as a step towards equitable clinical AI adoption.

title: Unsupervised Video Anomaly Detection with Swin Transformer and Temporal-Context Modeling
authors: Yi Sun, Bryan W. Scotney, Xiushan Nie, Shuai Zhang, Xingbo Liu, Lanting Qiu
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: Video anomaly detection has broad applications in public safety, health monitoring, and emergency response. Existing transformer-based methods often struggle to capture multi-scale spatio-temporal patterns effectively. In this paper, we propose an unsupervised video anomaly detection framework that enhances feature representation by combining a 3D Swin Transformer with temporal shift modules and dynamic large kernel (DLK) convolutions. The 3D encoder-decoder structure models normal behavior from video sequences, and anomalies are identified through reconstruction errors. We validate our method on three public datasets: ShanghaiTech, Avenue, and Ped2. Our model achieves competitive accuracy, demonstrating improved capacity for capturing temporal dynamics and contextual details.

title: Physically Constrained Generative Model for Visualizing Climate-Induced Wildfires Using Satillite Imagery
authors: Ahamed Ishaq, Jeya R
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: Effective climate risk communication, environmental assessment, and disaster preparedness all depend on the visual representation of climate-induced wildfires. The need for reliable visualization techniques using satellite imagery is highlighted by the increasing frequency and intensity of wildfires. While recent advances in generative models enable the creation of realistic satellite imagery, unrestricted generation can produce physically unrealistic wildfire patterns, reducing their usefulness in risksensitive situations. In this work, a physically limited conditional image-to-image translation framework for satellite-based wildfire visualization is presented. To direct the image synthesis process, a high-resolution conditional generative adversarial network is integrated with wildfire-specific physical constraints, including vegetation-based indicators and burn severity. The model generates post-wildfire satellite imagery that accurately highlights affected regions while maintaining geographical consistency by conditioning generation on physically meaningful constraint maps. According to experimental results, the suggested framework reduces implausible artifacts frequently seen in unconstrained generative models while producing visually realistic and spatially coherent wildfire visualizations. These results demonstrate how well physically limited generative modeling works for reliable wildfire visualization and climate-related decision support.

title: Evaluation of Monolingual and Multilingual Transformer Models for Nepali Headline Generation
authors: Sunil Dahal, Katsuhide Fujita
publish: ACM Transactions on Asian and Low-Resource Language Information Processing
abstract: None

title: Causality-Driven Test Case Minimisation for Cyber-Physical Systems
authors: Michael Foster, Christopher M. Poskitt, Nicholas R. Latimer, Neil Walkinshaw, Richard Somers, Robert M. Hierons
publish: ACM Transactions on Software Engineering and Methodology
abstract: None

title: CFBoost : Capacity-Boosting Feature Augmentation via Spatial-Geometrical Coordination
authors: Pin Liu, Yutao Li, Ruyue Xin, Yuzhu Wang, Bin Shi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Big Data
abstract: Fine-grained image classification constitutes a long-standing challenge, with its performance bottlenecks stemming primarily from high inter-class similarity and insufficient discriminative feature. Feature augmentation alleviates this constraint through cross-image feature fusion, and as an online augmentation approach, integrates seamlessly into network architectures without requiring independent training phases. However, existing methods typically exhibit either random region selection mechanisms or constrained spatial awareness. Such inherent shortcomings critically constrain their ability to generate adequately capacity discriminative feature necessary for fine-grained classification. To this end, we propose CFBoost, a feature augmentation method that incorporates spatial-geometrical coordination to generate diverse features with sufficient capacity. It adaptively modulates spatial-geometric properties within fusion regions while simultaneously constructing feature-capacity-aware semantic labels. CFBoost integrates three core strategies: precisely locating fusion regions and positions to prevent occlusion of essential features, independently smoothing edges in all orientations of the fusion region to mitigate strong edge effects, and generating soft labels from a semantic weight matrix to improve the representational capacity of sample labels. Experiments across four benchmark datasets demonstrate CFBoost's superiority, achieving an average improvement of 2.48% relative to the optimal baseline, with particularly pronounced performance gains on images containing dispersed high-capacity regions.

title: Deep Acoustic Monitoring for Illegal Logging Detection Using Temporal-Context Aware Sound Event Classification
authors: Sallauddin Mohmmad, Suresh Kumar Sanampudi
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Monitoring illegal logging in forest environments can be achieved through sound event detection. Acoustic detection of tree-cutting activity required to differentiate sound waves over time and detect temporally evolving acoustic patterns. This research proposed a hybrid deep learning framework that integrated the Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCN), Transformer encoders, multi-head attention mechanisms, and Gaussian noise to detect tree-cutting sounds in a forest environment. The TCNs capture local temporal values with dilated convolutions, and Transformer attentions used to find the global contextual relationships for longer time sequences. Gaussian noise introduced to improve robustness against environmental variability. The proposed approach included pre-processing, multi-feature extraction, hybrid temporal encoding, attention-based contextual analysis, and classification using an artificial neural network. The dataset used in this research consists of 10,501 audio samples across four classes: chainsaw, machine saw, axe-cutting (synthetically generated to increase variability), and negative environmental sounds. The results on feature groups show that the proposed TCN–Transformer–Attention architecture achieved 97.48% of accuracy with Group-1 features and 96.20% with Group-5 features. The embedding quality evaluated using temporal and contextual metrics such as Temporal Coherence Kernel (TCK), Contextual Continuity Index (CCI), and Temporal-Contextual Separation Index (TCSI). The TCK obtained value of 0.4136, the CCI value equals to 0.6892, and the TCSI obtained value of 1.9065. These results indicate that the model performed better at preserving temporal stability, contextual continuity, and discriminative separation within the learned embedding space.

title: Controllable FrFT-Driven Y-Net for Cross-Modal Image Fusion Scenarios
authors: Yifan Jiang, Kemin Li, Wenyuan Li, Guoheng Huang, Jietao Yang, Xiaochen Yuan, Xuhang Chen, Bingo Wing-Kuen Ling, Chi-Man Pun, Shunlan Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: Image fusion aims to synthesize high-quality representations by integrating complementary information from multimodal sources. While recent deep learning approaches have explored spectral domain learning to overcome the localized receptive fields of spatial convolutions, they predominantly rely on the standard Fourier Transform. However, two critical bottlenecks remain: standard Fourier bases assume signal stationarity (ill-suited for non-stationary natural images) and lack adaptive frequency-adaptive feature weighting, leading to spectral leakage, ineffective high-frequency-noise decoupling, and texture smoothing. To break these limitations, we propose the Fractional-Order Dynamic Perceptive Y-Net (FDP Y-Net), a unified framework driven by Controllable Fractional Fourier Transform (FrFT) Convolutions. By generalizing the spectral domain, our model adaptively rotates the time-frequency axis to optimally represent non-stationary features and dynamically weights frequency components. Specifically, a Dynamic Perceptive Module spatially localizes salient regions, the Controllable FrFT Block captures time-varying spectral characteristics, and an Information Enhancement Module explicitly reconstructs high-frequency residuals. Orchestrated within a Y-shaped architecture with specialized skip connections, these components ensure holistic feature fusion. Extensive experiments across Infrared-Visible, Medical, and Multifocus datasets demonstrate that FDP Y-Net effectively resolves spectral limitations and frequency weighting deficiencies, achieving state-of-the-art performance in visual fidelity and quantitative metrics. The code is available at: https://github.com/mousi30/FrFT-Driven-Y-Net.

title: Satellite-Based Modeling and Monitoring of Groundwater Storage Using Machine Learning
authors: Dhruv Kukadiya, Trapti Sharma
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Groundwater is disappearing. Not slowly — rapidly, in ways that outpace both natural replenishment and our ability to measure the loss. This paper tackles that measurement problem head-on, describing a pipeline that fuses two freely available satellite products — GRACE gravity anomalies and GLDAS land surface outputs — to track groundwater storage (GWS) changes month by month, across the globe, without a single borehole. The approach subtracts GLDAS-estimated soil and snow contributions from the GRACE total water signal; what remains approximates subsurface storage change. A compact two-layer Artificial Neural Network (ANN) was then trained on these residuals and raw satellite fields, after grid alignment, gap repair via interpolation, and channel-wise rescaling over a twenty-year window from 2002 to 2022. On held-out data, the network hit R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> =0.90, with RMSE and MAE both confirming that the fit is tight. Spatial outputs flagged northern India as a severe depletion zone — matching ground-truth records of agricultural over-pumping. Satellite data alone, it turns out, is enough.

title: Approximate and Weighted Data Reconstruction Attack in Federated Learning
authors: Yongcun Song, Ziqi Wang, Enrique Zuazua
publish: IEEE Transactions on Big Data
abstract: Federated learning (FL) is a distributed learning paradigm that enables multiple clients to collaboratively train a machine learning model without sharing private data. Although FL is normally regarded as privacy-preserving by design, recent data reconstruction attacks demonstrate that adversaries can recover clients' training data from the shared model updates. However, existing attack methods frequently fail in the widely used Federated Averaging (FedAvg) setting, where clients only transmit model parameters after executing multiple local training steps. To overcome this limitation, we propose an interpolation-based approximation method that renders attacks on FedAvg feasible by effectively estimating the intermediate model updates generated during local training. Furthermore, we design a layer-wise weighted loss function to enhance reconstruction quality. Specifically, weights are assigned to different layers based on the neural network architecture and are systematically tuned via Bayesian optimization. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approximate and weighted attack method outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches, yielding substantial improvements across multiple image reconstruction metrics.

title: Robust Wearable Motion Recognition Using a Two-Stage FMG Pipeline with Rest Filtering and Placement Adaptation_supp1-3674514.pdf
authors: Kang-Ho Lee
publish: N/A
abstract: Wearable force myography (FMG) sensors enable continuous motion monitoring through mechanical interaction with soft tissue, but their practical deployment is challenged by rest-related signal ambiguity and placement-induced variability. This study proposes a two-stage Action Discriminator– Action Classifier (ADAC) pipeline that explicitly separates rest-dominant segments from motion-related signals and applies domain-adaptive inference to mitigate placement-induced distribution shifts. Using a lightweight two-channel air-bladder cuff sensor, the ADAC pipeline achieves stable motion recognition under realistic wearing conditions. Under unified cross-participant evaluation with pooled multi-subject data, the proposed framework demonstrates an overall accuracy of 94.9% and an F1-score of 87.9% using cuff-only sensing, without requiring subject-specific calibration. Compared with conventional FMG systems that rely on dense sensing configurations or per-user recalibration, the ADAC pipeline achieves robust performance using only two pressure channels through integrated rest-aware filtering and placement-adaptive classification. These results demonstrate the feasibility of lightweight cuff-type FMG sensors for continuous motion recognition in real-world, multi-user scenarios and underscore the importance of system-level design in pressure-based wearable sensing.

title: FSS-DT: Secure Division-free Decision Tree Training and Inference via Function Secret Sharing
authors: Anxiao Song, Shujie Cui, Ke Cheng, Jianli Bai, Qifan Wang, Shangqi Lai, Giovanni Russello, Yulong Shen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Decision tree (DT), an efficient and interpretable machine learning algorithm, has been widely used in numerous industrial applications. As the demand for higher performance grows, there is increasing interest in cross-domain collaboration for training DTs using vertical datasets, where different owners hold distinct features but share the same set of samples. Due to privacy concerns, leveraging secure multi-party computation (MPC) to train DTs jointly has gained significant attention. Although promising, deploying these approaches across large scale vertical datasets presents formidable challenges for two bottlenecks: divisions and comparisons. MPC is not friendly to process divisions, whereas DT training algorithms involve division to evaluate each feature split for each tree node. DT training and inference also involve numerous comparisons, yet existing secure comparisons are still not efficient enough. We propose FSS-DT, a highly efficient two-party framework for secure DT training and inference on vertically distributed datasets. The efficiency of FSS-DT is achieved by designing a division-free MPC-friendly DT algorithm. Moreover, we present a lightweight secure comparison protocol using our optimized distributed comparison function (DCF), an instance of function secret sharing, via “0/1-encoding” and “early termination” techniques. We have implemented a prototype of FSS-DT. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FSS-DT achieves the same accu racy as models trained in plaintext and outperforms the state-of the-art Pivot and PriVDT. Specifically, for training on LAN and WAN over the large UCI dataset, FSS-DT is 17.5× and 51.1× faster than Pivot, and about 8.4× and 8.8× faster than Swan, respectively.

title: Study on current distribution characteristics of the Bi-2223 stacked-tape cable
authors: R. Shen, J. Jiang, X.S. Yang, X.B. Gao, Y.P. Zhu, J.W. Liu, X.F. Chen, H. Zhang, X.B. Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Applied Superconductivity
abstract: Bi-2223 high-temperature superconducting (HTS) tapes, with their relatively high upper critical field and current-carrying capability, the fine multi-filamentary microstructure, the excellent thermal stability under high magnetic fields and mature manufacturing processes, exhibit significant application potential in large-scale high-field magnets. The current distribution among Bi-2223 stacked tapes is a critical factor governing the stable operation of Bi-2223 stacked-tape cables. In this work, a five-layer Bi-2223 stacked-tape cable sample was investigated using an integrated measurement–inversion–simulation–validation approach, with a focus on the influence of joint resistance non-uniformity on the current distribution in multi-tape stacked cables. An annular Hall sensor array was employed to measure the circumferential magnetic field response of a short stacked-tape cable sample, and the current redistribution within the cable was identified and tracked through features of the derivatives of the magnetic field as a function of current. Taking account of the strongly nonlinear E–J characteristics and the anisotropy of the critical current density of the superconducting material, a two-dimensional finite element method (FEM) based on the H-formulation coupled with an equivalent circuit model (ECM) was used to investigate the effects of the non-uniformity of the joint resistance on the internal current distribution at different current ramping rates. A good agreement between experimental measurements and numerical simulations was obtained. The results provide experimental and theoretical support for joint resistance optimization, uniform current sharing, and current redistribution monitoring in HTS cables, and offer guidance for improving the current-carrying performance and operational stability of HTS stacked-tape cables for large-scale high-field magnets.

title: A Geometry-Scalable DC I–V Calibration Methodology for a Commercial Bulk CMOS Process Design Kit for Cryogenic ICs Operating Near 1 K
authors: Hee-Cheol Joo, Seunghoon Yi, Yoochang Kim, Seung Chae Jung, Hyunwoo Kim, Ryungyu Lee, Seungyun Ham, Hojin Lee, Young-Ha Hwang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: This paper presents a systematic methodology for calibrating a commercial process design kit (PDK) for cryogenic CMOS (cryo-CMOS) integrated circuit (IC) design. Built on the PSP compact model adopted in the baseline PDK, the proposed approach calibrates cryogenic DC I–V characteristics across multiple device geometries through a structured geometry-aware parameter-tuning flow based on cryogenic DC measurements. By leveraging geometry-dependent parameterization supported by the PSP compact model, the methodology preserves compatibility with standard electronic design automation (EDA) toolchains. Using this methodology, a prototype cryo-CMOS PDK (cryo-PDK) is developed by calibrating a commercial 28-nm bulk CMOS PDK with a selected set of tuning-point (TP) devices. Its prediction accuracy at 1.58 K is then evaluated across both the TP devices and additional cross-point (CP) devices, which use an intermediate width and/or length within the TP boundary dimensions and are excluded from tuning. Compared with the baseline PDK predictions at 1.58 K, the proposed cryo-PDK reduces the mean drain-current error, normalized to the measured drain current, by up to 67.8 percentage points for NMOS TP devices, 66.3 percentage points for NMOS CP devices, 40.6 percentage points for PMOS TP devices, and 45.5 percentage points for PMOS CP devices. These results demonstrate substantial error reduction in cryogenic DC I–V prediction and the geometry scalability of the proposed calibration methodology.

title: High-efficiency stacked Blumlein pulse generator using a multi-Boost topology with magnetic coupling suppression
authors: Xiaozhen Zhao, Haodong Wu, Haolin Tong, Shuo Zhang, Jiuxin Ma, Kui Li, Chenguo Yao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: With the expanding application of pulsed power technology in plasma physics, industrial processing, and defense and military weapon systems, the demand for compact and high-efficiency pulse generators has been steadily increasing. Conventional stacked Blumlein pulse generators (SBPGs) often suffer from limited voltage gain and low stacking efficiency due to electromagnetic coupling among transmission lines or parasitic coupling to ground. This paper presents a novel high-efficiency stacked Blumlein pulse generator that integrates a multi-stage Boost DC–DC converter with a magnetic coupling suppression method. The proposed approach directly embeds multiple nanocrystalline magnetic rings into a matching load or a small pre-load resistor, effectively increasing the coupling impedance and minimizing parasitic energy leakage. Meanwhile, the multi-Boost topology raises the charging voltage of each coaxial stage to several times the supply voltage, thereby achieving both enhanced voltage gain and improved stacking efficiency. Simulation and experimental results demonstrate that under a 400 V supply, each stage is charged to approximately 1.06 kV, producing an output voltage of 10 kV with a 49 ns rise time, 118 ns pulse width, and a stacking efficiency of 94.3%. This represents a 20% improvement over the case without the proposed method. The developed generator exhibits high gain, high efficiency, and wide tunability (20 kHz – 1 MHz), offering a promising solution for next-generation compact, solid-state, and high-repetition-rate pulsed power systems.

title: Computational Empathy in Affective LLMs: Architectures, Evaluation, and Clinical Deployment Challenges
authors: Zongtai Wu, Shin Chen, Jian Chen, Zeyu Gao, Hui Bao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Affective Computing
abstract: Mental health systems face a persistent gap between growing demand for support and limited access to timely, affordable care. At the same time, general-purpose large language models (LLMs) are increasingly being used as informal sources of emotional support, despite limited consensus on what empathy in such systems actually means and how it should be evaluated in clinical contexts. In this review, we synthesise recent work on computational empathy in affective LLMs for mental health, drawing on studies published between 2022 and 2026. We trace the field's evolution from rule-based affect detection and deep emotion modelling to contemporary generative agents, and argue that current systems simulate cognitive empathy rather than genuine affective empathy. We further conceptualise computational empathy as a modular pipeline involving affect inference, contextual and relational modelling, supportive dialogue generation, and safety management. Across this pipeline, recent advances include empathetic fine-tuning, reinforcement learning from human feedback, explicit affective modules, memory and rapport mechanisms, multimodal sensing, and agent-based scaffolds for structured therapeutic techniques. However, evidence for clinical readiness remains limited. Current evaluation remains fragmented across technical benchmarks, human ratings, theory-grounded scoring, and safety tests, with no accepted standard linking empathy metrics to patient outcomes. Major deployment barriers include shallow understanding, generic or culturally mismatched support, anthropomorphic over-trust, dependency risks, privacy concerns, and unreliable crisis handling. We conclude that affective LLMs are best positioned as bounded, low-acuity adjuncts within hybrid-care models that preserve safety protocols and human oversight.

title: ANEP: Adaptive Newton Ego Planner for Multi-Waypoint UAV Trajectory Optimization in Constrained Environments
authors: Tiancheng Liu, Ling Bai, David Zou, Zheng Liu
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Roof gutters are highly susceptible to the accumulation of debris, such as fallen leaves, which can block drain outlets and pose a significant threat to the structural integrity of a building. Traditional high-altitude cleaning methods primarily rely on manual operations with ropes, ladders, and extended tools. These methods are not only inherently dangerous, but also inefficient, making them inadequate for building maintenance. The use of drones offers a promising solution for high-altitude cleaning. However, drones still face significant technical challenges in ensuring precise arrival at target points after detecting debris while simultaneously navigating multi-point obstacle avoidance during flight. In particular, autonomous roof gutter cleaning requires robust multi-stage trajectory planning in a cluttered environment where state estimation and waypoint accuracy can be degraded by the noises and inaccuracies from sensing and Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS). This paper presents an Adaptive Newton Ego Planner (ANEP) framework for multi-waypoint trajectory optimization under constrained environments. The proposed ANEP integrates a novel Adaptive Newton-Raphson based optimizer (ANRBO) to enhance exploration and prevent premature convergence during online trajectory refinement. The optimizer leverages Newton-Raphson Search Rules (NRSR) and a Trap Avoidance Operator (TAO), while an online dynamic adaptation mechanism enables efficient multi-point exploration and real-time trajectory optimization. In this paper, we focus on trajectory planning and optimization. Perception, e.g., debris detection, and cleaning actuation were not evaluated in this simulation study. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed trajectory planning method improves multi-stage flight missions by generating smoother trajectories, lower trajectory errors, and a higher mission completion rate. These results suggest that ANEP can serve as an effective trajectory planning component for UAV-based roof gutter maintenance scenarios.

title: Optical Parametric Amplifier-enabled Wavelength-tunable Information Management across IQ Complex Plane
authors: Jiabin Cui, Xuanming Cao, Yanxia Tan, Guo-Wei Lu, Yuefeng Ji, Kunpeng Zhai, Sha Zhu, Jing Xu, Heng Zhou, Zhe Sun, Huashun Wen, Ninghua Zhu
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: An optical wavelength-tunable information management scheme across the IQ complex plane is proposed and verified. Through exploiting the information-power transfer characteristics of optical parametric amplifiers (OPAs), the system achieves simultaneous constellation manipulation and wavelength conversion in a single nonlinear stage. The system realizes the interconnection between on-off keying (OOK) and quadrature phase shift keying (QPSK) signals in both wavelength-maintained/-tunable modes. The operation principle and theoretical derivations of the information manipulation based on OPA are comprehensively elaborated. Signal eye diagrams, optical spectra, constellations, error vector magnitudes, amplitude/phase impact factors and bit error ratios are quantitatively evaluated and characterized to validate the proposed system. Under input optical signal-to-noise ratios of 20 dB and 25 dB, error-free conversions between OOK and QPSK signals at 10G/30G/50G Baud are achieved, and wavelength-tunable conversion between OOK and QPSK at 10G Baud is realized. The scheme provides a flexible, format-transparent solution for interconnection between intensity-modulation direct-detection access networks and coherent backbone systems, paving the way for dynamic resource allocation in future optical networks.

title:
A Dual-mode serial communication controller supporting both I
                    <sup>2</sup>
                    C and SPI protocols
authors: Dhanukonda Sai Sudeepthi, Lakamsani Sri Durga Kavya, Joann Joseph, Nitesh Kashyap
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper describes the design and simulation of a single dual-mode serial communication controller that can handle both Inter-Integrated Circuit (I2C) and Serial Peripheral Interface (SPI) communication protocols. The proposed communication controller eliminates the need for separate dedicated controllers for handling each protocol by providing the capability to switch dynamically between I2C and SPI modes through a mode-select signal. Finite state machine (FSM)-based control logic is employed in the design of the architecture for handling clock synchronization, protocol selection, data transfer, and acknowledgments. A centralized mode control FSM is combined with independent protocol engines for handling I2C and SPI protocols. Verilog HDL is used to describe the entire design and the Vivado design suite is used to simulate it. For both I2C and SPI modes, simulation results confirm proper data transmission, clock behavior, and control signal timing. The proposed dual-mode controller is suitable for embedded systems and system-on-a-chip (SoC) designs that need flexible multi-protocol communication with sensors, memory devices, and peripheral modules because it lowers hardware complexity, area overhead, and design redundancy.

title: Lattice Based Cryptography Implementation in Blockchain Technology
authors: Surendra Kumar Shukla, Vishan Kumar Gupta, Paras Jain, Lipika Goel, Ram Kishan Dewangan, Vikas Tyagi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The lattice-based encryption is one of the most appropriate choices to secure the blockchain technology during the post-quantum era withstanding security against a quantum adversary attack. The authors study lattice-based cryptography and integrate it with the blockchain architecture in order to address gaps that have recently been exposed because of the continued advances with quantum systems. However, a majority of the classical cryptographic designs such as RSA and ECC are designed around the difficulty of particular mathematical problems, one of which is the Shor algorithm, which makes them highly susceptible to quantum algorithms. Learning with Errors (LWE) and Short Integer Solution (SIS). The paper will discuss the feasibility of lattice-based digital signatures, zero-knowledge proofs, and encryption systems that should be deployed in blockchain so that the integrity of data should be maintained, transactions should be secure, and privacy of users should be preserved. Furthermore, it introduces performance features of blockchain where it considers the lattice-based cryptographic systems where it entails scalability, complexity of computing, and latency. The results are then employed to support the options of encryption with lattices to lay the groundwork for the architecture to quantum-resistant blockchain-based frameworks, and the ultimate stage of secure and scalable decentralized networks in the post-quantum world.

title: Deep Koopman Model Predictive Control for High-Precision Tracking of Delta Robots
authors: Haitham El-Hussieny
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: This research presents a novel application of Deep-based Koopman Model Predictive Control (DK-MPC) for high-precision trajectory tracking of the IRB360 delta robot. Unlike traditional modeling approaches that rely on rigid-body dynamics or linear approximations, the proposed method leverages deep neural networks to anticipate Koopman operator representations of the robot's nonlinear dynamics directly from numerical experimental data. The learned Koopman model provides a linear embedding of the system's evolution in a latent space, enabling efficient prediction and optimization within the MPC framework. By incorporating trajectory references and actuation constraints, DK-MPC generates optimal control inputs that ensure accurate tracking while maintaining system feasibility and robustness. Simulation validations on representative point-stablization and trajectory-following tasks demonstrate superior accuracy, and real-time performance. The proposed methodology highlights the potential of Koopman operator theory for bridging data-driven modeling and advanced control of high-speed parallel manipulators, offering a scalable framework for industrial robotics applications.

title: A Unified Downlink Multiple Access Framework: Bridging OMA, NOMA, and Rate-Splitting Transmission
authors: Gagandeep Singh, Nitesh Kashyap, V Narasimha Nayak
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Rate-Splitting Multiple Access (RSMA) has recently emerged as a powerful and flexible multiple access framework for next-generation wireless networks, owing to its ability to efficiently manage multi-user interference. Unlike conventional Orthogonal Multiple Access (OMA), which avoids interference through strict resource orthogonalization, and Non-Orthogonal Multiple Access (NOMA), which relies on fully decoding interference using successive interference cancellation (SIC), RSMA adopts a generalized strategy that partially decodes interference while treating the other part as noise. In this paper, we investigate and compare the performance of OMA, NOMA, and RSMA in a downlink multi-user communication system. A unified analytical framework is developed to fairly compare RSMA with OMA and NOMA under identical system assumptions. Closed-form expressions for the achievable data rates of all considered schemes are derived, and their performance is evaluated through extensive Monte Carlo simulations over Rayleigh fading channels. The numerical results demonstrate that RSMA consistently achieves superior sum-rate performance and improved spectral efficiency compared to both OMA and NOMA across a wide range of signal-to-noise ratios. In addition, at a fixed SNR, RSMA exhibits enhanced robustness to user distance variations, maintaining higher throughput under heterogeneous user locations. These findings establish RSMA as a robust and scalable multiple access solution, positioning it as a strong candidate for future wireless communication systems beyond 5G and towards 6G.

title: An Association Rule Based Approach to Analyze the Effect of Musical Rhythms and Brainwave Responses on Human Emotions
authors: Deng-Ying Liu, Tin-Yu Wu, Chia-Hua Kang, Rui-Chen Xu, Yu-Siang Huang, Yi-Zhen Liu
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Listening to music is widely used for emotional regulation, and different music genres are often adopted in movies to manipulate the audience. It is generally considered that tender slow-tempo music helps people relax and relieve stress to enhance <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha$</tex> wave activity and promote relaxation. Currently, most studies focus on the association between music features and brainwave activity, but few are dedicated to the correlation between one single musical characteristic and brainwave relaxation. This paper collected electroencephalography (EEG) data from 27 participants listening to rhythmic passages from Mozart's “Twinkle Twinkle Little Star” Variations. The analysis showed that a lively and repetitive rhythm significantly helps in alleviating anxiety and tension. This provides a practical guideline for healthy individuals seeking effective, short-term stress relief and mental restoration.

title: SCORA: An Open-Source Coherent SFCW Radar Framework on Low-Cost SDRs for Subsurface Remote Sensing
authors: Hui Huang, Kiyoshy Nakamura, Yangjie Xu, Jin Zhang, Radu State
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: High-resolution Ground Penetrating Radar (GPR) is a critical tool for subsurface remote sensing, but the high cost and strict size, weight, and power constraints of traditional systems limit their widespread deployment, particularly on Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs). While Software-Defined Radios (SDRs) offer a flexible alternative, implementing high-resolution Stepped-Frequency Continuous-Wave (SFCW) radar on low-cost SDRs introduces fundamental challenges: synthesizer phase incoherence caused by independent Phase-Locked Loops and severe data transport bottlenecks. This paper introduces the Stepped-frequency Coherent Radar Architecture (SCORA), an open-source framework designed to transform a low-cost, commercial SDR into a fully coherent SFCW radar for near-surface geophysics. The framework features built-in support for both Continuous Wave (CW) and Linear Frequency Modulated (LFM) sub-pulses, utilizing an extensible architecture that allows users to easily integrate custom, advanced waveforms. SCORA utilizes reference-channel compensation and incorporates system latency characterizations to optimize PRF and prevent spatial aliasing. The framework is experimentally validated through freespace ranging, sandbox landmine detection, and an airborne UAV survey that successfully mapped the buried infrastructure. This open-source release significantly reduces the barrier to entry for agile GPR instrumentation.

title: A Review of Different BLDC Motor Control Strategies in EV Application
authors: Akshatha T S, Aarya Premkumar, Meenakshi C, Pravika M
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: An efficient, reliable, and high-performance propulsion system is essential for electric vehicles (EVs). Brushless DC (BLDC) motors are widely used in EVs due to their compact size, high energy efficiency, high power density, and regenerative braking capability. However, EV operating conditions involve frequent variations in speed, load, and temperature, which challenge conventional control methods and affect performance stability. This paper reviews BLDC motor control strategies for EV applications and classifies them into four categories: conventional, adaptive, robust, and intelligent. Conventional Proportional Integral(PI) and Proportional Integral Derivative (PID) controllers are simple and computationally efficient but are sensitive to parameter variations and nonlinear motor behavior. Adaptive methods such as gain scheduling, model reference adaptive control (MRAC), and self-tuning PID improve performance by adjusting control parameters in real time. Robust control strategies, including sliding mode control (SMC) and disturbance observer based control, enhance system stability under uncertainties and external disturbances. Intelligent approaches, such as fuzzy logic, neural networks, neuro-fuzzy systems, H∞ control, and machine learning (ML) based methods, are also discussed for their ability to manage nonlinear dynamics and optimize performance using data-driven techniques. Each control strategy is evaluated based on its advantages, limitations, and suitability for EV applications. The paper also highlights key future research challenges, including ultra-low-speed sensorless operation, functional safety of Artificial Intelligence (AI) based controllers, and overall motor–inverter efficiency optimization. The aim of this review is to support the development of efficient, robust, and reliable BLDC motor control strategies for next-generation electric vehicles.

title: Postpartum Health Monitoring for Rural Women
authors: S. Devi Sri Mahalakshmi, V. Venkata Subhash, Y. Haswanth Phani Kumar, Alex David S, Prabhu Shankar B, Sakthi Karthi Durai. B
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The problem of postpartum health complication still remains to be a challenge, especially in the rural female population as in the majority of cases they have practically no access to the health facilities, diagnostics, and timely postnatal care. The differences result in maternal morbidity and mortality of the disadvantaged regions. In this paper, the authors offer a detailed data-driven strategy to keep track of and forecast on health indicators of postpartum with the help of publicly available health data and the high-end machine learning methods. To diagnose women at the risk of adverse outcomes, the proposed model will consider such primary clinical and demographic predictors as hemoglobin levels (anemia), systolic and diastolic blood pressure, the type of delivery (cesarean or normal), the presence of preexisting conditions, and the postpartum depression symptoms. The types of classification algorithms that are considered to determine the early warning signs with the highest accuracy are the Logistic Regression, random forest and XGBoost. Such measures as precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC are used to measure the model performance. The interpretability tools, such as SHAP values and feature importance charts, are applied in order to increase transparency. As it is, the predictive system aids frontline health workers and community caregivers by delivering actionable insights presented in the form of simple and understandable outputs. It is important to point out that the results indicate that predictive analytics have a high potential to reduce healthcare discrepancies in rural hospitals by facilitating proactive and personalized postnatal healthcare services. In addition, the proposed framework includes the need to implement AI and data science in rural maternal health programs and, therefore, allow healthcare providers and policy makers to make data-driven decisions. The research will assist in the creation of stable and robust maternal care ecologies in low-resource environments that will ultimately improve maternal and neonatal outcomes in the high-stress postpartum period. The code is located on the GitHub repository: https://github.com/maha-17005/PHM-for-women.

title: Artificial Intelligence for Teaching Social Theories and Decision Making: A Paradigm Shift in Pedagogical Fuzzy Models
authors: Neha Tripathi, Nagendra Singh Gaira, Kamoliddin Badalov, Babaniyazova Nargiza, Niyazmetov Anvar, Narzikulova Dilnoza
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The development of skills in teaching social theories and study methodologies is viewed by academic bodies as critical to worldwide competition.
