abstract: Myelin is a critical factor in brain development, aging, and neurological disorders, including Alzheimer's disease and traumatic brain injury. The ratio of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$T_{1}$</tex>-weighted to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$T_{2}$</tex> weighted (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$T_{1} \mathrm{w} / T_{2} \mathrm{w}$</tex>) MRI intensities is a proxy for intracortical myelin content, but the scarcity of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$T_{2} \mathrm{w}$</tex> MRIs limits the utility of this method in research and clinical practice. We present T2VNet, a 3D autoencoder synthesizing <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$T_{2} \mathrm{w}$</tex> MRIs from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$T_{1}$</tex> w MRIs to allow myelin computation. Trained on 1,033 participants and evaluated on an independent cohort of 140 participants, T2VNet demonstrates strong performance in recovering clinically relevant contrast (MultiSSIM3 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\simeq 0.90$</tex>, PSNR <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$>27 \text{dB}$</tex>). Group-level myelin estimates derived from synthetic <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$T_{2}$</tex> w MRIs correlate highly with those from genuine <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$T_{2}$</tex> w MRIs (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$r=0.75$</tex>), while individual-level correlations confirm subjectwise fidelity (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$r=0.53 \pm 0.094$</tex>). T2VNet lowers acquisition barriers to facilitate population studies of myelination, support research in under-resourced settings, and advance diagnosis for demyelinating conditions.

title: Personalized Federated Learning with Residual Fisher Information for Medical Image Segmentation
authors: Meilu Zhu, Yuxing Li, Zhiwei Wang, Edmund Y. Lam
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Federated learning enables multiple clients (institutions) to collaboratively train machine learning models without sharing their private data. To address the challenge of data heterogeneity across clients, personalized federated learning (pFL) aims to learn customized models for each client. In this work, we propose pFL -ResFIM, a novel pFL framework that achieves client-adaptive personalization at the parameter level. Specifically, we introduce a new metric, Residual Fisher Information Matrix (ResFIM), to quantify the sensitivity of model parameters to domain discrepancies. To estimate ResFIM for each client model under privacy constraints, we employ a spectral transfer strategy that generates simulated data reflecting the domain styles of different clients. Based on the estimated ResFIM, we partition model parameters into domain-sensitive and domain-invariant parts. A personalized model for each client is then constructed by aggregating only the domain-invariant parameters on the server. Extensive experiments on public datasets demonstrate that pFL -ResFIM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

title: SA-UNetv2: Rethinking Spatial Attention U-Net for Retinal Vessel Segmentation
authors: Changlu Guo, Anders Nymark Christensen, Anders Bjorholm Dahl, Yugen Yi, Morten Rieger Hannemose
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Retinal vessel segmentation is a crucial yet challenging task for early diagnosis of diseases such as diabetes and hypertension. SA-UNet achieves promising performance by introducing spatial attention in the bottleneck, but it underuses attention in skip connections and fails to address the severe foreground-background imbalance. We propose SA-UNetv2, a lightweight model that injects cross-scale spatial attention into all skip connections to strengthen multi-scale feature fusion and adopts a weighted Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE) + Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) loss to improve robustness to class imbalance. On the public DRIVE and STARE datasets, SA-UNetv2 achieves state-of-the-art performance with only <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 2 M B}$</tex> memory and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 2 6 M}$</tex> parameters (less than 50% of SA-UNet), and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1}$</tex> second CPU inference on <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$592 \times 592 \times 3$</tex> images, demonstrating strong efficiency and deployability in resource-constrained, CPU-only settings. The code is available at github.com/clguo/SA-UNetv2.

title: Field-of-View–Based Spherical Video Stitching and Object Detection for Multi-Camera Drones
authors: Ghassan Al Jayyousi, Ghaith Al-Refai, Mutaz Ryalat, Hisham Elmoaqet
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Engineering Systems (ICETES)
abstract: This work introduces a panoramic video stitching framework for multi-camera drones aimed at improving situational awareness and object detection in aerial monitoring applications. The system works with three video streams that were recorded at the same time from wide-angle cameras on the left, back, and right. To fix the distortion caused by the 160° lenses, each frame is first geometrically corrected using a field-of-view-based spherical warping model. We use ORB keypoints and RANSAC-based homography estimation to do feature-based alignment. There is also an interactive parameter tuning interface that lets you change the settings for the detector and alignment in real time. After that, a two-stage multiband Laplacian pyramid blending method is used to make a 270° stitched panorama that looks perfect. After that, the stitched frames are processed with a YOLOv5 object detection model to find objects in the larger field of view. Experiments show that the suggested framework works better for aerial perception tasks than single-camera detection. It also maintains the computational efficiency needed for onboard processing.

title: Assessing Privacy Risks in Android Applications Using ML and Explainable AI Techniques
authors: Abeeha Hasan, Sehba Masood
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Emerging Computational Intelligence (ICECI)
abstract: Android applications frequently request many permissions, making privacy risks difficult to measure. The study presents a machine learning framework to assess such risks. It generates a single privacy risk score by combining static features from app manifests and metadata with SHAP-based explainable AI techniques. The process includes: (i) collecting the dataset and cleaning it, (ii) feature engineering from permissions and contextual metadata, (iii) training and evaluating models using multiple algorithms, (iv) SHAP-based explainability, and (v) calculating a weighted RiskScore. 9,872 Android apps were used to carry-out the experiments, which shows that ensemble models like Random Forest and XGBoost can be accurate up to 88.9% and have a macro-F1 score of 0.89, which is 8 10 % better than linear and single tree baselines. SHAP analysis found important permissions like (ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION, READ_CONTACTS, RECORD_AUDIO) as dominant privacy-risk indicators. The proposed method provide a reproducible and interpretable foundation for assessing over-permissioned Android apps and can be used for privacy auditing and policy enforcement.

title: Dual-Domain Hierarchical Adversarial Learning for Semi-Supervised Multi-View Fetal Cardiac Ultrasound Segmentation and Disease Classification
authors: Kerong Zhou
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Accurate prenatal screening for congenital heart disease (CHD) relies on fetal echocardiography, yet clinical interpretation is heavily operator-dependent and hampered by the scarcity of high-quality annotated multi-view data. To address these challenges, we propose a Dual-Domain Hierarchical Adversarial Learning (DD-HAL) framework for the 2026 ISBI Fetal HearT UltraSound Challenge. DD-HAL incorporates three core designs: a multi-view-shared and view-specific multi-branch encoder-decoder architecture, a hierarchical adversarial domain adaptation module, and a Mean-Teacher semi-supervised framework with consistency regularization, which simultaneously addresses multi-view heterogeneity, label scarcity, and domain shift. Our codes will be publicly available at https://github.com/SnapeLocke/DDHAL.

title: UAV-Based Thermal Sensing: Community-Based Learning and Research
authors: Kelvin Kang Wee Tang, Alvin Lau Meng Shin, Tan Liat Choon, Muhammad Hafiz Mohd Yatim, Norhadija Darwin, Nor Alafiza Yunus
publish: 2026 22nd IEEE International Colloquium on Signal Processing &amp; Its Applications (CSPA)
abstract: Today rapid urbanization has intensified urban heat islands (UHI) effects, particularly in the tropical regions where high solar irradiance and limited nocturnal cooling amplify surface heat retention. Consequently, these overwhelming temperatures bring negative consequences to the environmental ecosystem and human health. Surface material properties, specifically those constructed urban pavements and building facades, significantly contribute towards UHI effect due to their thermal properties, including albedo and thermal inertial absorption or solar radiation. Over the years, satellitederived land surface temperature (LST) has been extensively used to characterize broad and long-term thermal variations; nevertheless, micro-scale thermal contributions in tropical urban environments remain poorly quantified. To capture the localized dynamics of heat distribution, unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) or drone equipped with thermal sensors were deployed to generate high-resolution thermal maps with less than $3 \times 3$ centimeters per pixel of a public streetscape in Taman Teratai, Johor Bahru. Surface materials including asphalt, grass, concrete, ceramic roofs, car roofs, water and metal bodies were classified and analyzed. UAV-derived LST was validated against in-situ infrared thermometer measurements using RMSE and correlation analysis. Results demonstrate that industrial zones exhibit significantly higher ambient air temperatures (up to $\mathbf{1 - 5 ^ { \circ }} \mathbf{C}$) compared to residential areas, with metal, ceramic roofs and asphalt surfaces contributing disproportionately to daytime heat accumulation. Strong agreement between UAV-based LST and in-situ temperature data ($\mathrm{R}^{2}=0.91$, $\mathrm{RMSE}=1.87^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$) confirms the reliability of UAV thermal sensing for micro-scale UHI assessment. The findings provide quantitative evidence to support material-based heat mitigation strategies and demonstrate the value of UAV thermal imaging for urban climate studies in tropical environments and also highlights the potential of UAV thermal sensing as a valuable tool for urban microclimate monitoring, sustainable design and climate-resilient city planning.

title: Radiomics LUS Transformer for Liver Tumour Segmentation
authors: Ali Khalife, Mohammad Alkhatib, Erol Ozgur, Emmanuel Buc, Bertrand Le Roy, Youcef Mezouar, Adrien Bartoli
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Accurately segmenting liver tumours in laparoscopic ultrasonography (LUS) is critical for computer-assisted intervention guidance with augmented reality (AR). However, LUS images are often degraded by speckle noise, low contrast, and acoustic artifacts, while the liver's complex anatomy and tumour heterogeneity further complicate segmentation. These challenges are exacerbated by the probe's limited field of view and restricted manoeuvrability, which frequently produce partial or inconsistent tumour appearances across frames. Moreover, isoechoic tumours exhibit echogenicity similar to the surrounding liver tissue, rendering them nearly invisible in individual frames. To address these limitations, we propose RadioLUS, a hybrid deep-learning framework that operates on sequences of LUS frames. It integrates hierarchical visual features from a Swin-UNet backbone with handcrafted radiomics descriptors that capture speckle, texture, and shape characteristics. By aggregating temporal information across frames, the framework enhances weak tumour boundaries and improves the detection of subtle tissue variations. Experimental results demonstrate that RadioLUS outperforms the state-of-the-art under challenging LUS imaging conditions.

title: Weakly-Supervised Lung Nodule Segmentation in CT with One-Click Annotation
authors: Tianyi Qu, Dichao Liu, Ze Jin, Yuqiao Yang, Kenji Suzuki
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Deep Learning (DL) has become a predominant choice for medical image segmentation. However, the requirement for a large volume of precisely-annotated manual segmentation data poses a significant barrier. This study proposes a weaklysupervised approach to mitigating manual annotation labor in lung nodule segmentation in CT. Unlike conventional segmentation models that rely on manual pixel-wise annotations, our approach iteratively refines pseudo labels for 3D semantic segmentation generated from “one-click” input through function maps derived from an Explainable AI (XAI) method. The iterative retraining process using pseudo labels resulted in the performance <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\text{Dice Score} =78.83 {\%}, \text{HD}_{95}= 2.178)$</tex> comparable to the “gold-standard” fully-supervised model trained with manual pixel-wise annotations <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\text{Dice Score} =79.26 {\%}, \text{HD}_{95}=2.335)$</tex>. Additionally, we compared our approach with state-of-the-art weakly supervised methods. Experimental results demonstrated that our model achieved better performance while significantly reducing both parameter count and inference time. These findings highlight the effectiveness of our method for pulmonary nodule segmentation in 3D CT volumes.

title: Self-adjusting LFER-based Dynamic Gate Driving Technique for Fast-Switching GaN HEMTs in 48-V Applications
authors: Xiangyu Teng, Junting Chen, Yuwei Wu, Ji Shu, Kevin J. Chen, Wei Jia Zhang
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This work presents a self-adjusting lateral field-effect rectifier (LFER)-based dynamic gate drive design for 48-V, high-current GaN power stages. Building on our previously reported 400-V GaN LFER-based two-stage driver, the proposed design is adapted for 48-V applications with an additional gate-to-source resistor, which modulates the effective resistance (R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">EFF</inf>) of the LFER branch as the gate current changes. Immediately after the turn-on, R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">EFF</inf> remains low and subsequently increases as the branch current grows. This self-regulating behavior allows fast switching transient while suppressing the gate ringing and shortens the post-switching damping interval. These features are critical for MHz range switching. Verified in a double-pulse test configuration, the proposed self-adjusting scheme has exhibited improved reduction in damping charge time by 25% compared to the prior LFER-based driver. The resulting gate overshoot has been lowered by 4.8% without compromising the fast turn-on time of 45 ns. These results indicate a higher switching frequency and lower EMI can be achieved for the 48-V converters at the multi-MHz frequencies.

title: Towards Fully Integrated Supercontinuum Sources in Silicon Photonics Platform
authors: Yijun Yang, Maria-Camila Diaz-Sanchez, Ilyes Betka, Etienne Herth, David Bouville, Samson Edmond, Jean-René Coudevylle, Daniele Melati, Delphine Marris-Morini, Quentin Wilmart, Carlos Alonso-Ramos, Benjamin Wetzel, Arnaud Mussot, Eric Cassan, Laurent Vivien
publish: 2026 IEEE Silicon Photonics Conference (SiPhotonics)
abstract: Supercontinuum generation (SCG) is essential for a wide range of applications, including metrology, imaging, spectroscopy, and optical communications. Although broad and efficient SCG has been demonstrated in integrated photonic platforms, most implementations rely on high-power and bulky pulsed laser sources. This paper reports recent advances toward fully integrated SCG sources including advances in on-chip SCG and pulse compression.

title: Semi-Supervised Carotid Plaque Segmentation with Multi-Scale Deep Supervision and Consistency Learning for Multi-View Vulnerable Plaque Classification
authors: Long Yang, Xiaoyin Chen, Bing Wang, Pengpeng Ou, Shaomin Niu, Peipei Zhang, Zeping Gao, Teng Ma
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Carotid plaque vulnerability is closely associated with cerebrovascular events, making accurate plaque analysis important for clinical risk assessment. In this study, we propose a framework for automated carotid ultrasound plaque analysis integrating semi-supervised segmentation and classification. A multi-scale deeply supervised dual-view DenseNet model with a UniMatch-based semi-supervised strategy was used for plaque segmentation, enabling effective utilization of labeled and unlabeled data. Radiomic features extracted from plaque, lumen, and union regions were used for vulnerable plaque classification with TabPFN and semi-supervised training. Evaluation on ultrasound examinations yielded a segmentation score of 61.72 and an F1-score of 63.83, with an efficient processing time of 163.54 ms per case (time score: 87.61), securing 7th place overall and demonstrating the potential of our framework for automated plaque analysis.

title: A Single-Phase Non-Isolated Six-Switch Buck-Boost Inverter using GaN Power Devices
authors: Halladi Shashwatha Kumara Kedlaya, Saleh Farzamkia, Vishal Kumar Meena, Alex Q. Huang
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This paper proposes a novel single-stage non-isolated single-phase inverter based on a six-switch buck-boost (SSBB) converter followed by an unfolding bridge. Due to the flying capacitor in the boost-stage of the SSBB, the converter can achieve high gain with small passive components, leading to higher power density. Moreover, single-stage topology reduces switching losses and enables high power efficiency. In this paper, the operating modes and modulation techniques of the SSBB for generating a half-sine voltage are explained. The performance of the proposed inverter and its modulation technique is validated through simulation and experiments.

title: Data-Efficient Motor Condition Monitoring with Time Series Foundation Models
authors: Deyu Li, Xinyuan Liao, Shaowei Chen, Shuai Zhao
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Motor condition monitoring is essential for ensuring system reliability and preventing catastrophic failures. However, data-driven diagnostic methods often suffer from sparse fault labels and severe class imbalance, which limit their effectiveness in real-world applications. This paper proposes a motor condition monitoring framework that leverages the general features learned during pre-training of two time series foundation models, MOMENT and Mantis, to address these challenges. By transferring broad temporal representations from large-scale pre-training, the proposed approach significantly reduces dependence on labeled data while maintaining high diagnostic accuracy. Experimental results show that MOMENT achieves nearly twice the performance of conventional deep learning models using only 1% of the training data, whereas Mantis surpasses state-of-the-art baselines by 22%, reaching 90% accuracy with the same data ratio. These results demonstrate the strong generalization and data efficiency of time series foundation models in fault diagnosis, providing new insights into scalable and adaptive frameworks for intelligent motor condition monitoring.

title: A Master-Slave Gate Driver Architecture for Paralleling of Medium-Voltage SiC MOSFET Power Module With Oscillation Feedback Path Blocking
authors: Zhixing Yan, Gao Liu, Hongbo Zhao, Stig Munk-Nielsen
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Gate oscillations in paralleled SiC MOSFET power modules occur during switching transients, potentially causing false switching behavior and device damage. In our previous work [1], mitigation strategies by adjusting parasitic inductances were proposed. However, as the number of paralleled modules increases, mechanical design constraints limit the effectiveness of optimizing parasitic distribution. To overcome this a master-slave gate driver architecture that simplifies the gate-driver circuit for multiple paralleled modules is introduced in this paper. Blocking inductors and damping resistors are incorporated into the slave driver board to suppress oscillation feedback paths. Experiments on a double pulse test platform with three paralleled 10 kV modules confirm the architecture’s effectiveness.

title: A Unified Off-Board Charging System for Integrated Wired and Wireless EV Charging
authors: Cheol-Hee Jo, Subhajyoti Mukherjee, Mostak Mohammad, Omer Onar
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This work explores a unified off-board charger approach that enables both wired and wireless charging within a single system architecture. Instead of deploying two independent converters, the proposed configuration switches between an LLC-based operation for wired charging and an LCC-S–type operation for wireless charging by rearranging the excitation of commonly used components. With this reconfiguration, the same switch bridge, resonant elements, and magnetic coupling coils can be utilized in both modes without relying on relays or additional hardware paths. To assess the basic operation of the proposed system, PLECS circuit simulations were performed for both wired and wireless charging modes. A 3.3-kW prototype was also built, and representative experimental results for each mode confirm that the unified converter can operate properly while reducing hardware duplication.

title: Attenuating Carryover Effects in Rapid Cycle Gas Sensing via Active Purging and Optimized Chamber Design
authors: Abdulnasser Nabil Abdullah, Kamarulzaman Kamarudin, Md Ashequl Islam, Syed Muhammad Mamduh Syed Zakaria, Zaffry Hadi Mohd Juffry
publish: 2026 22nd IEEE International Colloquium on Signal Processing &amp; Its Applications (CSPA)
abstract: Confined spaces are susceptible to oxygen deficiency and the accumulation of toxic or flammable gases due to limited ventilation, posing significant risks to workers and emergency responders. However, gas sensor modules operating in such environments often experience residual gas retention within the sensing chamber, which leads to delayed recovery, baseline drift, and reduced measurement reliability. This study presents the design and experimental validation of an enhanced purging technique for a gas sensor array system intended for confined-space hazard detection. The proposed system integrates an optimized sensor chamber, a multi-sensor metaloxide (MOX) sensor array, and a controlled air-purging mechanism to mitigate gas carryover and stabilize sensor responses. System performance was evaluated under two purging conditions: ambient air purging and purified air purging using an active carbon filter. Sensor response data were analysed using average response comparison and multivariate statistical analysis based on Principal Component Analysis (PCA). The PCA results demonstrate strong gas discrimination capability, with the first two principal components explaining $\mathbf{9 2. 1 6 \%}$ of the total variance. Experimental results show that the carbon-filtered purging method significantly improves baseline recovery, response repeatability, and measurement stability compared with conventional ambient purging. The proposed approach improves gas sensing stability and baseline recovery under the tested laboratory conditions and provides a practical basis for further development of confined-space atmospheric monitoring systems.

title: DICOM-CLIP: Zero-Shot Acquisition Parameter Retrieval from Unprocessed Dicom Files
authors: Prahlad Anand, Aaron Carass, Ellen M. Mowry, Scott D. Newsome, Jerry L. Prince, Blake E. Dewey
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Contrast variation in magnetic resonance (MR) images arises from acquisition-specific parameters such as echo time, repetition time, and flip angle, which vary widely across individual acquisitions and hardware. These parameters are included in imaging metadata tags, but not often used for image categorization. Instead, broad labels such as ” <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{T}_{1}$</tex>-weighted” and ” <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{T}_{2}$</tex>-FLAIR” are commonly used, but the resultant images exhibit wide intra-label contrast variation. Creating anatomy-invariant contrast representations that are faithful to intra-contrast variation is valuable for image contrast identification and downstream tasks such as image harmonization. However, in large-scale real-world acquisitions, metadata is often erroneous, inconsistent, or missing, making interpretation difficult and complex. To recover these parameters and learn fine-grained representations of MR contrast, we propose DICOM-CLIP, a multimodal contrastive learning framework that embeds raw DICOM slices in the same space as their associated metadata. DICOM-CLIP is trained in a single-stage supervised contrastive (SupCon) setting using ground-truth metadata, optimizing embedding separability using exact-match supervision. Our approach yields up to 6% more accurate retrieval and up to 23 % improvement in Mean Average Error (MAE) over the current state-of-the-art.

title: Modulation Method for Reducing DC-Link Current Ripple in Open-End Winding Motor Drive Systems
authors: Kota Sato, Ryusei Yamada, Kenta Emori
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This paper proposes a new modulation method that achieves a substantial reduction in DC-link current ripple for three-phase full-bridge inverters driving open-end winding motors. Although various ripple-suppression techniques have been proposed, most are designed for three-phase half-bridge inverters driving star-connected winding motors and cannot be applied to three-phase full-bridge inverters driving open-end winding motors due to fundamental differences in DC-link current behavior and the strict requirement for suppressing zero-sequence current. To overcome these limitations, this study analyzes the distinctive characteristics of DC-link current ripple in full-bridge inverters and the constraints imposed by zero-sequence current control and then proposes a modulation method that resolves both challenges simultaneously. The proposed method achieves a remarkable ripple reduction exceeding 70% across the practical operating range of traction motors, enabling a significant reduction—approximately 40%—in DC-link capacitor volume based on analytical evaluation. Importantly, the method can be fully compatible with zero-sequence current suppression and employs a carrier-based formulation, ensuring low computational burden suitable for real-time implementation without requiring any hardware modifications. The validity of the proposed approach is further confirmed through experiments on a full-scale prototype, demonstrating strong agreement with analytical predictions and verifying its effectiveness in practical applications.

title: PhysHVS-LDCT: Dose-Controllable Physics-Based Low-Dose CT Synthesis for Benchmarking and Data Augmentation
authors: Yousra Taifour, Marouane Tliba, Nour Aburaed, Azeddine Beghdadi, Faouzi Alaya Cheikh, Aladine Chetouani, Zuheng Ming, Rachid Jennane
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Reducing radiation exposure in Computed Tomography (CT) remains a clinical priority, particularly for radiosensitive groups such as pediatric and pregnant patients. Although Low-Dose CT (LDCT) reduces radiation-related risk, lowering the tube current increases noise and artifacts that can compromise diagnostic quality. Progress in LDCT reconstruction is further limited by the scarcity of paired full-dose and low-dose data, as ethical constraints prohibit multi-dose acquisitions. Moreover, most public CT datasets contain only clinical-dose scans, restricting their use for low-dose imaging research. To address this limitation, we introduce a benchmarking-oriented generative framework for synthesizing realistic LDCT images from existing clinical-dose data. We further present PhysHVS-LDCT, a dose-controllable synthesis pipeline designed as an effective dataset augmentation tool. By utilizing both physics-based and perceptual priors informed by human contrast sensitivity, PhysHVS-LDCT produces LDCT images that closely match real low-dose scans. Our results highlight that PhysHVS-LDCT achieves superior performance across all dose levels when compared with SOTA GAN- and VAE-based baselines, producing more accurate and realistic LDCT simulations.

title: Human-Machine Collaboration in Cybersecurity Operations for Faster Threat Response
authors: Rajesh Tiwari, Ramgopal Kashyap, Gopi Mannava, Pawan Kumar, Balpreet Singh, Sruthi P
publish: 2026 International Conference on Multidisciplinary Innovations For Smart &amp; Sustainable Future (MISSF)
abstract: The paper provides a human-machine working model that would enhance speed of threat investigation during internet security missions. The rising sophistication and pace of cyberattacks require solutions that would integrate the analytical pace of automated solutions, to the contextual reasoning of human specialists. To help solve the problem, the researcher suggests the Human-Machine Collaborative Threat Response (HMCTR) approach, which incorporates the machine-based threat response (detection, prioritization, and alert correlation), and the validation of the human-in-the-loop (decision) alongside the refinement of the response. The suggested approach allows the large-scale security data to be processed by automated tools in real time, and a human analyst to give a level of strategic judgment to decrease the false positive and increase the accuracy of response. The researcher undertakes the evaluation of HMCTR based on operational analysis improvement of the response time, situational awareness and the mitigation effect have been improved against fully automated response or human-only. The findings suggest that the collaborative model would greatly decrease the delay in threat response and contribute to how the adaptive defense mechanisms are applied in the dynamic cyberspace. The paper concludes that HMCTR provides a flexible and effective model of the current cybersecurity operations through synergistic human strengths and machine strengths. This framework will be very appropriate in the security operation centers that require faster and more dependable means of handling a threat in high volumes of alerts. The suggested approach attains a comprehensive detection accuracy of 97.4%.

title: GarmentRec: towards Individual Garment Reconstruction from a Monocular Human Image_supp1-3691682.pdf
authors: XB Gao
publish: N/A
abstract: Reconstructing high-quality garment models from monocular images is important as it provides a practical and effective solution for human digitization and virtual try-on etc. Recent implicit function-based garment reconstruction methods recover free-form geometry but struggle to reconstruct individual garment meshes from human images and tend to produce disembodied limbs or degenerate shapes for novel views. In contrast, explicit parametric garment template models can be utilised to construct separate meshes and constrain the shape reconstruction robustly. However, this limits the reconstruction of garment details and shape variations, such as the wrinkles and pockets etc. To address this problem, in this paper, we introduce a novel explicit garment template that is designed for both closed and open garment topology. Powered by our new garment template, we further propose a detailed garment reconstruction method based on a monocular view that can process both the closed and open types for shape recovery. To capture those challenging parts with unknown geometry and topology, we predict displacement maps on the parameterization domain for the target garment from the monocular image and elaborate it to the 3D garment surface via the UV coordinates, achieving realistic details on the 3D garment shape. Extensive experiments demonstrate the accuracy and robustness of our method and show that realistic details like garment wrinkles and pockets can be faithfully recovered in an explicit way. The code and dataset are available at https://github.com/worryDes/GarmentRec.

title: Recovering Reward Functions From Distributed Expert Demonstrations via Bi-Level Maximum-Likelihood Optimization_supp1-3688600.pdf
authors: Tian Lan
publish: N/A
abstract: Inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) seeks to infer the latent reward function and the associated optimal policy from expert demonstrations. However, most current IRL methods assume centralized access to all trajectory data, which is impractical in real-world scenarios characterized by decentralized data sources and privacy concerns. To this end, this article proposes a novel algorithm for federated maximum-likelihood IRL (F-ML-IRL) and provides a rigorous analysis of its convergence rate. The proposed F-ML-IRL leverages dual aggregation to update the shared global model and performs bi-level local updates: an upper level learning task to optimize the parameterized reward function by maximizing the discounted likelihood of observing human expert trajectories under the current policy, and a lower level learning task to find the optimal agent policy regarding the entropy-regularized discounted cumulative reward under the current reward function. We analyze the convergence rate of the proposed F-ML-IRL algorithm and show that the global model in F-ML-IRL converges to a stationary point for both the reward and policy parameters within finite time. That is, the log-distance between the recovered policy and the optimal policy, as well as the gradient of the likelihood objective, converges to zero. Evaluating our F-ML-IRL algorithm on high-dimensional robotic control tasks in MuJoCo, we show that it ensures convergence of the recovered reward in decentralized learning and outperforms centralized baselines due to its ability to utilize distributed data—attaining better recovered rewards than all baselines in 12 out of 20 tasks.

title: Inverse Laplacian Pyramid For Image Generation With Limited Data_supp1-3694739.pdf
authors: Yikui Zhai
publish: N/A
abstract: This paper demonstrates that a robust and data-efficient image generation model can be achieved by simulating the inverse Laplacian pyramid through a neural network. The key insight is that under appropriate parameterization, the score function of this neural network is approximately proportional to the negative high-frequency residuals in the forward degradation process. We refer to this framework as the Score-Matching Adversarial Model (SMAM). The model consists of two main components: the Laplacian Pyramid Discriminator (LPD) and the Inverse Laplacian Pyramid Generator (ILPG). The LPD includes a Laplacian pyramid decomposition module, which enables both the forward degradation process and multi-scale image discrimination for real and generated images. The ILPG, in turn, generates the prior of the forward degradation process as well as the high-frequency residuals needed for the inverse Laplacian pyramid reconstruction. This allows the Laplacian pyramid reconstruction module to recover a complete image from the generated data. SMAM is grounded in a solid theoretical framework and incorporates a strong coarse-to-fine inductive bias. The model’s inherent multi-scale nature and its ability to disentangle global color and shape features contribute to its superior performance in data-limited settings. SMAM significantly outperforms baseline model, and it performs on par with other leading methods in comparative experiments. Extensive experiments prove the effectiveness and superiority of SMAM. The code can be found at https://github.com/yikuizhai/SMAM.

title: HarmonicLIME: Frequency-Aware Local Explanations for Deep Music Genre Classification_supp1-3694788.pdf
authors: Sridharan Sankaran
publish: N/A
abstract: Deep learning models for music genre classification achieve high accuracy but remain opaque, producing decisions that domain experts cannot interpret or audit. Existing post-hoc explainability methods such as LIME and SHAP operate on raw mel-spectrogram bins, yielding saliency maps perceptually meaningless to musicians. We propose HarmonicLIME, a music-aware local explanation framework that perturbs audio in the harmonic-percussive-residual (HPR) decomposed space, producing component-level saliency maps aligned to tonal and rhythmic musical structure. Applied to a VGGish classifier fine-tuned on GTZAN (83.0% accuracy), HarmonicLIME achieves a mean Deletion AUC of 0.1990 (excluding the Classical anomaly), outperforming Mel-LIME (0.2127) and SHAP (0.2582), with statistically significant superiority on Country and Reggae (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$p < 0.001$</tex-math></inline-formula>, Wilcoxon signed-rank). The proposed Harmonic Localization Ratio reveals genre-consistent saliency structure: Hip-Hop concentrates 81.5% of saliency in the percussive component while Country assigns 76.9% to the harmonic component. Zero-shot transfer to FMA-small confirms cross-dataset generalization, independently replicating Clever Hans diagnostic findings on a structurally distinct genre taxonomy. Code is publicly available to facilitate reproducibility.

title: APEC 2026 Contributor Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)

title: Deep learning framework on physics-model data for accelerated design of nanoarray photocathode_supp1-3694453.docx
authors: Sihao Xia
publish: N/A
abstract: Photoemission models are widely used for the structural design and physical analysis of photocathodes. However, the computational cost becomes prohibitive due to the complex equation-solving process when applied to nanostructured photocathodes, particularly for large-scale nanoarrays. In this work, we introduce a hybrid deep learning framework that employs a convolutional neural network (CNN) trained on data generated by rigorous three-dimensional photoemission simulations. The network is first pretrained on photocurrent data from small-scale nanoarrays (dimensions≤100 nm), demonstrating its capability to capture the underlying photoemission physics embedded in the simulation data. A lightweight transfer learning strategy using only hundreds of larger-scale samples then generalizes the Photoemission CNN (PECNN) to broader geometries. This approach reduces the average prediction error from 36.82% to 2.91% for emitted photocurrent and from 54.80% to 3.34% for collected photocurrent. Concurrently, the framework achieves computational speedups of approximately 1.6×103 for small nanoarrays and 1.5×104 for larger systems compared to traditional numerical solvers. Post-hoc interpretability analyses, including SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP), Permutation Importance (PI), and Partial Dependence Plots (PDP), identify array spacing, nanorod diameter, and substrate thickness as the dominant parameters governing emission performance, and the learned relationships align consistently with established photoemission theory. By delivering cross-scale, interpretable, and ultra-fast predictions, the PECNN framework provides a powerful tool for the rational design and optimization of advanced photoemission devices.

title: 200-Mbps/7-m LED-to-LED Underwater Wireless Optical Communication with Dynamic Topology Reconfiguration
authors: Yuxin He, Xiaoxu Ma, Yufan Zhang, Wendong Liao, Chengye Cai, Baokang Chen, Haipeng Wang, Shaoda Yuan, Wenwen Guo, Jing Xu
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: In this paper, a light-emitting diode (LED)-to-LED underwater wireless optical communication (UWOC) system based on a commercial 4 × 4 red LED array is proposed. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to utilize dynamic topology reconfiguration for realizing transceiver multiplexing with the same LED array. Specifically, the 16-LED array is configured in series during transmission to achieve high optical power, and reconfigured into four branches, each consisting of 4 LEDs, during reception to improve the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) via diversity reception. Equivalent circuit models are established to describe the frequency response behavior of the LED in both transmission and reception modes. Based on the proposed model, a pre-equalizer is designed to flatten the system frequency response, extending the 3-dB bandwidth from 3.29 MHz to 23.23 MHz. Experimental results in a 7-m water tank demonstrate a 200-Mbps on-off keying (OOK) transmission, achieving the highest rate-distance product among LED-to-LED wireless optical communication (WOC) systems to date. Furthermore, diversity reception based on maximal ratio combining (MRC) yields a 17.6% improvement in data rate compared to the single-channel reception. In addition, the system demonstrates robust performance under harsh link conditions. It maintains reliable operation in turbid water with an attenuation coefficient of 2.4 dB/m and bubble-induced turbulence, while accommodating a wide misalignment tolerance of 24 cm. Given its high integration, cost-effectiveness, and robustness, the proposed scheme offers a promising solution for resource-constrained nodes in distributed underwater sensor networks and the Internet of Underwater Things (IoUT).
