title: Foundation Model Enhanced Hierarchical Learning for White Blood Cell Classification
authors: Fan Xiao, Zirui Chen, Jilan Xu, Junlin Hou
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: White blood cell (WBC) classification is essential for diagnosing hematological disorders, but automated methods face challenges from data heterogeneity and class imbalance. In this work, we propose a hierarchical deep learning framework for 13-class WBC classification. To address domain shift from various imaging conditions, we fine-tune foundation models and apply pseudo-labeling on unlabeled test samples. For severe class imbalance, we introduce a specialized pipeline combining binary voting and exemplar matching to improve rare subtype recognition. Our method achieves a macro F1 score of 0.77682 on the WBCBench 2026 dataset, demonstrating its effectiveness on heterogeneous data and clinically significant minority classes.

title: Bipolar Boost Converter for Transformerless Battery Energy Storage Integration in the Medium Voltage Distribution Grid
authors: Sabrina R. Helbig, Jacob A. Mueller, Jack Flicker, Brandon M. Grainger
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: A bidirectional bipolar boost converter was studied for integration of utility-scale battery energy storage systems (BESS) in medium voltage (MV) distribution grids. The converter accommodates the lower voltages produced by BESS and transforms them to a DC voltage level suitable for direct (transformerless) interconnection to an MV point of connection. For the application of BESS integration, this work analyzes the bipolar boost converter topology; develops a PI-based, dual-loop control method to track a voltage reference and to balance DC link capacitor voltages; and derives a representative dynamic model of the system. The dynamic model and control approach are validated in a scaled-down 80 V : 400 V, 1 kW prototype of the MV storage interface converter.

title: Fine-Tuning CLIP Models with Convolutional Features for Medical Anomaly Detection
authors: Jianfeng Fu, Minglong Xue
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: Despite the significant success of large-scale vision-language pre-training models in zero-shot and few-shot anomaly detection for natural images, their effectiveness is sharply limited in the medical domain by the substantial discrepancy between natural and medical image data. To address this domain shift, This study presents a new, lightweight framework named Multi-Level Convolution Adaptation (MLCA), which is designed for medical anomaly detection by fine-tune the CLIP model. Our methodology enhances the pre-trained visual encoder by integrating multiple residual adapters to progressively refine visual features across various layers. Furthermore, we implement a ConvTokenizer to align CLIP’s image tokens directly with the raw input information, thereby preserving critical original details. This adaptation process is rigorously supervised by a pixel-level visual-text alignment loss function, which deliberately recalibrates the model’s attention away from recognizing object semantics towards the accurate recognition of anomalies in medical images. The resulting adapted features exhibit markedly enhanced generalization across diverse medical data types, yielding promising zero-shot capabilities even when the model engagement sentirely novel medical modalities or underexplored anatomical regions during inference. Experimental validation on standard MAD benchmarks confirms that MLCA surpasses existing SOTA models, showing the average value of AUC improvement of 2.44% and 1.18% for anomaly classification under zero-shot and few-shot conditions, respectively, and corresponding average AUC gains of 1.33% and 0.57% for anomaly segmentation.

title: Face Recognition Using a Hybrid InceptionV3-Based Simple Neural Network and Autoencoder
authors: Mohammed Chalwi, Ali Muwafaq Shaban, Roaa Chalwi, Muthanna Mishlish, Mohammed Ahmed Salih, Mohammed M. Al-Ani
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Multidomain Integrated Learning Environments (ICSMILE)
abstract: This paper ultimately proposed a hybrid deep learning (DL) framework for face recognition that incorporates InceptionV3 for powerful feature extraction with an autoencoder layer to reduce the dimensionality of the representation. A simple neural network classifies the extracted compact feature representations and enables effective discrimination among fake and real face images. We utilize a large-scale dataset of fake and real face images obtained from Kaggle sources, consisting of 140000 such images. The proposed approach achieved high accuracy of 95.73%, outperforming several existing deep-fake detection approaches. The main contributions of this work contain the development of a hybrid InceptionV3–autoencoder feature extraction architecture, effective reduction of feature dimensionality while preserving discriminative information, and comprehensive evaluation demonstrating superior performance over related methods.

title: Towards Robust Urban Obstacle Perception for Visually Impaired Navigation: A Dataset and Baseline Study
authors: Maryam Koraichi, Samira Chabaa, El Mehdi Cherrat, Abdelouhab Zeroual, Mohamed Zarboubi
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Global Aeronautical Engineering and Satellite Technology (GAST)
abstract: Urban environments pose significant mobility challenges for visually impaired individuals due to the presence of diverse obstacles and complex scenes, while many existing assistive navigation approaches rely on generic datasets or simplifying assumptions that limit their applicability to real-world urban use. This paper presents a foundational study toward urban obstacle perception for visually impaired navigation by introducing a custom vision-based dataset comprising eight urban obstacle classes relevant to assistive mobility, manually annotated in YOLO format and reflecting realistic multi-object scenes. A lightweight YOLO-based model is trained as a baseline to assess detection performance on the proposed dataset. Experimental results demonstrate the feasibility of multi-class urban obstacle detection while highlighting challenges related to scale variability and scene complexity. This work establishes an initial benchmark and dataset to support future research on robust and deployable assistive navigation systems.

title: RAG-Induced Failures in Multi-Agent Large Language Model Debate
authors: Insaf Kraidia, Iyas Qaddara, Nida Al-Shafi, Mohammad O. Hiari, Rania Alzubaidi, Amira Benlecheb
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Engineering Systems (ICETES)
abstract: Multi-agent Large Language Model (LLM) systems based on collaborative debate have been proposed to improve reasoning accuracy and robustness by aggregating diverse reasoning trajectories. However, most debate frameworks implicitly assume that all participating agents behave honestly. This paper demonstrates that this assumption fails under a realistic threat model in which a single malicious agent manipulates Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to selectively retrieve externally sourced evidence that plausibly supports an incorrect conclusion. Unlike prior work that relies on prompt injection or complex persuasion pipelines, the proposed attack requires only retrieval control and uses legitimate documents whose relevance is strategically overstated or misinterpreted. Experiments on TruthfulQA, MMLU, MedMCQA, and SCALR show that RAG-based attacks can reduce majority-vote accuracy by up to 38% while increasing group consensus around false answers. These results indicate that retrieval-often used to improve factual grounding-can instead amplify misinformation when adversarially controlled, motivating stronger verification and trust calibration in multi-agent LLM systems.

title: Enhancing Different Hybrid Energy Systems Management and Their Optimisation Using AI Technology
authors: Ahmad Habboush, Bassam Elzaghmouri, Hussein Ahmad Al-Ofeishat, Takialddin Al Smadi
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Engineering Systems (ICETES)
abstract: Hybrid energy systems (HES) are necessary to make the energy supply less carbon dioxide-emitting, more flexible and cheaper. You know what? They use traditional backup and storage technologies, as well as renewable sources such as solar, wind, and biomass. This review covers the state of the art in HES optimisation, including engineering architectures, component technologies, and new approaches such as mathematical programming, metaheuristics, and surrogate models. Guess what? In agriculture, there is a strong emphasis on high-impact uses such as freezing, storage, and drying. This is because predictable heat loads facilitate the use of thermal energy storage and demand-shifting strategies to maximise the use of renewable energy. Essential key technical and economic metrics, such as Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE), Life Cycle Cost (LCC), and Net Present Value (NPV) are used to assess a project's performance. We also use environmental metrics based on Life Cycle Assessment (LCA). The study supports the technical and economic simulation of a biomass-solar-biomass hybrid cold storage facility in Jordan. This substantial amount of money, combined with reduced post-harvest losses, enables fuel consumption to be reduced. Finally, joint optimisation across multiple time scales, asset degradation modelling, and the need for standardized datasets and bankable business models are important research areas that warrant further work. This results in a targeted agenda for future research and policy.

title: Text-Guided Approach for Accurate Lesion Detection Using Vision-Language
authors: J. Sofia Priya Dharshini, Pogabuddi Abdul Thouhid, Gorige Indhu, Akula Vamsi
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: The role of accurate lesion segmentation is important in medical image assessment and diagnosis. Segmentation of key regions in images can be challenging, as these areas may vary in size and position. Common methods that rely solely on visual information often miss small or randomly located details, leading to reduced accuracy. This work aims to introduce a method for detecting lesions more accurately through text-guided training using a vision-language learning approach. During the training phase, this strategy enables the vision-based system to learn both image features and structured text descriptions. During inference, users do not need to provide structured text descriptions to identify lesion patterns. The model employs a cross-modality learning method to connect textual cues with visual patterns, which helps improve the detection of both large and small lesions. Experiments on the COVID-CT dataset show enhanced segmentation performance, achieving a Dice coefficient of 77.08 %, an Intersection over Union (IoU) of 66.06 %, precision of 83.54 %, and recall of 75.31 %, with an average processing time of 0.0189 seconds per image.

title: Social Robotic Surveillance and Manipulation: How Pervasive Data Collection, Personalisation, and Robotic Influence Could Harm Robot Users and Society
authors: Minja Axelsson, Hannele Seeck
publish: ACM Transactions on Human-Robot Interaction
abstract: None

title: Synergizing Deep Learning and Biological Heuristics for Extreme Long-Tail White Blood Cell Classification
authors: Duc T. Nguyen, Hoang-Long Nguyen, Huy-Hieu Pham
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Automated white blood cell (WBC) classification is essential for leukemia screening yet remains challenging under extreme class imbalance and domain shift. These limitations often cause deep models to overfit dominant classes while failing to generalize to rare pathological subtypes. To address this issue, we propose a three-stage hybrid framework. First, a self-supervised Pix2Pix restoration module mitigates synthetic noise and restores high frequency cytoplasmic details. Second, we integrate a Swin Transformer ensemble with MedSigLIP contrastive embeddings to enhance rare-class semantic representation. Finally, we introduce a biologically inspired refinement strategy combining geometric spikiness analysis and Mahalanobis-based morphological constraints to explicitly rescue suppressed minority predictions. Our hybrid framework achieves a Macro-F1 score of 0.77139 on the private leaderboard, demonstrating strong robustness under extreme long-tail distributions.

title: Shift-and-Add (SAdd): Hardware-Aware Channel Characterization with Polar NRZ Stimuli_supp1-3694993.pdf
authors: Mitchell Cooke
publish: N/A
abstract: High-bandwidth wireline transceivers rely on stochastic adaptation algorithms, such as the Least Mean Squares (LMS) method for equalizer calibration. While effective, these methods cannot provide upper-bounded latency guarantees, require tuning step-size parameters, and can be burdened by error propagation when calibrating Decision Feedback Equalizers (DFEs). However, if a high-fidelity estimate of the channel response is known, an alternative approach is to configure an equalizer to directly compensate for channel-induced distortion. To address the challenges of low-latency adaptation in wireline transceivers, we co-design a novel hardware-aware algorithm and a digital architecture for training-based channel response identification. We define and leverage a subset of polar Non Return to Zero (NRZ) stimulus that we refer to as Shift-and-Add (SAdd) sequences, which are designed to simultaneously favor an efficient hardware design and provide low noise amplification with precisely defined estimator variance properties. For deployment in practical resource-constrained wireline transceivers, we present an algorithm and architecture with low latency, upper-bounded computation time, low memory usage, and few arithmetic operations.

title: Why We Need Ubiquitous and Pervasive Computing as a Research Community More Than Ever
authors: Tanzeem Choudhury
publish: IEEE Pervasive Computing

title: Enhanced Flying-Capacitor Boost Multilevel Inverter (EFC-MLI) with Optimized Voltage Balancing and Current Spike Elimination
authors: Ahmed Awadelseed, Arkadiusz Lewicki, Charles Odeh
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: This paper proposes an Enhanced Flying-Capacitor Boost Multilevel Inverter (EFC-MLI) with optimized capacitor voltage balance technique. The proposed inverter configuration is derived from integration of H-Bridge (main cell) with new flying capacitor multilevel inverter (auxiliary cell). Moreover, the auxiliary cell generates the required voltage levels, while the main cell generates the base of the output voltage waveform. The proposed EFC-MLI utilized single dc source to achieve 9-level with dual voltage gain. The lower component count, reduced capacitor voltage rating, lowering cost, eliminates capacitor charging current spikes, high voltage gain, and capacitor voltage balance makes the proposed inverter attract+ive for renewable energy applications such as, Photovoltaic (PV) system, Fuel Cell system, and hybrid PV-FC system. Furthermore, to enhance the efficiency of the proposed EFC-MLI, a Hybrid PWM strategy with FC voltage balancing algorithm was implemented to ensure precise voltage regulation across capacitor while optimizing inverter performance. It minimizes voltage ripple, reduces switching losses, eliminates FC current spikes, and improves THD. Additionally, the proposed inverter was compared with recently proposed SC-MLIs. To support the proposed work, experimental results are provided which shows a good agreement with the analytical approach.

title: PQ-FRL: A Privacy-Preserving and Quality-Aware Federated Reinforcement Learning for UAV-Assisted Edge Computing
authors: Zifeng Dai, Hui Xie, Shengjun Wei, Changzhen Hu
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Optimizing service performance in UAV-assisted Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) systems often relies on effective cooperative trajectory and resource allocation. Federated Reinforcement Learning (FRL) provides a distributed paradigm to collaboratively learn these policies without sharing raw observations. However, practical deployments face severe coupled challenges: the model quality degradation caused by Differential Privacy (DP) noise injection, heterogeneous environments (Non-IID data), and complex physical constraints. To mitigate these issues, we propose PQ-FRL, a Privacy-Preserving and Quality-Aware FRL framework. Our approach utilizes an Output Perturbation DP mechanism to provide strict per-round protection for uploaded policy updates, representing a pragmatic engineering trade-off between securing immediate operational privacy and maintaining continuous-control DRL convergence. To address the mixed-action space, we incorporate a Straight-Through Estimator (STE) for differentiable offloading decisions, guided by a conditionally shaped reward to prevent suboptimal local equilibria. Furthermore, a novel Quality-Aware (QA) aggregation mechanism dynamically assigns weights based on explicitly DP-protected local reward signals, helping to disentangle noise from inherently poor performance. Simulation results indicate that PQ-FRL can effectively balance realistic non-linear energy and latency constraints. Under the evaluated heterogeneous scenarios and strict privacy budgets, our method demonstrates robust utility preservation and exhibits graceful degradation as physical airspace congestion increases with larger swarm sizes, offering a stable and practical solution for privacy-sensitive UAV edge computing.

title: ISAC Beamforming for Underwater Acoustic Networks Based on Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Hengyi Lin, Tianle Zhang, Xiaowen Ye, Xianxin Song, Yi Wu
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: This paper proposes DeepUIB, a deep reinforcement learning-based beamforming scheme for integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) in underwater acoustic (UWA) networks. The scheme aims to maximize the communication sum-rate under sensing constraints by addressing a key UWA challenge: long propagation delays that cause echoes from past transmissions to interfere with subsequent ISAC signals. Besides, a symmetric experience augmentation mechanism is developed for DeepUIB to accelerate convergence. Simulation results show that DeepUIB attains a higher sum-rate, converges faster, and exhibits stronger robustness than other schemes under various simulation setups.

title: Reliable Multi-View Clustering with Graph Neural Network
authors: Bao Zeng, Jielong Lu, Yanxin Xie, Shunxin Xiao, Jianmin Li, Chaohui Tang, Da-Han Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Deep multi-view clustering (MVC) typically leverages deep neural networks to extract consistency and complementarity information across multiple views. As a representative approach, graph neural networks (GNN) gather significant attention due to its ability to model both topology and feature simultaneously, making them highly attractive for researchers working on multi-view scenarios. However, existing GNN-based MVC algorithms often overlook the inherent heterogeneity and noise in multi-view data, which introduces uncertainty into the embedding learning process. Moreover, the message passing mechanism of GNN may further amplify this uncertainty, leading to unreliable embeddings and poor clustering performance. To address the issues, we propose a model called Reliable Multi-View Clustering with Graph Neural Network (RM-GNN). The proposed model first uses view-specific graph convolution encoders to project features of different views into unified semantic space. Then, we introduce an adaptive fusion method that effectively combines the representations obtained from each view. Furthermore, we propose a similarity-driven evidence learning module to effectively identify and distinguish unreliable samples in the embedding space. Finally, we introduce a unified transformation matrix to enforce global topological consistency. Extensive experimental results on nine benchmark datasets demonstrate that our algorithm generally outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

title: Adaptive Mamba Network Guided by Mixture of Experts for Infrared and Visible Image Fusion
authors: Quanrui Wen, Huayi Zhu, Rui Chen, Jianing Tang, Qiao Liu, Di Yuan
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: Current mainstream methods for multimodal image fusion rely on CNN and Transformers, which often suffer from severe information loss or high computational complexity. However, state-space models offer a promising alternative with their linear complexity and reduced historical information loss. This paper proposes a novel adaptive Mamba network, named MGMFuse, for infrared and visible image fusion, aiming to seamlessly integrate thermal targets with high-resolution texture details. Specifically, feature extraction is performed using a dual-branch architecture. We design the MoEGMamba module that uses the Mixture of Experts'prompt generation mechanism to dynamically guide Mamba for adaptive state space modeling. Furthermore, a specialized mechanism is proposed to execute modality-specific channel-wise rectification and cross-modal interaction to align deep semantic features. Experimental results on multiple datasets demonstrate that the proposed algorithm outperforms existing methods. Furthermore, it significantly enhances the performance of downstream visual tasks.

title: GNN-Based Malware Detection in IoT Networks: An Attack Graph Approach
authors: Irtika Khan, Anil Kumar Prajapati, Swaliya Khan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The rapid expansion of Internet of Things (IoT) devices is growing exponentially, significantly increasing the global attack space, making contemporary networks more susceptible to large-scale malware attacks with high sophistication. The heterogeneous and resource-constrained characteristics of IoT settings render traditional detection strategies, which rely on flow-level statistical characteristics, to be unable to reflect the relational and structural regularities of malware propagation. To address these limitations, we introduced a Graph Neural Network (GNN)-based attack graph framework that models IoT traffic as network graphs, capturing the extraction of complex behavioral dependencies among IoT devices. Traffic traces from real IoT and honeypot devices are captured in PCAP format and preprocessed to extract protocol, timing, and packet-level features. The proposed approach is evaluated using three GNN architectures: Graph Attention Network (GAT), Graph Isomorphism Network (GIN), and GraphSAGE, on the IoT-23 dataset. Experimental results demonstrate that GAT achieves highest accuracy, GraphSAGE provides consistently high detection performance, and GIN achieves competitive accuracy with precise accuracy, recall, and F1-scores, highlighting the effectiveness of GNN-based attack graphs for IoT malware detection. As a model framework, GNN provides an intelligent, robust, and scalable intrusion detection systems could be built future Internet of Things network.

title: CIPHER 2026 Cover Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)

title: QoE-Aware Multi-Representation Caching in Heterogeneous LEO Satellite–Edge Networks
authors: Ramiz Sharafat Rajput, Xiaohan Wu, Shihe Xu, Xinming Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: In the platform of Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite constellations combined with terrestrial edge servers, both satellite and edge nodes have limited storage and computing resources, and each content item may be available in multiple bitrate–quality representations, making cache allocation a challenging task. In this paper, we study QoE-aware multi-representation content caching in a heterogeneous LEO satellite– edge architecture, where a serving LEO satellite cooperates with multiple edge servers to deliver encoded video representations to users. We model the expected user Quality of Experience (QoE) by combining a content popularity distribution with a representation-dependent quality metric and a hierarchical delivery path that prioritizes nearby edge caches, followed by serving satellites and inter-satellite links or gateways. We formulate cache placement decisions at satellite and edge nodes as a joint optimization problem under storage and computing constraints and show that the resulting utility function is monotone and submodular. This allows us to cast the problem as a submodular knapsack maximization and to design a greedy caching strategy that iteratively selects cache actions with the largest marginal QoE gain per unit resource cost. Simulation results show that the proposed approach yields higher QoE than conventional ones.

title: Sensorless Fault-Tolerant Control of Single-Stator Dual-Rotor Contra-Rotating PMSM
authors: Huamin Gao, Weikang Huang, Zhuoran Zhang, Zhe Sun, Wenxin Huang, Wei Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: This article proposes a sensorless fault-tolerant control strategy for a single-stator dual-rotor contra-rotating permanent magnet synchronous machine (CRPMSM). The proposed approach addresses the fundamental challenges arising from single current measurement and strong multiplicative coupling between the electrical states of the two counter-rotating rotors. A control-oriented state-space model is established to characterize the coupled dual-rotor electrical dynamics. Based on this model, a full-order sliding mode observer (FOSMO) is developed to reconstruct the composite back electromotive force (back-EMF) without introducing additional low-pass filtering, and its stability and convergence properties are analytically proven. A phase-locked loop is then employed to extract a control-equivalent electrical position from the estimated composite back-EMF, enabling sensorless vector control and seamless transition to fault-tolerant operation under position sensor failure. It is demonstrated that the extracted electrical angle inherently converges to an intermediate position between the two rotor electrical angles, thereby enabling natural torque sharing and synchronous operation without explicit estimation of individual rotor positions. Simulation and experimental results validate stable operation under speed transients and asymmetric load disturbances, demonstrating the suitability of the proposed strategy for high-reliability electric propulsion applications.

title: Stage-Aware Secondary Frequency Regulation for Coordinated Flywheel Energy Storage Arrays and Thermal Power Units
authors: Yubin Liu, Fang Fang, Le Wei, Zijian Li, Tong Tong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: Coordinated secondary frequency regulation by flywheel energy storage arrays (FESAs) and thermal power units (TPUs) mitigates grid frequency oscillations caused by renewable integration. However, existing strategies often overlook stage-specific dynamics and multiobjective tradeoffs. To address this, a stage-aware, multiobjective, bilevel dynamic load allocation strategy is proposed based on grid assessment criteria. At the upper level, fuzzy adaptive control predicts TPU capacity, while field-based traction experiments establish precise physical power constraints for the FESA. An improved nondominated sorting genetic algorithm II (NSGA-II) with a comprehensive satisfaction model and smooth weights is developed to optimize power output across distinct regulation stages. At the lower level, a bidirectional coupling interface enables adaptive group selection, followed by an improved consensus algorithm based on the relative rate of change of state of charge (SOC) for rapid energy and state alignment. Validated with operational data from a 660-MW supercritical plant in Ningxia, China, results demonstrate superior robustness against 0.6 s network delays and 0.5 s unit failures. Compared to representative benchmarks, it increases regulation net profit by 21.83% and reduces energy losses by 19.8%, effectively balancing grid requirements with the operational safety and economic sustainability of storage-integrated plants.

title: Comparative Evaluation of CNN Models for Maize Crop Disease Diagnosis
authors: Sainadh Singh Kshagri, C. Raju, Kuruva Raju, S. Mallikarjunaiah, Banala Saritha, G Purnachandrarao
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: To ensure the security of food in the world, agriculture plays a significant role and maize stands among the most widely grown staple crops worldwide. However, maize productivity is seriously impacted by leaf diseases that result in huge yield losses every year. Early and accurate identification of these diseases will reduce the damage to the crops and to optimally utilize the agricultural resources. Conventional techniques of detection depends on manual inspection, which is impractical for large-scale agricultural. Although recent developments in Artificial Intelligence (AI) techniques have been promising for plant disease diagnostics in an automated fashion, most of the studies have only considered one model or none compared the modern convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures systematically. This causes a research gap of finding the most appropriate DL model for reliable classification of maize diseases. The objective of this work is such that it classifies maize leaf images into 4 classes, namely, Northern Leaf Blight, Common Rust, Gray Leaf Spot and Healthy and compares the performances of CNN pre-trained models. The methodology is based on using transfer learning methods based on MobileNetV2, EfficientNetB0 and ResNet50 models pre-trained on the Plant Village dataset that is publicly available. Fast computing Image preprocessing and fine-tuning is carried out to improve the classification performance. Key findings show closures in that ResNet50 can classify samples better than MobileNetV2 and EfficientNetB0 thanks to its simple model structure with depth and residual links that help with the process of extracting strong features. The results show the effectiveness of deep learning-based approaches can be used for an accurate maize disease detection. The proposed framework has important implications for smart and precision agriculture such as early disease diagnosis, lower crop losses, more optimal use of pesticides, and better decision support to farmers.

title: Analysis of Lightweight CNN Models for Multi-Crop Leaf Disease Classification
authors: Shivam Kumar Chauhan, Roop Pahuja
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Quick and precise plant disease detection is a key aspect in precision agriculture, particularly in resource-sensitive and field-scale settings. Whereas deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have proven to be very effective in leaf disease detection, most existing models rely on computationally intensive architectures that restrict real-time deployment. In this paper, a controlled comparative benchmark of two widely used lightweight CNNs (ResNet50 and InceptionV3) is presented for multi-crop leaf disease classification. An evidence-based preprocessing pipeline, including systematic data cleaning and Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE), is employed to optimize lesion visibility and feature consistency. Both models are trained on tomato and potato leaf disease classes from the PlantVillage dataset using transfer learning and evaluated under identical experimental conditions. The results indicate that ResNet50 converges stably with lower computational overhead, while InceptionV3 demonstrates stronger discrimination of complex disease patterns. Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) is included as an experimental interpretability tool to qualitatively analyze model attention. Although the generated heatmaps are not always precise, they provide useful insights into disease-relevant regions influencing predictions. Overall, the findings highlight the trade-offs between accuracy, efficiency, and interpretability when deploying lightweight CNN architectures for practical multi-crop disease diagnosis.

title: Analog Variance Noise Modulation: Detection by Extended Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filtering
authors: Nargess Sadeghzadeh-Nokhodberiz, Hadi Zayyani, Mohammad Salman, Felipe A. P. De Figueiredo, Rausley A. A. De Souza
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: This paper presents a novel framework for analog variance noise modulation (VNM) and its corresponding variance-based demodulation technique. Unlike traditional digital noise communication systems such as Kirchhoff–Law–Johnson–Noise (KLJN) schemes that encode binary data using discrete noise levels, the proposed analog VNM continuously maps a signal’s amplitude onto the variance of a white Gaussian noise (WGN) carrier. In this way, information is embedded within the statistical behavior of noise rather than its waveform, enabling inherently secure and noise-like transmission. To accurately reconstruct the transmitted signal, we develop a new state-space modeling and inference framework inspired by the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT). This model captures the temporal structure of the analog baseband signal through its harmonic components while treating the noise variance as a time-varying latent process. Building upon this structure, a Rao–Blackwellized Particle Filter (RBPF) is designed to estimate the underlying signal by jointly inferring its harmonic dynamics and the hidden variance states, enabling robust demodulation under stochastic noise conditions. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed analog VNM achieves accurate signal recovery and exhibits strong concealment properties compared to classical amplitude and frequency modulation schemes. Overall, this work introduces a new class of analog noise-based communication with an inherently simple and energy-efficient transmitter, while employing a higher-complexity receiver-side inference framework for accurate signal recovery. This asymmetric architecture makes the proposed VNM particularly suitable for low-power sensing and Internet of Things (IoT) scenarios where ultra-lightweight transmitters communicate with computationally capable gateways or base stations.

title: Tensor Methods Outperform Transformers: A Comprehensive Comparative Analysis of Knowledge Graph Link Prediction Methods
authors: Manal Helal, Hammad Khawaja
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: Knowledge graph link prediction has witnessed significant methodological evolution, with recent research heavily favouring transformer-based approaches. However, our comprehensive comparative analysis reveals surprising results: classical tensor factorisation methods significantly outperform modern transformer architectures across multiple domains. We systematically evaluate four major categories of link prediction methods-classical embeddings (TransE, DistMult, ComplEx), tensor factorisation (CP, Tucker Decomposition), graph neural networks, and transformer approaches-across three diverse knowledge graph datasets (UMLS, FB15K, DB15K). Contrary to prevailing trends, tensor methods achieved superior accuracy (Tensor-CP MRR: 0.2693) while transformer approaches demonstrated unexpectedly poor performance (LP-BERT MRR: <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 0 3 8 9}$</tex>). Our multi-dimensional evaluation, encompassing accuracy, computational efficiency, and scalability, identifies three distinct operational tiers and provides evidence-based guidelines for method selection. These findings challenge the current research focus on transformer architectures and suggest a renewed importance for tensor-based approaches in knowledge graph completion tasks.

title: Development of an Environmental Sensor System in the Life Mechatronics Symposium and its Use in Project-Based Learning
authors: Motoharu Fujigaki
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: In this presentation, joint research activities in Cambodia conducted under the Life Mechatronics initiative will be introduced. An environmental sensor system has been developed and installed at two experimental sites. Because the system is expected to be deployed more widely in the Cambodia–Japan collaborative project, it requires expandability and low-cost implementation, needs that existing commercial products do not satisfy. A new system tailored to future research activities is therefore being developed. Furthermore, this environmental sensing system has been incorporated into project-based learning (PBL) activities, and the practical implementation of these activities will also be reported.

title: DREAM-Mamba: Segmentation of VHR Remote Sensing Imagery Based on Dynamic Scanning and Adaptive Convolution
authors: Xiaowei Zhou, Chengjun Xu, Tao Fang, Jingqian Shu
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Remote sensing semantic segmentation faces significant challenges such as difficult multi-scale feature capture, complex and variable scenes, and boundary blurring. To address these issues, this study proposes the DREAM-Mamba model. The Dynamic Omnidirectional Scanning Mechanism is introduced to adaptively adjust scanning weights, synergizing global context with local details; the Adaptive Windmill Convolution Module is designed to accurately fit complex geometric features through dynamic sampling; and the Edge Enhancement Module is incorporated to suppress smoothing effects and preserve high-frequency edges. DREAM-Mamba achieves a 93.58% IoU on the WHU dataset and a 72.36% mIoU on the Potsdam dataset, notably becoming the only method to exceed 90% IoU (attaining 90.20%) in the Potsdam building category. The source code is available at https://github.com/wuduan521/DREAM-Mamba.

title: Nanotube Transistor Architectures: Device Physics and Scalability Perspective
authors: Dileep Kumar Gupta, Ashish Raman, Prabhat Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: When semiconductor scaling gets close to the sub-5nm range, regular MOSFETs have problems because of short-channel effects (SCEs) and the basic subthreshold swing (SS) limit of 60 mV/decade. Tunnel Field-Effect Transistors (TFETs) have become a promising alternative because they use a band-to-band tunneling (BTBT) process. This paper talks about the progress of Nanotube FET (NTFET) architectures, focusing on their hollow cylindrical shape, which gives them better electrostatic control than nanowire equivalents. We look at different types of structures, such as electrostatic doping (ED) and heterojunction integration (SiGe/Si, InGaAs/InP), and how they can improve ON-state current and make ultra-steep subthreshold slopes.

title: navCLIP: A Novel CLIP-guided Drone Navigation Model
authors: Hussein Samma
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Autonomous drone visual navigation in indoor environments remains a challenging problem due to the lack of reliable localization signals and the complexity of dynamic surroundings. Developing robust and cost-effective navigation solutions is therefore essential for real-world inspection and monitoring applications. This work presents a novel, low-cost visual navigation model named navCLIP, for indoor environments. navCLIP builds on a pre-trained CLIP (contrastive language–image pre-training) model for vision–text encoding and adds an MLP (multilayer perceptron) projection layer. This layer is trained via contrastive learning to align (1) an ensemble encoder (monocular depth estimation, semantic segmentation, and a scene description) with (2) discrete action texts (e.g., “move forward” and “rotate left/right”). During inference, navCLIP uses cosine similarity to select the best action for drone navigation. The proposed navCLIP model was evaluated in a realistic simulated indoor environment created with AirSim and Unreal Engine to represent the corridor area of the SDAIA–KFUPM JRCAI at KFUPM. The results indicate that navCLIP outperforms baseline models such as CNNs (Convolutional Neural Network) in terms of recognition accuracy in selecting the best action based on the collected data. Moreover, when reinforcement-learning agents navigate within the same environment, navCLIP provides better collision avoidance compared with other reinforcement-learning methods like DQN (Deep Q-Network) and enhanced-DQN agents.

title: SPI based Secure Communication Framework for Industrial IoT
authors: Soumyadip Motilal, Soumita Chatterjee, Ayas Kanta Swain, Kamalakanta Mahapatra
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The rapid growth and development of IoT has led to the need for safe and effective hardware based communication protocols. As any other network system, IoT systems are also prone to security breaches. To ensure data integrity and authentication, this paper proposes a novel design of a secure FPGA-based communication protocol-SPI on Xilinx Spartan-7 XC7S50-CSGA324 FPGA for Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) applications. The design involves design of a high-level synthesized (HLS) Intellectual Property (IP) of two security standards- Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) and Rivest–Shamir–Adleman (RSA) for encryption and decryption of the data. Following this, the serial interface is designed wherein the slave (sensor) transmits the encrypted data to the master (IoT Gateway). The decryption IP block then reveals the original message. The synthesis results show that the security protocols can achieve resource consumption as low as 3.79%. These security protocols have been also tested on Zybo, Pynq Z2 and Arty A7 boards. Additionally, these are also tested against various statistical strength measuring tests- NIST p-value and avalanche. The proposed secure design protocol is a stepping stone for next generation simple yet flexible secure IoT modules. The detailed experimental analysis also serves as a testimony to the fact that a highly secure protocol can be designed without requiring heavy resource usage or power overhead.

title: Integrating Lean Healthcare Practices with Discrete-Event Simulation to Reduce Patient Waiting Time in Outpatient Clinics at a Sri Lankan Public Hospital
authors: Navindya Hettiarachchi, Chathumi Kavirathna, Annista Wijayanayake
publish: 2026 IEEE International Research Conference on Smart Computing and Systems Engineering (SCSE)
abstract: Excessive patient waiting time is a persistent operational challenge in outpatient departments (OPDs) of public hospitals, particularly in high-volume and resourceconstrained healthcare systems. This study aims to analyze and reduce patient waiting time in the OPD of a major Sri Lankan public hospital by integrating Lean management practices with discrete-event simulation (DES). Lean tools were applied to map the current-state process and identify non-value-added activities, capacity imbalance, and workflow bottlenecks. Primary data were collected through time studies conducted over three consecutive working days, capturing patient arrivals, service times, and waiting durations at registration, consultation, and pharmacy stages. A DES model was developed using Arena Simulation Software to evaluate multiple Leanbased improvement scenarios, including partial appointmentbased demand leveling, layout optimization, consultation capacity balancing during peak hours, and digital queue management at the pharmacy. Simulation experiments were conducted for both peak-demand and reduced-capacity periods. Results reveal that doctor consultation is the primary bottleneck driving excessive waiting, with pharmacy services acting as a secondary bottleneck. While isolated Lean interventions yielded limited improvements, integrated Lean scenarios achieved substantial reductions in waiting time and lead time, together with notable increases in patient throughput, without infrastructure expansion. The findings demonstrate the effectiveness of simulation-supported Lean integration as a practical decision-support approach for improving OPD efficiency in public healthcare settings.

title: Keynote Title
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)

title: A Task-Oriented Characterization of Benchmark Datasets for Predictive Maintenance of Rolling Bearings
authors: Amanpreet Kaur, Harpreet Kaur
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Rolling-bearing predictive maintenance research relies heavily on publicly available benchmark datasets to develop and evaluate data-driven methods. However, these datasets differ fundamentally in experimental design, fault generation mechanisms, temporal structure, and labeling strategies, and are frequently applied to predictive maintenance tasks for which they violate core methodological assumptions. As a result, a significant portion of the existing literature reports performance results that are difficult to interpret, not comparable across studies, and in some cases methodologically unreliable. This paper presents a task-oriented characterization of widely used rolling-bearing benchmark datasets, including IMS, CWRU, XJTU-SY, FEMTO (PRONOSTIA), and Paderborn. The datasets are systematically analyzed and mapped to three core predictive maintenance objectives: fault diagnosis, degradation modeling, and remaining useful life (RUL) prediction based on their structural suitability. In addition, common dataset misuse patterns are identified and examined through the lens of experimental validity, highlighting threats to prognostic, construct, statistical, and ecological validity. The main contributions of this work are (i) a dataset–task compatibility framework that clarifies appropriate and inappropriate dataset usage, and (ii) a validity-driven analysis that explains why certain widely reported predictive maintenance results cannot be reliably generalized. This paper aims to serve as a corrective reference for dataset selection and experimental design in rolling-bearing predictive maintenance research.

title: Unique Window ESD Failure in LDMOS–SCR and Its Implication on Power-to-Fail Scalability
authors: Kranthi Nagothu, Gianluca Boselli, K. K. Shreenidhaa, Shravya N. Raj, Mayank Shrivastava
publish: IEEE Transactions on Electron Devices
abstract: The vulnerability of parasitic SCR, integrated with laterally diffused MOS (LDMOS) device (conventional LDMOS–SCR), to trigger during MOS/switching operation was addressed using an engineered (Flipped) LDMOS–SCR configuration, which in turn resulted in a unique low current/window electrostatic discharge (ESD) failure. While the failure was found to be universal to LDMOS–SCR devices with a weaker parasitic SCR, designed as an efficient MOS switch, it was found to be specific to a window of current between trigger and holding state. Besides, it couldn’t be detected using a standard 50-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Omega $</tex-math> </inline-formula> loadline transmission line pulse (TLP). The window failure resulted in severe power-to-fail scalability issues in LDMOS–SCRs, which were noticed in system-level stress, high impedance TLP, and long pulse stress. In this work, while using systematic experiments performed by a high-resistance load-line TLP and 3-D technology computer-aided design (TCAD) simulations, we have developed detailed physical insights into the low current or window ESD failure phenomenon in these LDMOS–SCR devices. Physical insights developed in this work have resulted in device design proposals to avoid such failures and mitigate power-to-fail scalability issues during ESD stress while keeping the MOS operation and performance intact.

title: Intelligent Scheduling of AI Tasks in Kubernetes
authors: Michael Abdelshaheed, Mohamed Ashour
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: Kubernetes has become the de facto standard for orchestrating containerized workloads, including machine learning (ML). ML workloads exhibit long runtimes, variable compute and I/O demands, and high sensitivity to execution order, making them challenging to schedule efficiently. Kubernetes lacks mechanisms for fine-grained job ranking and does not control the exact order in which pods begin execution. Instead, Kubelet starts containers in parallel without coordination, causing pods to compete for resources simultaneously and degrade system performance. The work in this paper presents a custom ML-aware scheduling framework that addresses the above two limitations of the default scheduler. First, a ranking approach is introduced that scores jobs based on their ML-specific characteristics including estimated training duration, convergence behavior, and computational intensity. Second, a scheduler implementation is presented that enforces execution order according to the proposed ranking through pacing and execution checks. The framework is evaluated on a real Kubernetes cluster using simulated ML workloads, testing multiple pacing configurations and analyzing their impact on average Job Completion Time (JCT) and makespan. The best-performing configuration, balancing serialization and throughput, is then adopted for comparison against the default Kubernetes scheduler and a reversed-order variant across three workload intensities. The proposed work achieves up to 31.6 % improvement in average JCT and up 10.2 % improvement in makespan compared to the default scheduler. An ablation study with reversed execution order degraded JCT by up to 58 % relative to the intended ordering, confirming the importance of enforcing intelligent job sequencing. These findings highlight that controlling execution order, not just scheduling order, is a critical factor in ML workload orchestration, even in unconstrained, single-node environments.

title: Adaptive Model Predictive Control of Magnetic Suspension Flywheel Battery Based on Autonomous Decision-Making Mechanism
authors: Weiyu Zhang, Yao Wu, Si Li, Qianwen Xiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: Under the continuous and variable disturbances caused by complex road conditions, the coupling effect among the subsystems within the vehicle-mounted flywheel battery intensifies. This will cause the dynamic model parameters and control parameters of the system to change in real time, thereby reducing the robustness of the flywheel battery system. Therefore, to address the problem of control accuracy decline caused by parameter mismatch in flywheel battery system under complex road conditions, an adaptive model predictive control based on an autonomous decision-making mechanism is proposed in this study. Firstly, the mathematical model of flywheel battery is established, and based on this model, a nominal model predictive control (MPC) is constructed. Then, an autonomous decision-making mechanism with predictive and intelligent decision-making capabilities is proposed. This mechanism can predict the state of flywheel rotor and adjust the key control parameters of the nominal MPC to improve the control accuracy of system. Finally, various experiments are conducted on the robust control of flywheel battery. The results show that, compared with the traditional MPC, the proposed control method has increased the control accuracy in the axial and radial directions by 46.15% and 24.66% respectively. This proves the effectiveness of this method.

title: COSMO: O-RAN-Based Service Management and Orchestration for Cross-Technology Multi-Tenant Radio Access Networks
authors: Miguel Catalan-Cid, Joan Josep Aleixendri, Jorge Pueyo, Pau Tomas, Daniel Camps-Mur
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: The evolution toward 6G networks envisions a heterogeneous Radio Access Network (RAN) comprising diverse access technologies, such as private 5G, public 4G/5G, and Wi-Fi, managed by multiple stakeholders. While considerable research effort has been devoted to O-RAN-based frameworks enabling rApp and xApp implementation and validation, few works provide integrated support for cross-technology RAN orchestration, end-to-end multi-tenancy, and a unified subset of SMO functionalities, including Non-RT RIC components. This paper introduces COSMO, a novel RAN Service Management and Orchestration platform designed to support heterogeneous 3GPP (5G NR, LTE) and non-3GPP (Wi-Fi) access networks. COSMO enables cross-technology multi-tenancy, defined as the capability to allow multiple tenants to dynamically share heterogeneous RAN resources with explicit resource allocation guarantees based on Service Level Agreements (SLAs). This is achieved through management primitives that support flexible and on-demand resource allocation. Additionally, the platform includes a cross-technology Non-Real-Time RAN Intelligent Controller (Non-RT RIC) that enables the development of intelligent rApps for closed-loop control and network orchestration. Beyond its architectural design, COSMO improves resource utilization and operational flexibility through unified orchestration of heterogeneous multi-tenant RAN resources. Through prototyping and benchmarking, we demonstrate the effectiveness of COSMO in resource allocation, SLA enforcement, and scalability. In our prototype, the SLA-based rApp reduces SLA violation from approximately 21% to below 10% under dynamic traffic conditions in a heterogeneous RAN deployment including 5G, 4G, and Wi-Fi access networks. Our results confirm that COSMO offers an efficient solution for managing and orchestrating future multi-tenant cross-technology RAN environments.

title: Dagent: A Multi-Agent System for Device-Aware Assistance
authors: Ahmed Hassan, Ahmed Gamea, Omar Abdelgawad, Abdelaziz Serour, Eyad Hussien, Amr Khamees, Ahmed Abdalfatah, Ahmed Fares
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: The limitations of large language models (LLMs) in interacting with local computing environments have hindered their applicability in device-aware tasks. This research addresses this gap by introducing DAgent, a modular, multiagent system designed to provide personalized, context-aware assistance through integration with the user's system environment. The architecture comprises four specialized modules: the Tracer module for monitoring system activities, the RAG module for retrieval-augmented generation leveraging system logs, the Coding Agent for interacting with the environment to infer information or execute actions, and the Multi-Agent OS Assistant for orchestrating workflows. DAgent was evaluated on correctness, completeness, and clarity, scoring highly with the baseline 14 B model: 4.87,4.53, and 4.41 out of 5, respectively. Additionally, an ablation study removing the RAG module demonstrated the importance of the coding agent module, which achieved an average score of 4. 4 5 when used independently. These findings highlight DAgent's ability to excel in device-aware tasks, providing actionable, comprehensible responses and enabling secure, supervised command execution. While our evaluation was conducted on synthetic scenarios, the results indicate strong potential for deployment in real-world environments, marking a step toward intelligent, device-aware assistants that integrate language reasoning with system interaction.

title: Explainable Heterogeneous Federated Learning for Intrusion Detection in IIoT
authors: Tony Elsayed, Marwa Zamzam, Mohamed Ashour
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: The Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) is increasingly exposed to advanced cyberattacks, creating an urgent need for Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) that are accurate, interpretable, and privacy-preserving. Conventional IDS often rely on centralized data aggregation, which increases the risk of exposing sensitive information. In contrast, purely local learning avoids data sharing but struggles to generalize across heterogeneous IIoT environments. Additionally, many Machine Learning (ML)-based IDS operate as black boxes, providing limited transparency and making it difficult for operators to trust or validate alerts. These challenges highlight the need for IDS that preserve privacy, generalize effectively under nonidentical data distributions, and offer interpretable predictions. This paper introduces a Federated Learning (FL)-based IDS for IIoT that integrates Explainable AI (XAI) through SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP). A comparative study of centralized, local, and FL paradigms is conducted using a multiclass Edge-IIoT-set dataset. Results show that centralized learning achieves the highest accuracy (97.7%) but requires aggregation of raw client data, while local models suffer from poor generalization under heterogeneous IIoT conditions. FL achieves competitive accuracy (95%), reduces disparities across clients, preserves privacy, and provides stable, comprehensive feature attributions. Overall, FL balances accuracy, fairness, and interpretability, making it a practical and privacy-preserving solution for IIoT intrusion detection.

title: Method for Identifying Changes Between Program Versions
authors: Mariam Arutunian, Hripsime Hovhannisyan, Arthur Malajyan, Karen Avetisyan, Hayk Aslanyan
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: This paper presents a two-stage method for detecting changes between program versions. It combines analysis based on metrics with a code clone detection algorithm. The approach uses control and data dependencies between instructions to match functions on a many-to-many basis. Additionally, it allows detecting changed instructions with high accuracy. The developed method provides reliable comparison of executable files, even in the absence of source code. Experimental evaluation on software projects has demonstrated its effectiveness in analyzing changes and detecting modifications.

title: Activity Monitoring System for Long-Term Care Bathrooms Using mm-Wave FMCW Radar
authors: Joshua Visser, Hajar Abedi, George Shaker
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: The growing demand for long-term care (LTC) services, coupled with critical staffing shortages, highlights the need for privacy-preserving solutions to monitor resident safety, particularly in high-risk environments such as bathrooms. This study presents a comprehensive system design and evaluation of an activity monitoring system using a low-cost commercial 60 GHz mm-wave frequency-modulated continuous-wave (FMCW) radar with a single-input multiple-output (SIMO) antenna configuration, designed to recognize both dynamic and static behaviors without compromising resident privacy. The system uses deep learning to leverage spatial radar features (range-elevation and range-Doppler heatmaps), enabling accurate classification of common bathroom activities, including sitting, standing, walking, handwashing, cleaning, and falls. Over 500,000 radar frames were collected across three bathroom layouts with ten participants. The proposed system achieved classification accuracies up to 95% in training environments and maintained over 90% accuracy in new layouts. The system demonstrated consistent fall detection (95% precision), reliable occupancy and multi-person detection, and effective generalization across variable environments through transfer learning. This work addresses a critical gap in LTC monitoring by demonstrating practical, validated deployment of radar-based activity recognition in bathroom environments—one of the highest-risk areas for falls—using commercially available, low-cost hardware. The results demonstrate the system’s potential for real-world deployment, supporting timely intervention and improving safety in vulnerable care settings.

title: Bilateral Neurodynamic-Reconstructed Olfactory Model for Agricultural Product Recognition in Electronic Noses_supp1-3693348.pdf
authors: Hong Men
publish: N/A
abstract: Electronic noses (E-noses) are intelligent identification systems inspired by biological olfactory perception mechanisms. However, data captured by conventional E-noses primarily represent mechanistic expressions of odor properties, and existing analytical methods fail to fully replicate the neurodynamic characteristics inherent in biological olfactory systems. To address the limitations of the classical KIII computational model in simulating bilateral synergistic perception, this study systematically reconstructed the topological structure and dynamic equations to establish the Bilateral Olfactory Neural Model (BONM), incorporating recent neuroanatomical findings on bilateral interaction mechanisms. Integrating the BONM module into the E-nose data processing pipeline facilitates a transition from simple pattern recognition to biomimetic perceptual processing. Self-similarity and chaotic dynamic indices, combined with small-world properties, were innovatively introduced to quantitatively validate the efficacy of BONM in simulating complex neural dynamics. Experimental odor signals from rice, eggs, soybeans, apples, and peanuts were collected using a PEN3 to construct a large-scale dataset. Results demonstrate that the BONM-integrated recognition system significantly outperforms the original KIII model in terms of feature robustness and compatibility with diverse classification algorithms, while maintaining high classification accuracy. This work provides novel insights into the structural evolution and biomimetic evaluation of olfactory models and offers an effective technical solution for developing high-performance, intelligent gas identification engineering systems.

title: Retina Diseases Identification With OCT Images Using Transfer Learning
authors: Asha Joseph, K Rajakumari
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Emerging Computational Intelligence (ICECI)
abstract: Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) is a non-invasive imaging technique widely used to diagnose retinal diseases such as choroidal neovascularization, diabetic macular edema, and drusen, where delayed intervention can cause irreversible blindness. Manual interpretation of OCT scans is time-consuming and subject to inter-observer variability, highlighting the need for automated solutions. This study introduces a clinically oriented deep learning framework employing transfer learning with a ResNet-50 backbone. A two-phase fine-tuning strategy, anatomically realistic data augmentation, and patient-level partitioning were applied to ensure robust training and prevent data leakage. The model achieved 98.21percentage accuracy, with weighted precision, recall, and F1 scores above 98 percentage, and macro AUC of 0.996. Cross-device validation confirmed strong generalizability, while Grad-CAM provided interpretable visual explanations. With rapid inference time of 6.4 millisecond per image, the framework offers an efficient, explainable solution for large-scale retinal screening, enhancing diagnostic reliability and reducing clinician workload.

title: Vision Foundation Models Pretrained on Skin Lesion Images Can Classify Oral Lesions Too!
authors: Vedatroyee Pal, Rajarshi Bandyopadhyay, Samarup Bhattacharya, Sanjoy Saha, Tapabrata Chakraborti
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Recognizing the critical limitations imposed by data scarcity in Oral Squamous Cell Carcinoma (OSCC) detection, this work demonstrates how the domain transferability of Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) provides a powerful solution. We present the first work to employ a VFM, Google's Derm Foundation, pretrained on dermatology images, as a frozen feature extractor for OSCC detection. By training only a shallow Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) classifier, we achieve high-performance with minimal computational load. This lightweight pipeline, validated across multiple datasets, offers robust performance comparable to fully trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), establishing an efficient paradigm for early screening in resource-constrained environments. An additional contribution of the work is the combination of multimodal data (image and non-imaging) into an integrated pipeline with improved results.

title: Optical Limit Cycles Induced by Nonlinear Bifurcation in Silicon Microring Resonators
authors: Menglong He, Abdou Shetewy, Kambiz Jamshidi
publish: 2026 IEEE Silicon Photonics Conference (SiPhotonics)
abstract: In this paper, the dynamics of optical limit cycles induced by free carrier and thermal nonlinearities have been investigated via mathematical modeling and numerical simulation. Besides, the experimental observation of a thermo-optic oscillation has been demonstrated with oscillation frequencies up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 5 ~ M H z}$</tex>.

title: A Vision Transformer-Based Hybrid Encryption Watermarking Algorithm for Medical Image Security
authors: Yu Fan, Jingbing Li, Uzair Aslam Bhatti, Yenwei Chen
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: Unauthorized access and tampering of medical images are key problems in medical image security. To address these concerns, this research introduces a Vision Transformer (ViT)-Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT)-based digital watermarking framework incorporating Logistic Map chaotic encryption and a hash-based authentication mechanism function to improve watermark robustness and confidentiality. First, the ViT model is used to extract global features from medical images, and DCT frequency domain transformation is applied to enhance feature representation. A perceptual hash function is then employed to generate a feature vector, and the original watermark undergoes chaotic encryption using the Logistic Map. The resulting encrypted watermark is fused with the image feature descriptor using an exclusive-OR (XOR) operation, generating a logical key that is securely stored for subsequent authentication and integrity verification. During the extraction process, the same feature analysis is performed on the test image to obtain a corresponding descriptor, which is then decrypted using the stored logical key through an XOR to recover the watermark. The extraction effectiveness is assessed by the normalized correlation (NC) coefficient. Experimental evaluation confirms that the proposed scheme provides excellent resistance to both conventional and geometric attacks. Moreover, since the watermark embedding process does not directly modify the medical image content, the original image quality remains unaffected. Overall, this algorithm holds promising potential for ensuring medical image copyright protection and data integrity assurance.

title: Detecting Hallucinations in Arabic Large Language Models Using Automatic Fact-Verification Signals
authors: Mohammad Alhawarat
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Engineering Systems (ICETES)
abstract: In many Natural Language Processing tasks like Arabic Large Language Models have shown great performance. But they often produce hallucinations - fluent but not factually correct responses - which are very dangerous in many critical applications in education / healthcare / decision support systems. Though hallucination detection is a well-studied topic for LLMs, Arabic is still mostly understudied despite the appearance of more and more Arabic-capable models. In this paper an automated detector for hallucinations in Arabic LLM outputs is presented based on fact-verification signals. In our approach we use three complementary detection mechanisms: Logical consistency/semantic similarity analysis with sentence embeddings and ensemble classification against Arabic Wikipedia - Arabic Natural Language Inference (NLI). We test two Arabic-capable LLMs (Jais-13B-chat & Meta-LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct) on Arabic question-answering datasets (ARCD and TyDiQA for both training/validation and for cross-dataset testing). Experimental results show that in hallucination detection with both NLI and retrieval signals F1-scores are higher than single-signal baselines. Ablation studies show that NLI is most important to detect accuracy whereas retrieval consistency is very important to complement that evidence. It is the first systematic benchmark for Arabic LLM hallucination detection & offers suggestions for better - tuned Arabic NLP systems.

title: Performance Analysis of Hybrid-Electric Propulsion Systems for Regional Commuter Aircraft
authors: Gustavo Muller Hauck, Cleverson Bringhenti, Mauricio Andrés Varela Morales, Jesuino Takachi Tomita, Antonio Bruno De Vasconcelos Leitao, Franco Jefferds Dos Santos Silva, Konstantinos Kyprianidis
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Aviation plays a fundamental and valuable role in the modern world, yet it faces significant challenges, including high fuel costs and substantial environmental pollution. These issues have prompted the aviation industry to establish emission standards and develop less-polluting propulsion systems, such as those utilizing synthetic fuels, fuel cells, and electrification. Among these, electrification holds promise as a potential solution for reducing emissions in commuter aircraft, despite the mass limitations posed by batteries. In this study, a methodology was developed and implemented within in-house software to simulate the performance of a commuter aircraft with a hybrid-electric propulsion system. The analysis focused on key metrics like fuel economy and climb time to cruise altitude. The EMB-120 Brasilia was chosen as the base aircraft for this research. Its long-standing use by the Brazilian Air Force (FAB), the authors' extensive familiarity with its performance, and the availability of experimental data for lift and drag coefficients made it an ideal model for our simulations. To evaluate the performance of the hybrid propulsion system and compare it with the standard case, a gas turbine was utilized as the primary engine. Mathematical models were developed for the PW118 gas turbine, which powers the real aircraft that was considered the standard case, and for the PT6A-68C, which was suggested as a substitute for the hybrid system. To evaluate the aircraft's performance, a standard mission was simulated on a short-range route of 926 km (500 NM), flying at an altitude of 7,620 m (25,000 ft), a common mission that is used by Brazilian Air Force. For hybrid simulations, battery packs were tested with specific energies ranging from 0.125 kWh/kg to 0.750 kWh/kg, in multiples of the initial value. Batteries with 0.250 kWh/kg were considered the current state-of-the-art, while the 0.750 kWh/kg packs represent an extreme upper bound associated with far-future technological developments. The results demonstrated significant performance gains depending on the chosen battery technology and the power split, the hybrid system achieved fuel savings of up to 18% to 19% and reduced climb time by 17% to 25%.

title: Lightweight Privacy-Preserving and Fault-Tolerant Truth Discovery for Mobile Crowdsensing Systems_supp1-3694180.pdf
authors: Xiaofen Wang
publish: N/A
abstract: As a paradigm for encouraging users to contribute data spontaneously, mobile crowdsensing (MCS) has received considerable attention recently. It is crucial to evaluate the truthfulness of MCS data by proper truth discovery mechanisms. Although recent truth discovery schemes can determine truthful information, they either provide limited privacy preservation or have heavy computation and communication overheads. Moreover, most of them are not resilient to malicious faults and active attacks. To tackle the above problems, we propose two fault-tolerant and privacy-preserving truth discovery solutions. Our first scheme is mainly used for scenarios with a relatively stable number of users, where participants do not frequently join or leaves. Integrating ring signature with the perturbation technique, we design an anonymous and privacy-preserving truth discovery scheme, namely RsAnonTD, which can achieve privacy preservation and resist active attacks. To address the challenge with dynamically changed workers, we devise a multi-client inner product functional encryption scheme with a lightweight zero-knowledge proof protocol (namely McFeKDeTD) for defending against active attacks. The security analysis shows that both schemes can preserve the privacy of sensory data, weights, and estimated truths while resisting active attacks, thereby guaranteeing fault tolerance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our designs achieve superior performance than other schemes in terms of accuracy, convergence speed, and system overheads. For example, compared with the state-of-the-art approach RPTD-II, which has a security level comparable to ours, our proposed schemes, RsAnonTD and McFeKDeTD, reduce the computational overheads approximately by 98% and 69%, respectively.

title: Predicting Future Land Use Patterns in Kurnool District, India using Cellular Automata and Artificial Neural Network Models
authors: Sunandana Reddy Machireddy, Keerthan Kumar Mallipeddi, Sathish Kumar Reddy Baasireddy, Umesh Kodidala
publish: 2026 International Conference on Multidisciplinary Innovations For Smart &amp; Sustainable Future (MISSF)
abstract: The focal point of this study is the concept of land use and land cover (LULC) changes in Kurnool district, India, using Cellular Automata (CA) and Artificial Neural Network (ANN) models in geographic information system environment during the period of 2019-2025. The satellite images were used to study and categorize the land into those categories like water bodies, vegetation, cropland, and dry land using Landsat 8 satellite images. The analysis of changes in 2019-2022 and the forecasting of 2025 were performed using GIS software (QGIS and ArcGIS) and MOLUSCE plugin using Cellular Automata (CA) and Artificial Neural Network (ANN) models. The results indicated that the outputs produced a minor change in the water bodies, increase in the cropland areas and dry lands and generally a good match between the simulated and the real 2025 maps (71% accuracy, Kappa = 0.623). This work will help the policy makers and planners to understand land transformation and make better decisions to ensure sustainable development.

title: Automated Prediction of Paravalvular Regurgitation Before Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation
authors: Michele Cannito, Riccardo Renzulli, Adson Duarte, Farzad Nikfam, Carlo Alberto Barbano, Enrico Chiesa, Francesco Bruno, Federico Giacobbe, Wojciech Wanha, Arturo Giordano, Marco Grangetto, Fabrizio D'Ascenzo
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Severe aortic stenosis is a common and life-threatening condition in elderly patients, often treated with Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation (TAVI). Despite procedural advances, paravalvular aortic regurgitation (PVR) remains one of the most frequent post-TAVI complications, with a proven impact on long-term prognosis. In this work, we investigate the potential of deep learning to predict the occurrence of PVR from preoperative cardiac CT. To this end, a dataset of preoperative TAVI patients was collected, and 3D convolutional neural networks were trained on isotropic CT volumes. The results achieved suggest that volumetric deep learning can capture subtle anatomical features from pre-TAVI imaging, opening new perspectives for personalized risk assessment and procedural optimization.

title: Extending the Input Voltage Range of an Always-Dual-Path Hybrid DC-DC Converter Using Phase-Shift Operation
authors: Katsuhiro Hata, Shinsaku Tanaka, Toru Ashikaga, Yasuhiro Rikiishi
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This paper presents a method to extend the input voltage range of an always-dual-path hybrid (ADPH) DC-DC converter using phase-shift operation with soft charging through a sub-inductor. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method can achieve a regulated 12 V output even when the input voltage drops from the nominal 48 V to 40 V at a rated load of 8 A. Furthermore, the impact of duty-cycle and phase-shift combinations on efficiency is analyzed, and practical design guidelines are discussed to improve overall performance.

title: Integrating Multi-Modal Fundus Photography for Precision Diagnosis in Ophthalmology
authors: Fan Xiao, Junlin Hou, Yiqian Xu, Ruiwei Zhao, Haidong Zou, Lina Lu, Yi Xu, Juzhao Zhang, Rui Feng
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: In recent years, multi-modal fundus imaging has become crucial for comprehensive retinal assessment, since different imaging modalities provide complementary information about retinal structures and lesions. As two different imaging tools for diabetic retinopathy (DR) grading, color fundus photography (CFP) and infrared fundus photography (IFP) are highly-correlated and complementary in clinical applications. CFP offers rich color information, where as IFP captures pathological changes from deeper retinal layers. This complementarity is especially valuable in eyes with media opacities, such as cataract or vitreous haze, where CFP quality often degrades. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to explore a novel multi-modal deep learning framework that effectively fuses information from CFP and IFP for more accurate and robust DR grading, particularly under such challenging conditions. Specifically, we construct a dual-stream architecture Cross-Fundus Transformer (CFT) to fuse the ViT-based features of two fundus image modalities. Moreover, we adopt both the single-modality and multi-modality supervisions to maximize the overall performance for DR grading. Extensive experiments on a clinical dataset consisting of 1,713 pairs of multi-modal fundus images demonstrate the superiority of our proposed method. Code will be released for public access.

title: A Computationally Efficient Method of Power Module Junction Temperature Estimate Considering Ripple Elements for Yearlong Reliability Simulation of Power Electronics Converters
authors: Shahabuddin Khan, Purushottam Khadka, Siraj Ahmed Shahnewaz, Liwei Wang, Buck Brown, Zheyu Zhang
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: The reliability of power electronic converters is strongly influenced by the power module, where junction-temperature magnitude and cycling accelerate the dominant aging mechanisms in semiconductor devices.
