authors: Francis Joseph G. Libres, Hanz Ian B. Silva, Kim Miguel P. Sobrepena, Lysa V. Comia
publish: 2026 22nd IEEE International Colloquium on Signal Processing &amp; Its Applications (CSPA)
abstract: This study presents a retrieval-augmented clinical decision support chatbot designed to assist mental health assessment through knowledge-grounded conversational reasoning. The system integrates document chunking, semantic embeddings, and vector-based retrieval with the DeepSeek-R1 Distill-Llama-8B model to generate diagnostically relevant responses grounded in structured psychiatric knowledge. Diagnostic text was processed into semantic chunks, embedded using the all-MiniLM-L6-v2 model, and indexed in a ChromaDB vector database to enable efficient context retrieval. Evaluation across multiple simulated clinical scenarios demonstrated strong performance, achieving perfect relevance (1.00), high faithfulness (0.95), and mean cosine similarity of 0.76 between generated responses and reference diagnostic descriptions. The retrieval module also achieved robust results with recall of 1.0, precision of 0.79, and an F1-score of 0.88. These findings demonstrate that retrieval-augmented large language models can provide transparent, contextually grounded, and explainable support for conversational mental health decision systems.

title: An Optimization Approach for Sustainable Transformer Design in Photovoltaic Power Plants
authors: Emir Yükselen, Ebrahim Rahimpour
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Smart Grid, and Renewable Energy (PESGRE)
abstract: Transformers are critical in delivering electrical energy generated from renewable sources to the grid and end users. However, traditional transformer design methods are generally based on continuous maximum load conditions and often result in oversizing and increased costs. The increasing utilization of renewable energy sources has further highlighted these inefficiencies in modern energy systems, where energy derived from renewables is primarily converted into electrical energy for widespread public consumption. This study focuses on the optimization methods used in designing a transformer for photovoltaic power plants with unique loading cycles to optimize transformer parameters, aiming for minimal material costs, a compact size, and improved efficiency. Additionally, an electromagnetic simulation analysis is performed to validate the design approach and ensure the accuracy of the applied methodology. The results highlight the potential of optimized transformer designs to improve operational efficiency and support the integration of renewable energy sources into smart grids.

title: IoT-Enabled Text-to-Image Generation: A Review of Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Approaches
authors: Subuhi Kashif Ansari, Nusrat Hamid Shah, Mohammad Ashfaq Hussain, Roma Fayaz, Ahmad Unnisa Begum, Durdana Taranum Khan
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Engineering Systems (ICETES)
abstract: Internet of Things (IoT) and text-to-image generation with the help of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) have made it very easy to generate contextually suitable and personalized visual content. In this paper, we investigate the combination of the IoT data streams with AI architectures (e.g., GANs and transformer-based models) for text-to-image generation. Incorporation of real-time contextual and environmental information input by IOT devices and such models, the models can generate images of better quality, contextuality and personalization. The technical difficulties like computational overhead and latency are analyzed in section III, and some mitigation could be achieved by leveraging edge computing. Issues of privacy and security that emerge out of use of sensitive IoT data are handled as well. We further demonstrate the potential applications of IoT-driven text-to-image generation in various sectors, including smart homes, healthcare, and urban computing. We here aim to enhance user experiences and improve decision-making. Lastly, we outline future research directions. Here we emphasize on the creation of innovative AI models that consider IoT domain context-awareness and data integration/fusion techniques, along with ethical and societal considerations. We conclude by discussing the significance of ethical innovation and interdisciplinary collaboration in the development, implementation, and regulation of IoT-based text-to-image generation.

title: DC Modeling of 8 kV Depletion Mode Gallium Oxide MOSFET
authors: Abu Shahir Md Khalid Hasan, Mohammad Dehan Rahman, Md Majharul Islam, Md Maksudul Hossain, H. Alan Mantooth
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Gallium oxide (Ga<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>) has recently gained significant attention for high-voltage power electronics owing to its ultra-wide bandgap, high critical electric field (~8 MV/cm), and the potential for cost-effective single-crystal wafer production. Among its polymorphs, β-Ga<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> is the most stable and has enabled the demonstration of depletion-mode lateral MOSFETs with breakdown voltages up to 8 kV. Building on our recent work on a 750 V β-Ga<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> MOSFET, this paper extends a physics-based compact modeling approach to higher-voltage devices. The model formulation combines channel current with drift resistance, where drift mobility is extracted from Hall mobility accounting for four dominant scattering mechanisms and their temperature dependence. Simulation results show good agreement with measured transfer and output characteristics, validating the model’s scalability across voltages. This compact model provides a physics-informed framework for incorporating Ga<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> devices into power electronic circuit simulation and design.

title: Observer-Based Detection and Mitigation of False Data Injection Attacks in Smart Grids Using Discrete Event Systems
authors: Meriam Ghajdami, Mohammed Msaaf, Rafik Channa
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Global Aeronautical Engineering and Satellite Technology (GAST)
abstract: Smart grids rely on extensive sensing and communication infrastructures to support real-time monitoring and control. While this cyber–physical integration enhances efficiency and reliability, it also introduces new vulnerabilities to cyberattacks. False data injection (FDI) attacks are especially difficult because they manipulate sensor measurements while remaining consistent with normal system behavior. In this paper, we model FDI attacks using Discrete Event Systems (DES) and finite automata. We demonstrate that FDI attacks can be understood as unobservable events that corrupt observations, resulting in observer belief deviation and incorrect control actions. This deviation may progressively drive the system toward cascading failures and large-scale blackouts. To overcome this issue, we provide an observer-based mitigation model that ensures unsafe states are never reached silently. The proposed approach provides formal guarantees on both safety and resilience against stealthy cyberattacks.

title: Sustainable Solar Hydrogen Hybrid Backup Lighting for Off Grid Applications
authors: Vijayakumar M, Ramasamy M, Vijayakumar Madhaiyan, Elavarasan M, Veeva K P, Balamurugan M
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: This paper presents a sustainable solar-hydrogen-battery hybrid backup lighting system designed for off-grid and remote applications requiring reliable and long-duration energy availability. The proposed system integrates a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$12 \mathrm{V}, 10$</tex> W solar photovoltaic module, a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3.7 \mathrm{V}, 2000 \text{mAh}$</tex> lithium-ion battery and a PEM hydrogen fuel cell within a low-power DC architecture. A hierarchical energy management strategy prioritizes solar energy utilization, followed by battery support and hydrogen-based backup during extended low-irradiance conditions. The battery is charged using a constant-current/constant-voltage scheme, while surplus solar energy is converted into hydrogen through an electrolyze for long-term storage. Simulation-based evaluation under a 24-hour operating cycle demonstrates stable LED operation (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3-5 \mathrm{W}$</tex>), regulated bus voltage and LED current, controlled battery state-of-charge and seamless source transitions without load interruption. The results confirm efficient energy utilization, reduced battery stress, extended backup duration and overall system efficiency in the range of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$70-75 \%$</tex>, validating the suitability of the proposed hybrid architecture for rural lighting, emergency illumination and sustainable off-grid infrastructure.

title: A Controllable Emotional Intensity Data Augmentation Model for Mongolian Speech
authors: Lengduribu Da, Siyuan Guo, Kuibo Wang, Yixiong Qin
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: When augmenting Mongolian speech with emotional features, the emotional characteristics are derived from predefined emotional labels or fixed emotional speech samples. This approach lacks variations in emotional intensity, resulting in a singularity of emotional features in the augmented speech. To address this issue, this paper proposes a Mongolian speech data augmentation model with controllable emotional intensity, which is based on the StarGANv2-VC architecture and incorporates an Emotional Intensity Control Module (EICM). The EICM generates VAD values with specified emotional intensity through an intensity mapper. The emotional rendering unit then generates frame-level acoustic features based on these VAD values. The prosody prediction unit integrates the frame-level acoustic features with emotional intensity and the content features of the speech, and predicts prosodic variations according to the VAD values, thereby generating emotional intensity features to control the emotional intensity of the augmented speech. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model is capable of augmenting speech data with different emotional intensities and achieves high speech quality. The model outperforms in five evaluation metrics: FFE (F0 Frame Error), MCD (Mel Cepstral Distortion), DDUR (Differences of Duration), EECS (Emotional Embedding Cosine Similarity), and EIMD (Emotional Intensity Matching Degree), with respective values of 12.75%, 19.61, 0.37, 0.79, and 80%.

title: Modeling and Optimization of MHz Self-resonant Planar Coil for Wireless Power Transfer
authors: Ghovindo Siadari, Jungwon Choi
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This paper presents a modeling and optimization framework for a high-frequency, self-resonant planar coil used in wireless power transfer (WPT) systems to maximize the coil’s quality factor (Q) and achieve high efficiency. While self-resonant planar coils inherently provide internal capacitance and enable compact, high-power operation, achieving high Q at MHz frequencies is challenging due to magnetic and conductor losses. To overcome this limitation, we develop a gradient-based optimization method using an equivalent circuit derived from closed-form expressions of inductance, capacitance, and series resistance based on an Archimedean spiral geometry. The optimized coil is driven by a class Φ<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> inverter, chosen for its single-switch architecture, which simplifies control and reduces device stress compared to conventional inverter topologies. A 24 cm-diameter copper-sheet prototype tested at 13.56 MHz achieved a measured Q > 700 and delivered 640 W over a 13 cm transfer distance with 95% coil-to-coil efficiency, validating the proposed design methodology for high-power MHz WPT applications.

title: ZVS Analysis of 3L-ANPC Based GaN DAB Converter with Non-Unity Voltage Conversion Ratio
authors: Yiju Wang, Xin Gao, JiangBiao He
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Dual active bridge (DAB) converters typically lose zero-voltage switching (ZVS) when operating at non-unity voltage conversion ratio. This paper analyzes the ZVS characteristics of a GaN-based 2-level to 3-level (2L–3L) step-up DAB converter in which the high-voltage side adopts a 3-level active neutral point clamped (ANPC) half-bridge structure to enable an 800 V DC output using 650 V gallium nitride (GaN) power devices. Non-ideal switch models are used to derive explicit resonant transitions at two critical switching instants, yielding analytical dead-time conditions and current boundaries for achieving ZVS across all switches within the ANPC bridge, including cases where only partial ZVS is attainable. The derived conditions identify the critical current boundary that limits light-load ZVS. Experimental results from a 200-to-450 V prototype operating at 100 kHz validate the ZVS analysis and confirm the predicted partial ZVS behavior.

title: Arc Fault Localization in Low-Voltage Distribution Systems Using Transformer-Aided DOA Estimation
authors: Ratnakar Nutenki, Haraprasad Badajena, Aurobinda Routray, Ashok Kumar Pradhan
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Smart Grid, and Renewable Energy (PESGRE)
abstract: The accurate localization of arc faults in low-voltage distribution systems is critical for the early detection and mitigation of electrical hazards. This study adapts direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation techniques for arc-induced electromagnetic emissions using sensor arrays. While traditional subspace methods (MUSIC, Root-MUSIC, and ESPRIT) struggle with the non-stationary, broadband nature of arc discharges, we propose TransMUSIC, a transformer-based framework for robust DOA estimation under noisy conditions. TransMUSIC replaces eigendecomposition with a transformer encoder that learns the noise subspace from temporal snapshots, followed by a neural DOA estimator for arc localization from the MUSIC spectrum. Experimental results on synthetic arc discharge data, including colored noise and switching transients, show TransMUSIC significantly outperforms classical approaches in localization accuracy and low SNR robustness. The framework provides a scalable foundation for real-time arc fault localization in smart distribution systems, with future implementation targeting laboratory and field measurements.

title: Active Power Decoupling Method Using a Center-Tap DAB Matrix Converter Without Additional Switches
authors: Kousei Irimura, Masamichi Yamaguchi, Hiroki Watanabe, Jun-Ichi Itoh
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This paper proposes an active power decoupling (APD) method for a dual-active-bridge (DAB) matrix converter. The proposed method compensates for the second-harmonic power ripple that appears during single-phase operation while sharing circuit components with the existing three-phase configuration, thereby reducing the overall component count. The proposed APD approach does not require any additional switching devices because the buffer current is controlled through a center-tap circuit on the secondary side of the transformer. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method reduces the 100 Hz ripple component in the output current by 82% and achieves a power factor of 0.99 under a 500 W power transfer condition.

title: Fast Restart Method for Sensorless Vector-Controlled Induction Motors Considering Speed Variation
authors: Hye-Won Kang, Joon-Seok Kim, Joon-Hyoung Ryu, June-Seok Lee
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Induction motors are widely used in industrial applications because of their cost-effectiveness and high reliability. However, when a momentary power interruption occurs, the motor enters a free-running state driven by inertia. Under this condition, improper restarting may cause significant inrush currents, torque shock, and mechanical stress. This paper proposes a fast and stable sensorless restart strategy for induction motors. The proposed method applies three short-circuit current pulses during the free-running state to estimate the time-varying rotor speed and acceleration. Based on the estimated speed and acceleration, a short-circuit voltage–current model of the induction motor is employed to calculate the rotor flux axis position. The proposed strategy requires no additional power consumption, allowing rapid and reliable restart operation. As a result, the restart time is significantly reduced compared to conventional approaches, while stable and robust performance is maintained over a wide rotor speed. The effectiveness of the proposed strategy is verified through simulation results under nonlinear load conditions.

title: Potential of Ultraviolet Laser Diodes in Future Photonic Integrated Circuits
authors: Omar Alkhazragi
publish: 2026 IEEE Silicon Photonics Conference (SiPhotonics)
abstract: To fully unlock the potential of the applications of ultraviolet (UV) light in integrated systems, semiconductor laser diodes are needed. Here, we review the recent research efforts dedicated to the development of UV laser diodes and their potential use in emerging applications.

title: A Validated 4D Clinical-Computational Taxonomy and Translation Framework for GAN-Based MRI Super-Resolution
authors: Satvik V. Khara, Monali Suthar, Prayas Chavda, Nisarg Chauhan, Anurag Soliya, Vraj Thakkar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Multidomain Integrated Learning Environments (ICSMILE)
abstract: Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) super resolution (SR) with deep learning techniques has recently been explored as a promising solution to improve spatial resolution without prolonging acquisition time, increasing hardware complexity or imposing additional burdens on patients. Among learning-based solutions, Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) have been shown to possess remarkable potential in the task of restoring high-resolution anatomical details and texture realism, although the current state of research is highly disorganized in terms of architectures, modalities, training strategies and applications. In support of the United Nations’ Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 3 - Good Health and WellBeing, which strongly promotes the availability of high-quality healthcare technologies for all, this paper conducts a systematic and reproducible literature review of GAN-based MRI superresolution techniques and proposes a validated Integrated FourDimensional Clinical-Computational Taxonomy (4D-CCT). A literature selection procedure is followed to ensure methodological stringency and then taxonomy mapping and meta-analysis of the trends of PSNR, SSIM and perceptual assessments are done. To enhance comparability and traceability, a benchmarking and reproducibility checklist for GAN-based MRI SR studies is suggested. To facilitate responsible and scalable clinical translation in accordance with SDG-driven innovation in healthcare, a five-phase clinical translation framework is developed, which includes multicenter validation, reader studies, workflow integration and regulatory readiness. The proposed taxonomy and evaluation framework facilitate method positioning, comparison and development in accordance with the clinical context, offering a reference model for advancing trustworthy and deploymentready GAN-based MRI super-resolution systems.

title: Local Verification of Current-State Opacity and Static-State Opacity in Distributed Discrete-Event Systems
authors: Yin Tong, Kai Cai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control
abstract: The integration of physical processes and cyber-networks in cyber-physical systems (CPSs) has made information security crucial, particularly in preventing unauthorized access and data leakage. Current-state opacity (CSO) in discrete-event systems (DESs) formalizes the security property that an intruder can never infer secret states based on its observation. This paper addresses CSO and its verification in distributed DESs composed of multiple local systems. Existing methods of verifying CSO in distributed DESs either rely on sufficient conditions for local verification or require costly synchronization of local systems and observers. This paper first proposes necessary and sufficient conditions for locally verifying CSO of distributed DESs, reducing the time complexity from <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {O}(2^{n^{m}})$</tex-math></inline-formula> to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {O}(mn)$</tex-math></inline-formula>, where <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$m$</tex-math></inline-formula> is the number of local systems and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$n$</tex-math></inline-formula> is the largest state number of local systems. We also introduce static-state opacity (SSO), a new information security notion for distributed systems characterizing intruders' incapability to deduce global secrets from local ones. We show that SSO can be verified (necessarily and sufficiently) through a matrix involving CSO of local systems with a time complexity of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {O}(m2^{n})$</tex-math></inline-formula>. Furthermore, we explore the relationship between CSO and SSO, showing scenarios where they are equivalent.

title: Fuzzy IoT Temperature Regulated Storage System with Consumer-Oriented Dashboard
authors: Ryan Rhay P. Vicerra, Lance Raphael T. Beltran, Marcus Allen P. Gomeri, Arthur Caleb L. Co, Rennan G. Baldovino, Ethan Bryce S. Kaw
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: This paper presents a scalable fuzzy-based IoT temperature-regulated storage system designed to optimize conditions for temperature-sensitive products using intelligent control. The proposed system integrates IoT sensors to continuously monitor environmental parameters and applies a fuzzy logic algorithm to adjust cooling mechanisms in real time. A consumer-oriented dashboard provides users with live data visualization, alerts, and remote-control capabilities for system management. Test results show temperature stability and enhanced user engagement compared to conventional control methods.

title: Time-Variant Transformer for Predicting Alzheimer's Disease Progression from Longitudinal Data
authors: Habiba Assem Sharaf, Mohamed Hamed Kholief, Hany Hanafy Mahmoud Said
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: Alzheimer's disease gradually affects cognitive functions such as memory, reasoning, and daily activities. Although transformer-based models have improved disease progression forecasting, they often face limitations in handling uneven visit times, incomplete records, and diverse data modalities. This study introduces a Time-Visit Transformer (TVT) tailored to predict Alzheimer's progression by explicitly modeling the timing gaps between patient follow-ups. TVT (i) encodes each visit, (ii) augments representations with Time2Vec to capture the temporal phase and scale, (iii) adjusts attention using the real-time intervals between visits delta-Time-FiLM, and (iv) incorporates site-specific context through Relational-FiLM. An attention layer then reads the sequence of visits in order and can gracefully handle missing entries. In head-to-head evaluations on longitudinal cohorts, TVT achieves an AUC-ROC of 0.9331 for conversion from cognitively normal (CN) to mild cognitive impairment (MCI), and 0.9529 for MCI to Alzheimer's disease (AD), demonstrating a time-sensitive model suitable for practical clinical use.

title: Affiliate Plan of the IEEE Nuclear and Plasma Sciences Society
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Nuclear Science

title: Nine-year observation of Global Precipitation Particle Size Distribution and Radar Reflectivity
authors: Qingpeng Wu, Wei Cheng, V. Chandrasekar, Tiantian Yu, Shuangshuang Bian, Hao Wang, Qiangyu Zeng
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Understanding global precipitation microphysics is essential for quantitative precipitation estimation (QPE) and numerical weather prediction (NWP). Using nine years (2015-2023) of the Global Precipitation Measurement (GPM) mission Dual-frequency Precipitation Radar (DPR) observations over the quasi-global domain (67°S-67°N), we characterize precipitation particle size distribution (PSD) properties for rainfall and snowfall. We analyse the mass-weighted mean diameter (Dm), normalized intercept parameter (Nw), radar reflectivity (Z), and dual-frequency ratio (DFR), stratified by land/ocean, convective/stratiform type, altitude, and phase. Rainfall rates are grouped into six classes and snowfall rates into five classes to quantify intensity dependence. To interpret DPR dual-frequency signatures, we develop an observation-constrained forward-scattering framework that adopts DPR-consistent PSD and particle-shape assumptions and performs T-matrix simulations for rain, dry snow, and wet snow with varying liquid-water volume fraction (Fvw), evaluated against nine-year near-surface DFR-Z statistics. Quasi-global PSD peaks (Dm∼1.0 mm; log10Nw∼3.4) closely resemble oceanic precipitation, and convective and stratiform regimes substantially overlap in Dm (0.9-1.8 mm) and log10Nw (3.0-4.0). Vertically, Dm skewness and kurtosis decrease from 2-6 km, with distinct features near ∼10 km and ∼15 km. Rainfall DSDs become more variable at high intensities (>5 mm h-1), whereas snowfall PSDs remain comparatively stable (Dm mainly 1.0-1.5 mm; log10Nw∼3.4). Forward-scattering comparisons show that dry-snow assumptions cannot reproduce the observed DFR-Z space, while increasing Fvw yields close agreement, indicating that near-surface snowfall is consistent with wetted melted particles. These results provide observation-based constraints for improving solid-hydrometeor scattering representations in DPR snowfall retrievals and microphysical parameterizations in NWP.

title: Nonlinear Adaptive Filtering with Robust Adaptive Exponential Functional Link Networks under the Impulsive Interference
authors: Parishmita Deka, Bijit Kumar Das
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Adaptive filtering for nonlinear system identification has been explored using many different approaches, and adaptive exponential functional link networks (AEFLN) are one of the popular choices. Although performance deteriorates under the impulsive interference with non-Gaussian characteristics. To address this limitation, this work integrates a generalized soft-root-sign (GSRS) cost function with AEFLN, resulting in a robust algorithm termed AEFLN-GSRS. Performance results demonstrate that the proposed AEFLN-GSRS approach effectively mitigates the influence of impulsive noise while maintaining desired convergence behavior and achieves improved steady-state performance and enhanced robustness compared to conventional AEFLN and robust AEFLN algorithms under Gaussian and impulsive interference.

title: The Impact of Generative AI and Large Language Models on Telemedicine Diagnostics: A Systematic Review
authors: Sarthak Srivastava, Harshvardhan Chandrakar, Harshpreet Kaur
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The combination of Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) and Large Language Models (LLMs) is redefining telemedicine diagnostics by improving diagnostic accuracy, automating routine clinical tasks, and expanding access to quality healthcare for underserved and remote populations. This systematic review compiles recent evidence on the use of these technologies within telehealth environments, focusing on the diagnostic tasks they support and their clinical and operational impacts. Using a structured search and screening process, relevant literature covering language-based reasoning, multimodal image and signal analysis, and workflow augmentation was analyzed to evaluate performance, reliability, and real-world readiness. The findings indicate that current Large Language Models and multimodal systems demonstrate high accuracy in structured diagnostic contexts and can significantly reduce documentation workload. However, existing evidence remains largely preliminary and concentrated within controlled experimental settings. Major challenges hindering safe implementation include model hallucinations, limited interpretability, dataset bias, data governance and privacy concerns, and fragmented regulatory frameworks. This review highlights key research priorities such as specialty-specific validation, integration of explainable AI mechanisms, comprehensive bias auditing, and regulatory harmonization to promote responsible adoption.

title: State of Power Online Estimation for Series-Parallel Connected Lithium Titanate Battery Packs Considering Cell Inconsistencies and Branch Voltage Consistency Constraints
authors: Sidi Dong, Xuexia Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: Accurate estimation of the state of power (SOP) for series–parallel connected battery packs is essential for ensuring safe and reliable operation. However, most existing works focus on SOP estimation at the battery cell level, while investigations on series–parallel connected battery packs remain limited due to their increased structural complexity. To address this issue, a model-based online SOP estimation method for series–parallel connected lithium titanate battery packs is proposed. First, a centralized online modeling strategy based on the ensemble Kalman filter (EnKF) is developed to explicitly account for cell inconsistencies, in which the branch currents and model parameters of all battery cells are simultaneously identified online. Subsequently, a branch-voltage-oriented systematic SOP estimation algorithm that considers both cell inconsistencies and branch voltage consistency constraints is designed. The algorithm maximizes the pack power while ensuring that all battery cells operate within their safe operating areas and that the voltage consistency among parallel branches is strictly maintained. An integrated online SOP estimation framework is then proposed by combining the EnKF-based centralized model with the designed SOP estimation algorithm. The developed online model is validated under locomotive operating conditions. Experimental results demonstrate that, compared with the unscented Kalman filter (UKF)-based centralized model, the proposed EnKF-based centralized model reduces the root mean square error (RMSE) of voltage estimation by an average of 29.24% and decreases the computational cost by 59.86%, enabling more accurate and efficient tracking of cell voltage dynamics. Furthermore, the proposed framework is applied to online SOP estimation under locomotive conditions, and the effects of cell inconsistencies and branch voltage consistency constraints on SOP are systematically analyzed.

title: Core Temperature Estimation and Prediction for Batteries Using the Multi-task Coordinated Framework with Limited Data
authors: Nan Wang, Zhi Li, Zhongrui Cui, Yun Zhang, Guisen Ye
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: The core temperature of lithium-ion batteries substantially impacts their safety and reliability in electric vehicles and energy storage systems. However, its direct measurement remains challenging, particularly under complex operating conditions, wide temperature variations, and limited data due to sparse temperature sensing. To address these challenges, we propose a method that integrates a temperature-sequence diffusion model (TSDM) for data augmentation with a Bayesian optimization–gated recurrent unit–attention mechanism multitask coordinated framework. TSDM learns the thermal properties of batteries to realistically augment data, supplementing extreme-condition samples, while multitask coordinated framework enables efficient multitask coordination through the joint optimization of network parameters and task weights. This integrated approach accurately estimates the core temperature over a wide temperature range (from -10 °C to 55 °C) and achieves precise prediction up to 120 s ahead, while maintaining strong performance for aging batteries. Experimental results show maximum RMSE of 0.1720 °C in core temperature estimation and 0.8504 °C in 120-s ahead prediction, substantially outperforming existing methods. The proposed scheme provides crucial technical support for BMS in real-time thermal safety monitoring and long-term fault early warning.

title: A Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning based Offloading and Resource Allocation for Vehicle Edge Computing
authors: Huilin Yang, Xinjie Yang, Jiazhi Yang, Ruizhe Li, Nan Ma, Pei Xiao
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: In the Internet of Vehicles (IoV), vehicles have the capability to offload their computational tasks to the Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) servers in order to reduce service delay. However, the majority of existent task offloading and computational resource allocation schemes are static and lack consideration of the heterogeneous nature of tasks. Furthermore, in scenarios involving both collaborative and competitive resource utilization, there remains considerable room for performance enhancement for delay-sensitive tasks. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a novel Global Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (GHMARL) that is an enhancement to the general MARL. In GHMARL, each vehicle and MEC server is represented by an agent, and intelligent collaboration and dynamic resource allocation are employed to balance resource usage and delay performance. In particular, GHMARL introduces a global Critic network and a local Critic network, working in a collaborative manner. The former is responsible for guiding the overall system performance to ensure the service delay performance, and the latter is responsible for MEC servers’ performance to ensure the resource usage efficiency. Simulation results demonstrate that, in comparison with alternative schemes, GHMARL significantly enhances the overall system performance, particularly with regard to resource usage efficiency. Furthermore, GHMARL offers distinct advantages in balancing task delay and resource consumption under various system dynamics, making it a robust solution to address issues of resource wastage and delay violations for IoV systems.

title: High-Frequency Silicon Platelet Feedhorns for LiteBIRD
authors: Sarah A. Stevenson, Jason E. Austermann, James A. Beall, Shannon M. Duff, Nils W. Halverson, Johannes Hubmayr, Gregory C. Jaehnig
publish: IEEE Transactions on Applied Superconductivity
abstract: LiteBIRD is a planned space mission to observe the polarized cosmic microwave background in the frequency range 34 GHz to 448 GHz, with the goal of measuring or constraining the tensor-to-scalar ratio r. We have developed silicon platelet feedhorns for LiteBIRD, consisting of stacked metalized silicon wafers with deep-etched holes to create an array of feedhorns with profiled tapers and a coefficient of thermal contraction that is matched to the detector wafer. We present beam pattern measurements for silicon platelet feedhorn prototypes designed for 166-322 GHz, 200-388 GHz, and 356-448 GHz, corresponding to each of the planned LiteBIRD high-frequency pixel types. The measurements were carried out on a room-temperature millimeter-wave vector network analyzer and are in excellent agreement with simulation.

title: Towards Urban Digital Twins: A Framework Integrating CARLA with 3D City Models
authors: Shunsuke Kamioka, Taku Yamazaki, Takumi Miyoshi
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Digital twins, which reproduce real-world urban environments in virtual space, have recently attracted increasing attention. In Japan, the Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism (MLIT) is developing nationwide 3D city models through Project PLATEAU. Although PLATEAU provides detailed static information, it lacks dynamic elements such as pedestrian and vehicle motion and therefore cannot function as a fully dynamic urban digital twin on its own. Meanwhile, CARLA, a widely used open-source road-traffic simulator, offers a highly realistic simulation environment but does not provide an efficient mechanism for ingesting and updating real-world data. Existing studies have explored integrating external data into CARLA to construct digital twins; however, these approaches primarily focus on generating actors from external data and struggle to update large numbers of actors in real time, limiting their applicability to city-scale digital twins. In this paper, we propose a method for constructing a dynamic urban digital twin by importing 3D city models into CARLA and continuously updating actor states based on externally acquired vehicle information. We implemented the proposed method and demonstrated that it can reproduce large-scale urban environments while maintaining stable real-time updates for a large number of vehicles.

title: Member Get-A-Member (MGM) Program
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Nuclear Science

title: Machine learning methods for SWIPT enabled NOMA networks under imperfect SIC conditions
authors: Vegi Neelima Thulasi Aparna, A Vijayasankar, Peruri Sai Meghana, Surada Sridhar, Yaddanapudi Sarada Devi, Kota Nageswara Rao
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The integration of Simultaneous Wireless Information and Power Transfer (SWIPT) with Non-Orthogonal Multiple Access (NOMA) has shown significant promise for achieving energy-efficient and reliable communications in Internet-of-Things (IoT) networks. However, practical system performance is often constrained by imperfect successive interference cancellation (SIC), non-linear energy harvesting (EH), and channel fading, which adversely impact outage probability and overall reliability. In this study, we introduce a machine learning-driven strategy utilizing an oracle policy to minimize outage probability in SWIPT-enabled NOMA networks. This approach dynamically optimizes power allocation and splitting ratios, improving both system reliability and energy efficiency under realistic channel conditions. Simulation results indicate that the oracle-based policy surpasses traditional schemes, offering notable reductions in outage probability and valuable guidance for efficient resource management in next-generation wireless networks.

title: Computationally Efficient SDA-MPC With Capacitor Voltage Balancing for 3P-5L-ANPC Converters
authors: Zhixun Ma, Jiyuan Gong, Haichuan Niu, Xu Zhang, Zhongshu Shao, Xiang Wu
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: This paper proposes a computationally efficient sphere decoding algorithm model predictive control (SDA-MPC) method for the three-phase five-level active neutral-point clamped converter. The core of this approach is the dynamic incorporation of the neutral-point potential and the three-phase flying capacitor voltage balancing errors into the calculation of the hypersphere radius and the sphere centroid distance, which effectively achieves precise multi-capacitor voltage balancing. Furthermore, the strategy reconstructs the search tree, which contains redundant switching states, by imposing level transition constraints and sector partitioning constraints, thereby significantly reducing the maximum computational burden of the SDA-MPC. Experimental results fully validate the feasibility and superiority of the proposed method.

title: Design and Implementation of a Smart Agriculture STEAM Program in Higher Education
authors: Hirooki Aoki
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: This paper reports on the design and implementation of a short-term STEAM education program for smart agriculture. We invited two faculty members and three undergraduate students from the Institute of Technology of Cambodia and conducted intensive training that integrated image electronic engineering and agricultural engineering. The program consisted of three modules: LED matrix control for agricultural signage, three-dimensional plant measurement using an RGB-D camera, and water quality monitoring in an IoT-based aquaponics system. Undergraduate and graduate students at our university designed and delivered the classes in English. Through project-based group work, participants gained hands-on experience with core technologies for agricultural DX and developed international communication skills.

title: Enhnacing the Production Scheduling in a Multi-Product Powdered Mix Factory
authors: Lina Ismail, Farida Haytham Wagdy, Mayar Essam Yassin, Khaled S. El-Kilany
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: Efficient production scheduling remains a key challenge in modern manufacturing, especially in multi-product systems that rely on shared resources and experience sequencedependent changeovers. This study developed a discrete event simulation model in ExtendSim to enhance production scheduling performance in a powdered mix factory in Egypt. The developed model represented real production processes, including sieving, mixing, and packaging operations considering crucial factors/parameters such as sequence-dependent changeovers, machine breakdowns, and operational constraints. Validation against actual production data showed a deviation of only 6.27 %, confirming the model's accuracy and reliability. Through extensive scenario testing, the enhanced scheduling configuration achieved a 15 % reduction in average cycle time and a 17 % increase in total throughput compared with the factory's existing scheduling method. These improvements highlight the effectiveness of discrete event simulation as a decision-support tool for production scheduling and resource management. The results demonstrate that simulation-based models can significantly enhance system performance in complex, changeover-intensive food production environment.

title: Enhancing LLM Reliability and Efficiency with Query Refinement in Retrieval Augmented Generation
authors: Aya Mohamed Abd ElHamied, Ahmed A. AboelFarag, Yasser El-Sonbaty
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: Large language models have achieved great success in various natural language processing tasks, yet they still face several serious challenges, such as errors and hallucinations, and high inference cost. The contribution of this work is an extension framework called Refined Query Retrieval Augmented Generation (RQ-RAG) to enhance query handling through rewriting, decomposition, and disambiguation. The algorithm works with a specifically chosen dataset of some 42,810 samples, and search results from DuckDuckGo are used to provide answers that seemingly make sense in context. We are interested in exploiting LLaMA-4 Maverick, a state-of-the-art model with 17B parameters, as their base. We demonstrate on RQ-RAG dataset how the query refinement technique impacts upon facts' accuracy. On the single-hop (ARC-C, OBQA, POPQA) and multi-hop (HotpotQA, 2Wiki, MuSiQue) experimental results demonstrate that our method outperforms the baseline models and previous state-of-the-art retrievalaugmented approaches in terms of Accuracy and Match Score as well as F1 Score. The results show that query refinement can increase the reliability, robustness, and efficiency of LLM enabled systems.

title: Vector Perturbation Through SNR Maximization for Uplink Overload MIMO Spatial Multiplexing
authors: Ryoki Honjo, Satoshi Denno, Yafei Hou
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: This paper proposes vector perturbation techniques in overloaded MIMO spatial multiplexing suitable for uplinks of wireless systems where the number of transmit antennas is less than that of receive antennas. The number of the spatially multiplexed signal streams is increased to that of the receive antennas in spite of the number of the transmit antennas. The proposed vector perturbation comprises a lattice reduction-based transmit power reduction with modulo function and brute force search for the signal to noise power ratio (SNR) maximization. The performance of the overload MIMO with the proposed vector perturbation is evaluated by computer simulation in terms of the average SNR and the bit error rate (BER). The simulation results show that the proposed vector perturbation achieves better performance than the conventional vector perturbation in a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 \times 4$</tex> overloaded MIMO channel where the number of transmit antennas and that of the receive antennas are 2 and 4.

title: Revised Carrier Transport Model Based on Depletion Region Boundary Dynamic Equilibrium for MWIR HgCdTe
authors: Fuxing Dai, Fang Wang, Xiao Fu, Ruiqi Jiang, Xiao Zhuo, Liqi Zhu, Boris Mizaikoff, Xun Li, Chun Lin, Weida Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Electron Devices
abstract: Mercury cadmium telluride (HgCdTe) mid-wavelength infrared (MWIR) photodetectors exhibit complex nonlinear responses under high-power laser irradiation, challenging the classical boundary condition of low minority carrier concentration at the depletion region edge. We establish a revised carrier transport model by introducing a finite-concentration dynamic equilibrium at the boundary, governed by the balance between diffusion, field-driven drift, and recombination. Through combined electric field simulations and high-precision photocurrent measurements, we propose a semiquantitative formula based on a dynamic equilibrium process model, establishing the relationship between macroscopic current and microscopic electric field. The spatially resolved photocurrent mapping further visualizes the impact of voltage changes on the distribution of light response during dynamic equilibrium processes. This new dynamic equilibrium model contributes to fully revealing the carrier transport mode of narrow-bandgap semiconductors.

title: Edge Computing Energy Management with Reinforcement Learning for Smart EV Charging
authors: Sin-Hang Chang, Yao Chiang, Hung-Yu Wei
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: The rapid rise of electric vehicles (EVs) and distributed energy resources (DERs) has increased the complexity of local energy management, creating a need for intelligent coordination at the edge. This paper proposes a three-layer edge computing framework that integrates photovoltaic (PV) generation, an energy storage system (ESS), and multiple EV chargers under a unified cost objective. We adopt a multiagent deep reinforcement learning (MADRL) algorithm for EV charging control, while the ESS follows a rule-based dispatch strategy. Simulation results show that the proposed scheme effectively reduces total operating cost while maintaining grid stability and acceptable EV charging performance.

title: Modification and Testing of Patch Antenna for Wireless Communication System
authors: Maklayne Barros de Pontes Santiago, Maryam Liaqat, Maria Luysa Teles Barcelar, Matheus Henrique Barros Da Silva, Frederico Dias Nunes, Antonio Celso Dantas Antonino
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: Osteoporosis is a progressive bone disease characterized by reduced bone mineral density, leading to increased fracture risk. Conventional diagnostic techniques such as X-ray and dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) involve ionizing radiation and high operational costs, limiting their use for continuous monitoring. Mineral deficiencies, notably calcium and Vitamin D, precipitate a critical decline in bone mineral density (BMD), resulting in structural degradation and heightened fracture susceptibility—a condition exemplified by severe hypocalcemia observed in regional postmenopausal populations (e.g., Peshawar, Pakistan, is below 1300 mg/day WHO standard). The early diagnosis is the solution of this hypocalcemia which can be possible by Microwave Imaging (MWI). Dielectric properties of defected bones can be measured using MWI, non-conventional non-ionizing methods. Furthermore, its reliance on low-cost, miniaturized MWI as an ideal candidate for a portable, point-of-care diagnostic system. The MWI technique offers a pivotal solution for the early-stage stratification and preventative management of osteoporotic defects, mitigating future morbidity associated with pathological bone fragility. In this work, a modified dual-band microstrip patch antenna is proposed for microwave-based bone health assessment.

title: Distributed Triangulation Filter for Target Motion Estimation via Bearing-Only Measurements
authors: Yuhan Liu, Pei Chi, Jiang Zhao, Song Jiang, Luning Yang, Xuyang Gao, Yingxun Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This paper introduces the novel consensus-based distributed spatial-temporal coordinated triangulation filter (CD-STCTF), a unified framework designed to overcome the fundamental challenge of achieving reliable and high-precision motion estimation of non-cooperative targets using bearing-only measurements in multi-agent systems. First, our approach centers on the spatial-temporal coordinated triangulation (STCT) model, which optimally integrates historical and spatial bearing information to transform the nonlinear problem into a robust, pseudo-linear state estimation problem. Second, this powerful geometric linearization is embedded within a consensus-on-information-matrix distributed architecture, ensuring robust operation and network consistency across multi-agent systems with sparse communication topologies. Third, a key technical feature is the incorporation of a dynamic measurement noise covariance matrix, which correctly models the distance-dependent uncertainty in angular measurements. Furthermore, we prove the global bounded stability of the CD-STCTF dynamic error. Simulation and real-world experimental results against existing distributed algorithms and the centralized Kalman filter (CKF) benchmark demonstrate that the CD-STCTF achieves superior estimation accuracy, faster convergence rates, and enhanced robustness and is comparable to the CKF, establishing its efficacy for high-performance cooperative bearing-only target motion estimation.

title: A Flight-to-Safety Principle for Cost-Sensitive Distributionally Robust Log-Optimal Portfolio
authors: Chung-Han Hsieh, Xiao-Rou Yu
publish: IEEE Control Systems Letters
abstract: This paper investigates how distributional ambiguity interacts with transaction costs in a log-optimal portfolio control formulation. Return ambiguity is modeled by a 1-Wasserstein ball centered at the empirical distribution, and trading frictions are incorporated via a convex, positively homogeneous transaction cost function applied at each rebalance. We establish a flight-to-safety principle: as trading frictions rise, the critical ambiguity radius—the smallest radius beyond which the optimizer retreats entirely to the risk-free asset—weakly decreases. Rolling-window experiments on S&P 500 constituents illustrate this mechanism, showing that higher transaction costs systematically shift the robust allocation toward the risk-free asset.

title: An Architecture to Provide Sensory Expansion for Deaf and Hard-of-Hearing Individuals in Digital Television
authors: Juliana C. Silva, Claudio F. M. Pinheiro, Eddie B. de Lima Filho, Rômulo Fabrício, Rafaell G. Santana
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Television systems offer accessibility features for people who are deaf or hard of hearing, such as Closed Captioning or sign language, but these resources often lack personalization for different levels of hearing loss. This paper presents a system architecture that leverages smartphones connected to digital TV receivers to capture audio from the tuned service and perform real-time audio processing and tactile vibration generation. The proposed architecture enables personalized audio adaptation based on user hearing profiles and explores the use of smartphone-based haptic feedback to enrich television accessibility. Rather than focusing on clinical validation, this work aims to describe the design principles, processing pipeline, and integration mechanisms required to support future assistive television applications for deaf and hard-of-hearing users.

title: NOMA-Enabled Joint Sensing and Communication for MISO-Based Non-Terrestrial Networks
authors: Wali Ullah Khan, Jaehyuk Choi, Fahd N Al-Wesabi, Hadeel Alsolai, Xingwang Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: This letter proposes joint sensing and communication (JSAC) for a high-altitude platform station (HAPS) that serves downlink non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) users while simultaneously sensing a ground target. Unlike designs that allocate a dedicated sensing beam, we illuminate the target with the superimposed communication beams and process the echo with a single receive combiner. We formulate a joint transmit–receive design that minimizes the sensing mean-squared error (MSE) under user's minimum rate constraints. The nonconvex problem is addressed by alternating optimization, where the original joint problem is solved in two stages. First, solve the HAPS transmit beamformers problem using semidefinite programming, with the fixed combiner. Then, with the given beamforming, we update the receive combiner in closed form using the linear minimum MSE solution. The proposed NOMA-JSAC solution in HAPS guarantees a monotonic MSE decrease and converges to a stationary solution with low computational complexity. Simulation results demonstrate a clear rate–sensing trade-off versus transmit power, validating the practicality and effectiveness of the proposed NOMA-enabled HAPS JSAC design.

title: Novel Nanocrystalline Alloy Symmetrical Magnetic Focused Eddy Current Array Sensing for Microcrack Detection
authors: Xumei Yang, Qiuping Ma, Bin Gao, Rui Chen, Jiaqi Zhou, Chen Zhu, Yukuan Kang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: Nondestructive detection and characterization of micronear-surface cracks is a critical and challenging task for evaluating material performance. However, existing methods are limited by low sensitivity, poor anti-interference capability, and bad adaptability to complex surfaces. This article proposes a novel eddy current array microcrack detection method based on a symmetrical magnetic focused sensing structure. In this design, a symmetrically distributed magnetic path generates a focused and uniform eddy current region of interest (ROI), significantly enhancing the field intensity for high-sensitivity sensing. This advancement improves detection sensitivity and anti-interference capability for microcracks. Theoretical derivations and simulations validate the effectiveness of the proposed method. In particular, experimental tests conducted on various samples with microcracks demonstrate the reliability and applicability of the approach. The proposed method is capable of detecting artificial crack defects with a width and depth of 0.2 mm at a liftoff of 4 mm, as well as natural weld cracks with minimum detectable dimensions of approximately 5 × 0.07 × 0.07 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>. Furthermore, the array design can cover the entire convex weld region with a width of 10 mm, enabling single scan imaging of multioriented cracks with dimensions of 10 × 0.1 × 1 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>. It is applicable to the detection of surface and near-surface cracks in both ferromagnetic and nonferromagnetic materials.

title: Identifying Barely Visible Impact Damage in Carbon Fiber Reinforced Plastic Using a Raster-Rotational Transmit-Receive Eddy Current Probe
authors: My Nguyen, Ian Gravagne, Scott Koziol
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper presents a system for identifying barely visible impact damage (BVID) in carbon fiber reinforced plastic (CFRP) using a raster-rotational transmit-receive (TR) eddy current probe. The system can differentiate among: 1) Barely visible impact damage (BVID), which has left a small barely visible indentation with some internal damage and 2) Barely visible no damage (BVND) which is just a small barely visible indentation with no internal damage and 3) Areas without any visible impact damage or internal damage (non-BVID). The probe runs in a raster-rotational pattern to image BVID defects over an area at different rotational angles to account for the anisotropic nature of carbon fiber. A coupon with barely visible indentations without internal damage was manufactured to test for lift-off effects. Pre-processing methods are applied to compensate for noise and uneven scan surfaces during the scanning process. Visual and thresholding techniques are then employed to classify a BVID area. After the procedure, a measurable difference among BVID areas, BVND areas, and non-BVID areas were identified. The lift-off effect from the BVND areas could be measured, and they are still distinguishable from BVID parts.

title: Novel Deep Learning Architectures for Soybean Kernel Defect Classification
authors: Akshay Kumar, Niteen Sapkal, Ankur Miglani, Dheeraj Kumar, Pavan Kumar Kankar
publish: IEEE Transactions on AgriFood Electronics
abstract: Reliable identification of soybean kernel defects is essential for automated quality inspection in grain processing and export systems. However, current approaches face challenges when dealing with subtle defect patterns and heavily imbalanced class distributions. This study introduces a soybean kernel defect classification framework built on a high-resolution dataset of 9866 red, green, and blue images spanning eight defect categories, including underrepresented classes, such as purple-mottled and stained, stinkbug damage, and tear damage. To address performance degradation in rare categories, we propose a novel unified training pipeline that combines data augmentation and class-weighted focal loss. The proposed pipeline is evaluated using three deep learning architectures: EfficientNet-B0, ResNet-50, and the hybrid convolution–attention model CoAtNet0. Experimental results demonstrate that CoAtNet0 achieves the highest performance, with 83% accuracy and a 6%–9% improvement in F1-score over convolutional neural network baselines. gradient-weighted class activation mapping analysis further confirms improved localization of defect-relevant regions in CoAtNet-0, supporting stronger recognition of minority classes. Overall, this work highlights the benefit of combining hybrid modeling, imbalance-aware training, and visual interpretability to enable practical, scalable deployment for real-world soybean kernel defect inspection.

title: Distributed Fixed-Time Secondary Control for Microgrids With Communication Delays and Disturbances
authors: Xin Li, Xiaodong Wang, Mingfei Ban, Yiqi Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Smart Grid
abstract: The paper addresses the distributed secondary control (DSC) problem of the islanded microgrid composed of six distributed generators (DGs) with communication delay and external disturbances. Firstly, by utilizing the Artstein model reduction method, a distributed delay-compensated strategy is proposed to restore the voltage and frequency of each DG to reference values in a fixed time. Secondly, a novel sliding mode reaching law is designed to resist disturbances and accelerate convergence, thereby ensuring the sliding surface is reached within a fixed-time. Thirdly, the proposed approach integrates an event-triggered (ET) mechanism with an integral sliding mode surface, effectively conserving communication resources while avoiding Zeno behavior. In summary, the proposed delay-compensation strategy integrated with sliding mode control (SMC) guarantees the fixed-time synchronization of voltage and frequency for DGs with coordinated power sharing control. Finally, the simulation results validate the control strategy’s effectiveness, robustness to load changes, plug-and-play capability, and indicate that the proposed ET mechanism can greatly decrease communication frequency.

title: An Adaptive Load-Shedding and Thermal Management (ALSTM) Framework for 5G AND 6G Infrastructure Resiliency in Tropical Climatic Zones
authors: J.O. Aji
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The increased intensity of fifth-generation (5G) wireless infrastructure deployment, has brought about significant challenges of systems thermal management, specifically like in high ambient temperature environments which is the characteristic of tropical regions. The traditional thermal control mechanisms, which are usually reactive do not properly mitigate sustained thermal stress overtime, which leads to performance degradation, reduced reliability, and increased operational expenditure (OPEX).This report proposes an Adaptive Load-Shedding and Thermal Management (ALSTM) framework, which is designed to improve thermal resilience in climate constrained telecommunications infrastructure. The proposed approach introduces a proactive, thermal aware workload regulation mechanism; that dynamically adjusts process demand based on real time thermal conditions, effectively decoupling the workload intensity from temperature increase. Simulation results demonstrate that, the ALSTM framework maintains CPU operating temperatures within a safe threshold of 80°C - which is significantly below the critical limit of 85°C. The proposed method, achieves an estimated shift in Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) of 18% to 35%, thereby reduces the peak temperature by approximately 12% and cooling energy consumption by about 20% to 30%. These improvements, translate into an an extimated overall site level energy savings of about 10% to 18%. These outcomes thereby transslates into an estimated reduction in maintenance driven capital expenditure (CAPEX) of 12% to 25% over a period of 5 years. The analysis indicates that there is a reduction in temperature difference of 30% which confirms an enhanced thermal stability and operational consistency. These results also showed a practical trade off between instant network throughput and thermal reliability, which demonstrates that a controlled workload adaptation can prevent sudden system failures while ensuring near continuous service availability. The proposed ALSTM framework provides a scalable and data driven solution for improving the reliability, energy efficiency, and sustainability of next generation telecommunications systems, particularly in emerging markets and environmentally constrained deployment scenarios.

title: CNN-Based Point Cloud Geometry Compression with Feature Extraction for Object Recognition
authors: Hideaki Kimata
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Many studies have advanced to improve performance in both compression coding and object recognition for point clouds. A point cloud comprises numerous points, resulting in large data volumes, thus efficient compression methods are desirable. However, high compression using CNNbased methods is anticipated to reduce the object recognition performance. Point cloud compression techniques suitable for object recognition are necessary. We propose a novel compression method that improves object classification performance even at low coding rates by training the network to perform feature extraction for object recognition, achieved through modifying the loss function during DNN training for point cloud geometry compression. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method can enhance object classification performance.

title: A Smart Virtual Makeup Try-On and Recommender System Using Artificial Intelligence and Augmented Reality
authors: B. Lanitha, Anushya R, Jagasri V R, Preethi P P, Sowmiya P
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The cosmetics industry is rapidly adopting artificial intelligence (AI) and Augmented Reality (AR) to improve customer experience and personalization. Unfortunately, most of the virtual makeup test applications currently in use rely on flimsy overlays and ignore important aspects such as skin conditions, lifestyle, and allergies, which results in sub-par recommendations and unhappy users. In this paper a new AI-based framework for a smart virtual makeup try-on and recommender system that combines deep learning, augmented reality, and computer vision is presented. For high resolution skin analysis (acne, pigmentation, dryness, and redness), the system uses Vision Transformers (ViT/FastViT). For contextual personalization, Tabular Transformers/MLPs are used to process user profile data. Visual and profile features are dynamically balanced by an attention-based fusion mechanism, and recommendations are transparent thanks to Explainable AI (SHAP/LIME). Immersion and interaction are made possible by a real-time AR try on module that superimposes recommended items on the user’s face. In this work, personalized recommendations, AR visualization, and skin health analysis are combined to provide safer, more reliable, and user-focused beauty technology for the cosmetics and e-commerce sectors.

title: From Large Language Models to Reasoning and Agents
authors: Miroslaw Staron, Silvia Abrahão

abstract: Software engineering is shifting from standalone large language models to reasoning-driven and agent-based systems. Drawing on ASE 2025 research, this column highlights how AI agents now test, debug, adapt, and explain software, making a transition toward more autonomous and observable AI-assisted development.

title: SLD: Segmentation-Based Landmark Detection for Spinal Ligaments
authors: Lara Blomenkamp, Ivanna Kramer, Sabine Bauer, Theresa Schöche
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: In biomechanical modeling, the representation of ligament attachments is crucial for a realistic simulation of the forces acting between the vertebrae. These forces are typically modeled as vectors connecting ligament landmarks on adjacent vertebrae, making precise identification of these landmarks a key requirement for constructing reliable spine models. Existing automated detection methods are either limited to specific spinal regions or lack sufficient accuracy. This work presents a novel approach for detecting spinal ligament landmarks, which first performs shape-based segmentation of 3D vertebrae and subsequently applies domain-specific rules to identify different types of attachment points. The proposed method outperforms existing approaches by achieving high accuracy and demonstrating strong generalization across all spinal regions. Validation on two independent spinal datasets from multiple patients yielded a mean absolute error (MAE) of 0.7 mm and a root mean square error (RMSE) of 1.1 mm.

title: Dynamic On-Resistance and Dynamic Threshold Voltage of GaN-on-Si HEMTs With Varying Substrate Connections in Steady-State Hard Switching
authors: Hongchang Cui, Xin Yang, Zineng Yang, Hengbo Zhang, Hehe Gong, Yuhao Zhang
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: The dynamic stability of GaN-on-Si HEMTs with different substrate connections is a critical for understanding the device reliability and designing the substrate bias management circuits for monolithic bidirectional switches. Prior studies have mainly focused on dynamic on-resistance (R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ON</inf>) characterization using pulse I–V or single-pulse double-pulse tests, which deviate from practical operating conditions. In addition, dynamic threshold voltage (V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">TH</inf>)—essential to understanding R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ON</inf> shifts-has rarely been evaluated under different substrate conditions. This work presents a circuit test platform enabling in-situ characterization of dynamic R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ON</inf>, dynamic V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">TH</inf>, and substrate bias under steady-state hard-switching. Using this setup, GaN-on-Si HEMTs are tested under source-connected, drain-connected, and floating substrate configurations. Under the floating-substrate condition, we find the substrate bias exhibits oscillatory behaviors between positive and negative values after reaching the steady-state hard switching. A physics-based model is further developed to capture the substrate bias dynamics. The combined measurements and modeling clarify the mechanisms behind dynamic R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ON</inf> shifts under different substrate configurations and can guide the future development of active substrate bias management for GaN-on-Si HEMTs.

title: A Survey of Federated Graph Learning in Real-World Applications
authors: Lijun Guo, Zhihao Deng, Rong Li, Long Xu, Peng Gao, Lijia Ma
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: Federated learning (FL) combined with graph neural networks (GNNs) offers a powerful solution for processing distributed graph data, particularly in privacy-sensitive domains such as healthcare and finance. Building on this potential, this survey provides a systematic and structured overview of recent advances in federated graph learning (FGL). We propose a problem-driven taxonomy that organizes existing FGL research into four core challenges: (i) graph-specific characteristics, (ii) heterogeneity, (iii) privacy preservation, and (iv) communication efficiency. For each category, we review representative solutions and analyze their underlying design principles. In addition, we summarize commonly used datasets and evaluation metrics, and provide insights into their application scenarios. Finally, we discuss emerging trends, open problems, and promising future research directions, aiming to offer a comprehensive reference for the continued development of FGL.

title: Deep Learning-Based Fetal Lung Segmentation and Lung Development Evaluation from Diffusion-Weighted Mri Images
authors: Zhennan Xiao, Katharine Brudkiewicz, Zhen Yuan, Rosalind Aughwane, Magdalena Sokolska, Joanna Chappell, Trevor Gaunt, Anna L. David, Andrew P. King, Andrew Melbourne
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Fetal lung development is critically important for optimal neonatal outcomes. Neonates with structural anomalies or from pregnancies affected by fetal growth restriction often suffer chronic lung disease due to poor lung development, but this is difficult to predict prenatally. Intra-voxel incoherent motion (IVIM) analysis shows promise for non-invasive fetal lung development assessment but relies on labor-intensive manual segmentation, which limits its clinical applicability. In this work, we present an automated fetal lung development evaluation pipeline for diffusion-weighted magnetic resonance images that consists of a deep learning-based fetal lung segmentation model and a model-fitting lung development assessment. A 3D nnU-Net model was trained on manually segmented images selected from the baseline frames of 4D diffusion-weighted MRI scans. The segmentation model demonstrated robust performance, yielding a mean Dice coefficient of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$82.14 \pm 6.68 \%$</tex>. There was no statistically significant difference between IVIM parameters derived from automated and manual masks, demonstrating the reliability of the automated approach for quantifying tissue microstructure and perfusion. This single-centre feasibility study indicates that automated segmentation can provide IVIM measurements equivalent to manual delineation.

title: Center-Aware Detection with Swin-Based Co-DETR Framework for Cervical Cytology
authors: Yan Kong, Yuan Yin, Hongan Chen, Yuqi Fang, Caifeng Shan
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Automated analysis of Pap smear images is critical for cervical cancer screening but remains challenging due to dense cell distribution and complex morphology. In this paper, we present our winning solution for the RIVA Cervical Cytology Challenge, achieving 1st place in Track B and 2nd place in Track A. Our approach leverages a powerful baseline, integrating the Co-DINO framework with a Swin-Large backbone for robust multi-scale feature extraction. To address the dataset's unique fixed-size bounding box annotations, we formulate the detection task as a center-point prediction problem. Tailoring our approach to this formulation, we introduce a center-preserving data augmentation strategy and an analytical geometric box optimization to effectively absorb localization jitter. Finally, we apply track-specific loss tuning to adapt the loss weights for each task. Experiments demonstrate that our targeted optimizations improve detection performance, providing an effective pipeline for cytology image analysis. Our code is available at https://github.com/YanKong0408/Center-DETR.

title: Parallel Route Coherent Mixup Deep Learning for Single Source to Multi-Domain Generalized Prostate Cancer Segmentation for Mr Guided Adaptive Radiotherapy
authors: Josiah Simeth, Sudharsan Madhavan, Andreas Wibmer, Daniel Gorovets, Sean McBride, Himanshu Nagar, Victoria Brennan, Neelam Tyagi, Harini Veeraraghavan
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) guided adaptive radiotherapy for prostate cancer (PCa) allows daily treatment adaptation to the geometry of tumor and organs to deliver precise treatments while sparing organs from radiation. However, manual delineation of tumors and organs limits the adoption of this technology. Deep learning (DL) methods, though promising for in-distribution (ID) data that resembles training, deteriorate in performance when applied to out-of-distribution (OOD) datasets. Hence, we developed a novel parallel-route coherent feature mixup (PaRC-mix) training approach for single source to multi-domain generalization. PaRC-mix performs feature augmentations at multiple network layers through matching linear combinations of features extracted across training batches. PaRC-mix training was implemented in UNet++ to segment PCa from apparent diffusion coefficient images and compared to other mixup approaches. Models were trained on 2,029 samples from 3.0T GE MRI and tested on 557 PCa samples from 3 datasets used for diagnosis, treatment planning, and during radiation scans acquired using 3T Siemens, 3T Philips, and 1.5T Elekta Unity MR-Linac scanners from public and institutional datasets. PaRC-mix training led to significantly more accurate tumor detection (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{p}<$</tex> 0.001) and segmentation (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{p}<0.001$</tex>) compared to other methods.

title: Adapt: Multibeamformer with Tunable Weight Fusion and Patch-Wise Learning for Ultrasound Imaging
authors: Gopika Gopikrishna, Mahesh Raveendranatha Panicke, Timothy Li, Ng Aik Ben, Simon Chong-Wee Se
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: In the recent past, deep learning (DL)-based beamformers have been employed to accelerate adaptive beamforming for ultrasound imaging. However, most of these approaches are trained to mimic a particular beamformer type for a predefined depth of imaging and require complete retraining to adapt to a new beamformer and depth. In this work, we propose a novel deep learning framework, ADAPT (Adaptive Depth-Agnostic Patch-wise Tunable-multibeamformer), that employs a depth-wise patching strategy for computational efficiency and depth-independent learning. In order to develop a beamformer agnostic framework, the proposed approach first trains identical neural networks separately to derive the individual beamforming algorithm model weights, which are then fused via a tunable weighted averaging mechanism. This provides clinicians with dynamic and adaptable diagnostic perspectives, blending the acceptability of delay and sum (DAS) beamformer with the contrast of filtered delay multiply and sum (FDMAS) beamformer and the resolution of minimum variance (MV) beamformer simultaneously at very low complexity. ADAPT achieves 73 % speedup in training, 94 % reduction in computations and 94 % reduction in activation memory over a model trained over full depth.

title: Oculomix: Hierarchical Sampling for Retinal-Based Systemic Disease Prediction
