title: Single Inverter Driven Segmented DWPT Transmitter Track Based on Separated-Rectangular Quadrature Pad
authors: Hai Xu, Junwei Liu, Jingyu Wang, Fei Xu, Xian Zhang, Zhicong Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: Stable output feature and wide array distance are the contradictions of segmented dynamic wireless power transfer (DWPT) systems. Hybrid coupler designs, such as double-D quadrature pad (DDQP) and rectangular solenoidal pad (RSP), are effective technical routes to mitigate this contradiction based on the magnetic flux complementary. However, the magnetic flux zero-crossing polarity reversal results in the need for a specific angle driven via dual-inverters or additional detection and active control efforts, which increases circuit components, size, cost, and complexity of DPWT systems. To overcome this challenge, this paper proposed a novel single inverter driven segmented DWPT transmitter track, which integrates auxiliary separated-rectangular pads and quadrature pads (SRQPs), while the receiver side configures a simple rectangular pad. This segmented coupler exhibits axisymmetric and peak-to-valley complementary features of mutual inductances during dynamic displacement to achieve stable output. Such that this design serves to reduce circuit complexity and simplify the control method, thereby eliminating the need for active detection and control mechanisms. Additionally, a novel DWPT system with a self-decoupled transmitter design method to eliminate cross-coupling effects and a detailed parameter design methodology for further stable power output based on the proposed coupler is developed. Finally, a 300 W DWPT experimental prototype is built to experimentally verify the proposed design. The experiment results show that the output current is around 3 A with less than 4.5% fluctuation, and the efficiency can be maintained at about 89% during the receiver pad displacement process.

title: Announcing the Shaw Prize in Computer Science
authors: Jennifer T. Chayes, Tony F. Chan, Jack Dongarra, John L. Hennessy, Yann LeCun, Harry Shum, Joseph Sifakis, Jeannette M. Wing
publish: Communications of the ACM
abstract: None

title: Ethical dimensions of Artificial Intelligence in Social Science: Integrated Digital Pedagogy and Fuzzy System in Decision Making
authors: Dhenesh Raj, Pankaj Kumar, Nilufar Omonova, Abdimumin Usmanov, Sultanov Rustem Abdiraximovich, Muminjon Salayev
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper examines the ethical considerations of the AI in the social science depending on how fuzzy decision-making systems and digital pedagogy are used. This raises such a challenge as the lack of transparency, fairness, and ethical use as the AI-driven tools gain more and more control over the social simulations, learning environment, and behavioral analysis. The given paradigm will accept the ambiguity and complexity of social processes by combining the paradigm of fuzzy logic with AI-enhanced digital pedagogy. Coupled with other ethical issues of data protection, algorithm bias, accountability, and informed permission, this integration would lead to more adaptable learning engagements and more intricate decision aids. The paper determines the need of the ethical principles that promote inclusiveness, interpretability, and equity by examining the connection between fuzzy systems and digital learning in the AI-assisted social science scenario. The results can be applied in the creation of human-friendly AI solutions to improve the result of learning and decision making without infringement on the rights or social values of a person.

title: Multimodal AI for Stroke Detection and Subtype Classification
authors: Ramjivasanth M, Abishkar A, Manisekar R, Mrs.K. Nithya
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Throw remains a leading cause of mortality and disability worldwide, really,, emphasizing the need for rapid and reliable diagnostic tools. Existing AI models face key limitations, including reliance on a single modality, poor handling of class imbalance, and simplistic feature fusion. To overcome these challenges, we propose a multimodal framework that integrates clinical records and CT imaging through a two-stage balanced learning strategy. Notably, course, as a vision expert for stroke subtype recognition, First,, kind of, a convolutional neural network ( CNN ) is pre-trained on a class-balanced dataset. Look, naturally, its representations are then fused with clinical embed-dings using a transformer-based attention mechanism, followed by fine-tuning with a freeze-thaw cycle. Obviously, experiments on public datasets prove strong performance, achieving 90% recall for binary throw prediction and recalls of 90.5 % ( ischemic) and 87.5 % ( hemorrhagic ), outperforming unimodal and naïve merger baselines. These findings underscore the clinical potential of advanced multimodal coalition for generalizable shot diagnosis.

title: Discrete Diffusion-Based Sampling for Massive MIMO Detection
authors: Lanxin He, Zheng Wang, Zhen Gao, Shaoshi Yang, Yongming Huang, Dusit Niyato
publish: IEEE Transactions on Communications
abstract: In this paper, we study a sampling-based detection strategy for massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems, driven by a modified discrete diffusion model formulated as an analytical, non-learning sampling process. Built upon this framework, the proposed discrete diffusion-based sampling (DDS) algorithm improves decoding performance by leveraging residual-dependent sampling, compared to the independent randomized successive interference cancellation (SIC). Specifically, the modified diffusion model incorporates a shortcut perturbation toward the SIC solution, a forward diffusion step to enhance diversity, and step-wise alignment with the perturbed received signal. Within this framework, the DDS algorithm further adopts one-dimensional discrete Gaussian distribution, involving a reformulated discrete Gaussian noise and an explicitly characterized sampling range, but retains computational complexity amenable to practical deployment. Moreover, we theoretically demonstrate an improved expected decoding radius over randomized SIC. Finally, simulation results based on massive MIMO detection are presented to confirm performance gain of the proposed DDS algorithm.

title: A Multi-Layer Smart Computing Architecture for Techpreneurial Optimization Using Machine Learning and Evolutionary Algorithms
authors: Syed Rizwan Ali, Shaikh Zunain, Muhammad Faraz, Talha Bin Shuja, Muhammad Mumtaz Khan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: The growing number of techpreneurial ventures are faced with high-stakes decisions in uncertain environments with limited resources and rapidly changing technological and industrial environments. In this research paper, the authors propose and expand upon their Smart Computing Techpreneurship Optimization Framework (SCTOF). The SCTOF is a three-layer conceptual model which includes: (i) A Cognitive Intelligence Layer (using AI/ML technology) which allows predictive sensing and identifies opportunities; (ii) An Evolutionary Optimization Layer (using e.g. Genetic Algorithm, Particle Swarm Optimization or Ant Colony Optimization) which allows adaptive resource allocation and strategy tuning; and (iii) An Entrepreneurial Strategy Layer to allow human experimentation to interface with computational guidance. Using an analytic theoretical design (including exploratory synthesis and cross-domain mapping) the researchers defined the key constructs (decision agility, computational opportunity recognition and resource-allocation efficiency) and identified measurable indicators. To establish the first empirical basis for testing SCTOF, the researchers developed a small proof-of-concept simulation using synthetic startup portfolio data in comparison to a baseline heuristic to compare performance of SCTOF inspired optimization to a baseline heuristic under conditions of volatility. The results showed faster decision-making and improved allocation efficiency, indicating that SCTOF has potential to be used as a testable artifact in future empirical studies.

title: Sign Language Detection for Physically Challenged People
authors: V. Rajesh, Siva Reddy Vanga, K Anagha Lakshmi, G Narasimha, K Sai Kavya
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Communication without spoken words often depends on body movements to deliver messages. Actions involving the hands, facial features, lips or other body parts are collectively referred to as gestures. The approach described in this paper, hand gesture recognition to interpret the signs commonly used by Persons with hearing and speech impairments. The aim of is to close the communication gap between them and the general population. Earlier methods employed accessories such as colored Gloves or markers for the detection of gestures. However, these often caused processing delays and created usability issues. However, these often caused processing delays and posed usability issues. The method proposed in this work avoids such tools instead focusing on natural hand gesture recognition to improve detection speed, enable real-time application and enhance overall system accuracy. Unlike earlier studies that relied only on either sensor-based or vision-based methods our work brings both together. By combining IMU sensors (MPU6050 and flex sensors) with vision techniques (MediaPipe enhanced by CLAHE preprocessing) we developed a real-time recognition system that is specifically designed for assistive applications.

title: Non-volatile and Volatile Memristors for Hybrid Logic-in-Memory Computing and Analog Neuromorphic Computing
authors: Tong Li, Qingxin Chen, Zhentao Yue, Jialin Meng, Tianyu Wang
publish: IEEE Electron Device Letters
abstract: This study proposes an in-memory computing circuit for non-volatile and volatile memristors based on respective models and designs related to digital logic and analog neural network circuits. Compared to traditional CMOS circuits, the hybrid circuit requires fewer components and lower power consumption. The logic circuit based on non-volatile memristors achieves stable logic functions, including AND and OR. The volatile memristors realize four key characteristics of biological neurons for neuromorphic computing. A sound localization circuit was developed by simulating the binaural time difference of the human ear, enabling azimuth recognition. These results show great application potential in digital computing and brain-inspired neuromorphic computing.

title: Robust Wearable Motion Recognition Using a Two-Stage FMG Pipeline with Rest Filtering and Placement Adaptation
authors: Ye-Jin Lee, Kyoung Tae Kim, Keonyoung Oh, Kang-Ho Lee
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Wearable force myography (FMG) sensors enable continuous motion monitoring through mechanical interaction with soft tissue, but their practical deployment is challenged by rest-related signal ambiguity and placement-induced variability. This study proposes a two-stage Action Discriminator– Action Classifier (ADAC) pipeline that explicitly separates rest-dominant segments from motion-related signals and applies domain-adaptive inference to mitigate placement-induced distribution shifts. Using a lightweight two-channel air-bladder cuff sensor, the ADAC pipeline achieves stable motion recognition under realistic wearing conditions. Under unified cross-participant evaluation with pooled multi-subject data, the proposed framework demonstrates an overall accuracy of 94.9% and an F1-score of 87.9% using cuff-only sensing, without requiring subject-specific calibration. Compared with conventional FMG systems that rely on dense sensing configurations or per-user recalibration, the ADAC pipeline achieves robust performance using only two pressure channels through integrated rest-aware filtering and placement-adaptive classification. These results demonstrate the feasibility of lightweight cuff-type FMG sensors for continuous motion recognition in real-world, multi-user scenarios and underscore the importance of system-level design in pressure-based wearable sensing.

title: Image-Guided Navigation Control for Automated Nanopositioning Based on Dense Photometric_supp1-3688826.mp4
authors: Hai Li
publish: N/A
abstract: Image-guided automated positioning of microobjects in dynamic environments is highly needed in robot micro–nano manipulation. In this article, rendering the problem of collision-free microobject nanopositioning problem as a visual servoing problem, an image-guided navigation control scheme based on dense photometric is proposed. Specifically, the finite convergence domain existed in classical dense photometric-based visual servoing is treated as a constraint and uniformly modeled together with obstacle constraints in the navigation process. Subsequently, an online constraint handling unit based on artificial potential fields is developed to generate collision-free poses while consistently constraining the image evolution to remain within the local convergence region. Additionally, an inverse compositional algorithm is adopted to accelerate control computation, thereby enabling real-time performance. Finally, a series of experiments are conducted to verify the performance of the proposed method. Experimental results demonstrate that the method achieves reliable automatic nano-positioning and effective collision avoidance in both static and dynamic obstacle environments, confirming its feasibility and robustness in practical applications.

title: Role-based Adaptive Coordination for Resilient Signal Coverage in Stratospheric Balloons_supp1-3694500.pdf
authors: Zhenglin Liang
publish: N/A
abstract: Superpressure balloons in the stratosphere are emerging as a promising platform for extending network connectivity to remote and disaster-affected regions. Yet, their dependence on altitude control and absence of horizontal maneuverability render them highly susceptible to wind drift, leading to unstable coverage. Coordinating multiple balloons further challenges current multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) methods, as wind-induced moderation effects can simultaneously disrupt all agents. Here we present a novel Adaptive Role-Switching Cooperative Learning (ARSCL), a dual-policy MARL framework that achieves resilient, cooperative coverage through adaptive role specialization. ARSCL assigns complementary roles —a main policy maintaining primary coverage and a compensatory policy introducing diversity to counteract correlated drift—linked by a dynamic role-switching mechanism that adapts to spatial deviation. A drift-prevention module further constrains learning to feasible trajectories. Simulations demonstrate that ARSCL achieves an average coverage duration exceeding 94% within a 25 km radius over a 24-hour period, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art MARL baselines in both efficiency and resilience.

title: Synthetic Volumetric Data Generation Enables Zero-Shot Generalization of Foundation Models in 3D Medical Image Segmentation
authors: Satrajit Chakrabarty, Sourya Sengupta, Gopal Avinash, Ravi Soni
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Foundation models such as Segment Anything Model 2 (SAM 2) exhibit strong generalization on natural images and videos but perform poorly on medical data due to differences in appearance statistics, imaging physics, and three-dimensional structure. To address this gap, we introduce SynthFM-3D, an analytical framework that mathematically models 3D variability in anatomy, contrast, boundary definition, and noise to generate synthetic data for training promptable segmentation models without real annotations. We fine-tuned SAM 2 on 10,000 SynthFM-3D volumes and evaluated it on eleven anatomical structures across three medical imaging modalities (CT, MR, ultrasound) from five public datasets. SynthFM-3D training led to consistent and statistically significant Dice score improvements over the pretrained SAM 2 baseline, demonstrating stronger zero-shot generalization across modalities. When compared with the supervised SAM-Med3D model on unseen cardiac ultrasound data, SynthFM-3D achieved <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2-3 \times$</tex> higher Dice scores, establishing analytical 3D data modeling as an effective pathway to modality-agnostic medical segmentation.

title: Optimization Framework for Hard-Switching Transients of Power Semiconductor Devices to Minimize Switching Losses and EMC-Relevant Noise Emissions
authors: Jochen Streit, Robert Kragl, Manuel Riefer, Hadiuzzaman Syed, Karl Oberdieck, Ingmar Kallfass
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This paper presents an optimization framework for hard-switching transients of power semiconductor devices that simultaneously minimizes switching losses and electromagnetic emissions (EME) while respecting component constraints. The framework integrates a state-space simulation environment with lab-verified SiC MOSFET models, a genetic algorithm-based optimizer providing quasi-continuous gate current control through variable resistance arrays, and a novel cost function incorporating the Heisenberg-Gabor inequality for broadband EME quantification. Unlike existing approaches focusing on sequential parameter adjustment or single-frequency ringing reduction, this framework enables systematic analysis of loss-EME trade-offs with high temporal resolution. The framework reveals that optimized gate current profiles exhibit bell-shaped pulses achieving sigmoidal voltage transitions, establishing a foundation for future work, including gate loop design and power semiconductor device optimization.

title: A High-Power Compact Capacitive WPT System for EV Charging
authors: Raquel Sarabia-Soto, Dheeraj Etta, Syed Saeed Rashid, Khurram K. Afridi
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Large airgap capacitive wireless power transfer (WPT) systems are increasingly achieving higher power levels, bringing their use in electric vehicle (EV) charging closer to reality. However, the high-power systems demonstrated so far are large and not suitable for deployment in EV charging applications, whether stationary or dynamic. This digest presents a compact, lightweight capacitive WPT system in which the power electronics have been reduced in size and co-packaged with the charging pad, enabling seamless integration with the EV underbody and the road. The system’s compact design is achieved by optimizing the design of the matching network and power combining components to reduce their size. These inductors and transformers are realized using optimal toroidal geometries with innovative interleaved winding designs that enable size reduction while retaining high quality factor. This approach to miniaturization also reduces electromagnetic interference without compromising efficiency. Additionally, a lightweight charging pad designed with optimized dielectric profile contributes to lowering the overall system thickness, weight, and cost. A 10-kW 6.78-MHz 12-cm airgap compact capacitive WPT system with a power transfer density greater than 150 kW/m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> is developed. An experimental prototype based on this architecture is built and tested up to 7.1 kW, obtaining a peak efficiency of 82%.

title: DIFF-MAMBA: Efficient State-Space Modeling for End-To-End Ultrasound Video Diagnosis
authors: Jinghan Yang, Tian Jie, Kun Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Breast cancer remains the most common malignancy among women worldwide. B-mode grayscale ultrasound is widely used for screening and diagnosis in dense breast tissue due to its safety, accessibility, and cost-effectiveness. While conventional computeraided diagnostic (CAD) systems rely on static frames, clinical interpretation depends on temporal cues such as probe motion, boundary stability, and posterior acoustic changes. To address this gap, we propose Diff-Mamba, an end-to-end framework that directly processes grayscale ultrasound videos and leverages inter-frame differences to capture motion-consistent structural and textural patterns. Built upon the Mamba selective state-space model, Diff-Mamba efficiently encodes long-range temporal dependencies while preserving temporal continuity. The dual-branch attention pooling module further integrates spatial and temporal information for malignancy prediction. Experiments on two public ultrasound video datasets demonstrate that Diff-Mamba achieves state-of-the-art performance compared to existing deep learning baselines. The code and dataset resources are publicly available at https://github.com/hanliumu/DIFFMAMBA.

title: Evidence-Grounded Temporal Reasoning in Operating Room Long Video Understanding
authors: Yaoqian Li, Jinpeng Li, Pheng-Ann Heng
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Operating rooms (OR) are complex, high-risk environments that demand long-horizon video understanding for intelligent surgical assistance and quality assurance. Existing domainspecific OR models and datasets mainly address short-clip or frame-level analysis, lacking the ability to reason across extended temporal contexts. In contrast, general long-video multimodal large language models (MLLMs) exhibit strong temporal reasoning but fail to capture the structured interactions and clinical semantics unique to surgical workflows. To bridge this gap, we introduce OR-LongVQA, a benchmark for long-video understanding in operating rooms, covering diverse factors and causality-centered questions. We further propose a reasoning retrieval framework based on temporal scene graph and evidence-grounded reasoning modules that retrieve relevant subgraphs and perform interpretable step-by-step inference. We compare the proposed reasoning framework with existing state-of-the-art methods on the OR-LongVQA benchmark. Our approach outperforms prior methods by achieving significant improvements in longhorizon reasoning accuracy and interpretability, demonstrating its effectiveness and reliability for clinically-oriented, structured operating room video understanding.

title: AI Integrated Cybersecurity Risk Assessment Framework
authors: Ragesh K, Hariharan J, SanjayKumar S, Sarathi Ganesh S, P. Mohan Kumar
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: The blistering-growing digital infrastructures, including Internet of Things (IoT), cloud infrastructures and cyber-physical systems, have greatly augmented the elements of complexity in the contemporary cyber environments. This development has also brought more cybersecurity threats particularly in those systems, which are based on artificial intelligence (AI). Conventional models of cybersecurity risk assessment are commonly rigid and they do not take into account AI-specific threats like adversarial attacks, model evasion, and data poisoning. This research presents an intelligent framework of AI-based cybersecurity risk assessment tool that will help assess dynamic threat in smart settings. The framework combines risk scoring based on the machine learning algorithm, adversarial threat modeling, explainable artificial intelligence methods, and the real-time threat intelligence. The architecture proposed is that which constantly monitors the risks in the AI lifecycle such as the data collection phase, the model training phase, the deployment phase, and the operational phases. The experimental assessment shows the enhanced threat detection and increased the risk transparency and adaptive support to the security management of complex digital ecosystems.

title: Design and Control of a GaN-Based High-Frequency Converter for Low-Voltage Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle with Extreme Voltage Gain
authors: Sang Min Lee, Hyeon Soo Kim, Yun Seong Hwang, Seung Hyun Kang, Ju-A Lee, Joo Seung Lee, Ju Han Kim, Jeong Hee Park, Byoung Kuk Lee
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This paper proposes a DC-DC converter with extreme voltage gain suitable for a 55kW rated fuel cell electric vehicle (FCEV) with fuel cell (FC) stack in the range of 50 to 150V to enhance cost effectiveness. The proposed converter can achieve a voltage gain more than 10 times, which satisfies the voltage range of 350 to 650V for driving motor even with a low FC voltage around 100V. Additionally, the efficiency of proposed converter is measured over 96% under maximum power conditions. The operational modes according to the duty ratio are theoretically analyzed, and its feasibility is verified through experimental results of a 2.6kW prototype.

title: Fast Frequency Regulation with Supercapacitor Energy Storage Systems
authors: Michael Hetzel, Roberto Bellapianta, Jonghun Yun, George Weiss, Federico Silvestro, Giovanni De Carne
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: In this paper, the influence of grid following Supercap Energy Storage System (SCESS) on the frequency response of power systems is investigated. For this purpose, a single machine model with a gas turbine for primary control is augmented by a SCESS. To present the realistic behavior of SCESSs, the analysis is conducted using a 1 MW Power Hardware in the Loop (PHIL) setup comprising a Real Time Simulator (RTS) and a SCESS with a nominal power of 400 kW. The SCESS is operated using a Virtual Synchronous Machine (VSM) control algorithm to provide virtual inertia by injecting active power, thereby contributing to grid frequency stabilization. With the introduced model, a study is performed with parameters that mimic the whole behavior of the European Network of Transmission System Operators for Electricity (ENTSO-E) grid to show which impacts SCESSs can have on frequency regulation. The results demonstrate that SCESSs can enhance Rate of Change of Frequency (RoCoF) and the nadir of the grid significantly.

title: Electrical model and characterization of near-field antennas for communications from induction cooktop
authors: Javier Hernandez, Oscar Lahuerta, Sergio Llorente, Francisco Villuendas, Jesús Acero
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Induction cooktops can act as wireless power transmitters thanks to their internal structure, being able to transfer up to several kW of power. This range is well suited for real-time wireless operation of other household appliances such as cooking robots, which would be wirelessly powered by the cooktop itself and therefore would not require connection to the power grid. For successful operation, communication is generally required between the transmitter and receiver, which can be achieved by means of a near-field antenna placed between the heater and the receiver. These antennas are typically designed to operate at a frequency of 13.56 MHz with impedance matching to the amplifier. However, the elements that are part of the induction heating system can affect the impedance of the antenna and may compromise its correct operation. In this work, the influence of these elements is analyzed.

title: Multi-Query: Information Extraction-Driven Multi-Perspective Query Generation for Financial Report Generation
authors: Jiali Li, Yinshuang Liao, Cong Pan, Yihao Wang, Jingyao Meng, Jianeng Zhuo
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: For the task of generating financial reports from financial news, generating systems typically face numerous challenges, including insufficient original news information, semantic redundancy, and a lack of necessary domain-specific financial knowledge. These issues make it challenging for the generated content to strike a balance between accuracy and professionalism, thereby limiting the effectiveness and scalability of existing methods in practical applications.To address this issue, this paper proposes an optimized Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) method, which incorporates a structured information extraction module and combines a novel multi-view query generation strategy. Unlike traditional RAG methods that rely on a single original query or template-based queries, which often lead to semantic dilution and inaccurate retrieval, this paper introduces a novel approach. First, we build a structured Information Extraction module based on Machine Learning Comprehension (MRC) and a Pointer Network mechanism to automatically identify and extract key event elements from financial news texts. Then, using the extracted structured event information, we design prompt templates and input them into a Large Language Model (LLM), guiding it to generate multiple semantically focused natural language queries (Multi-Query) from several predefined semantic perspectives. Finally, we conduct systematic experiments on the public News-Report dataset to evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed method. The results show that, compared to full-text generation methods using raw news as query input and template-based query construction strategies, our Multi-Query generation approach achieves significant improvements on mainstream text generation evaluation metrics such as BLEU and ROUGE.

title: Evaluation and Application of Kilovolt GaN-on-Sapphire Diode IC
authors: Zineng Yang, Hengbo Zhang, Hehe Gong, Xin Yang, Hongchang Cui, Yuan Qin, Yuhao Zhang
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This work presents a comprehensive evaluation of a kilovolt-class monolithically integrated GaN-on-sapphire diode bridge IC designed for high-frequency, low-current, high-voltage power conversion. Building on our latest application of this IC in a 10 MHz, 1000 V Cockcroft-Walton voltage multiplier (CWVM), this study further studies the diode’s switching behaviors, the CWVM’s loss analysis, and the diode IC’s voltage scalability. Experimental double-pulse testing confirms the absence of reverse recovery charge, validating the unipolar nature of the device for megahertz operation. A detailed decomposition of the CWVM multiplication gain (Av) reveals that performance losses are primarily dominated by static forward voltage drops and load-dependent drops, while dynamic switching losses remain negligible due to the low-parasitic monolithic design. Furthermore, an investigation into the dynamic voltage blocking mechanism of the monolithically-integrated, series-connected diodes identifies a non-uniform voltage distribution governed by the parasitic capacitance network, where the top-most diode sustains the highest voltage stress. These results provide critical insights for the design and scalability of future monolithic high-voltage GaN ICs.<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>

title: Mixed Reality and Desktop Hand Hygiene Training with Deep Learning–Based Step Recognition and Real-Time Decision Support
authors: Syed Muhammad Umair Arif, Tomiris Rakhimzhanova, Abylaikhan Myrzakhanov, Huseyin Atakan Varol
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Hand hygiene (HH) is essential for preventing healthcare-associated infections, yet conventional monitoring approaches primarily capture event occurrence and provide limited insight into procedural quality, timing, and individualized feed back. To address these limitations, we present a real-time HH training and assessment framework that combines deep-learning based WHO step recognition with a protocol-aware decision support engine deployed on both a Desktop LED display and a Mixed Reality (MR) headset. The system supports two complementary modes: Concurrent-Feedback Coaching (CFC), which provides real-time sequence guidance and corrective prompts, and Uncued Retention Assessment (URA), which evaluates un guided execution and summarizes detected steps and errors. To support real-time deployment, we retrained and evaluated YOLOv12+MV, TimeSformer, and TSM on four heterogeneous HH datasets (PSCUH, Jurmala, METC, and Kaggle). While several YOLOv12 variants achieved strong recognition performance, the compact YOLOv12-n+MV model provided the most favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off for deployment, achieving F1-scores of 0.99, 0.87, 0.72, and 0.58 across Kaggle, Jurmala, METC, and PSCUH, respectively, with low computational cost. This lightweight recognizer was integrated with temporal majority voting and a protocol-aware controller to support stable closed loop interaction on Desktop and HoloLens 2. We evaluated the framework in a controlled mixed-methods study with N = 20 participants using a 2×2 design (Desktop vs. MR; CFC vs. URA). Desktop yielded significantly faster, more temporally stable, and less error-prone HH performance than MR, whereas CFC reduced total completion time and URA reduced weighted mistake scores, indicating a speed-accuracy trade-off. Subjective results showed higher perceived usability for Desktop than for MR and for CFC than for URA. NASA-TLX further showed a higher workload for MR than Desktop across five subscales under counterbalancing, while URA increased perceived effort relative to CFC. Overall, these findings suggest that Desktop is more suitable when efficiency, stability, and lower workload are priorities, whereas CFC and URA can be selectively used to emphasize guided acquisition or independent recall within Desktop and MR HH training workflows.

title: IEEE Women in Engineering
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Terahertz Science and Technology

title: Research on High-Speed Interrogation Method for Optical Fiber Fabry–Perot Sensors Based on Photonic Time-Stretch
authors: Yuru Chen, Miao Hu, Liang Jin, Jie Luo, Meihua Bi, Mengmeng Xu, Kairen Xu, Lijun Cheng
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: Optical fiber Fabry-Perot (FP) sensors are widely used for measuring parameters such as strain and temperature. However, they still face limitations in measurement rates when dealing with high-frequency parameters like vibration and explosive shock. The time-stretch-based demodulation method for FP sensors, for the first time, integrates FP sensing technology with photonic time-stretch technology, mapping the FP interference signals in the frequency domain to the time domain. This approach combines the advantages of high-speed time-domain acquisition and high-precision frequency-domain interference demodulation, representing a promising development trend

title: Distance-Aware World Model-based Reinforcement Learning for Mobile Manipulation Behaviors
authors: Xiaoxu Feng, Takato Horii
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Separate control of mobile manipulation offers a promising paradigm for managing its complexity by planning for these modalities independently. However, end-to-end learning of such behaviors necessitates effective mechanisms for both embodiment selection and motion planning. For low-level motion planning, prior world model-based reinforcement learning (RL) approaches have employed the similarity of latent states as a dense reward proxy. However, using latent similarity as an implicit distance metric yields inaccurate representations and depends heavily on predefined goal states. In this work, we address the limitations of similarity-based rewards by explicitly integrating 3D spatial information into the world model and deriving more accurate rewards from that distance-aware model for policy training. For high-level embodiment selection, prior model-based methods have either utilized reachability rewards inferred from predicted task rewards or relied on heuristic composite rewards. In this study, we retain the reachability reward and demonstrate that it yields superior performance in embodiment selection compared with composite reward formulations. By combining reachability-based selection and distance-guided planning, the model improves the performance of end-to-end learning for separate mobile manipulation behaviors. Extensive evaluations were conducted across two simulation environments with both fixed and randomized target settings. For motion planning, the distance reward achieves near 100% success rates across all configurations, significantly outperforming the baseline similarity reward, which achieves only 60% success in fixed-target scenarios and drops below 20% when targets are randomized. Regarding embodiment selection, the reachability-based separate control rapidly achieves a stable result with over 85% of arm activations occurring rationally within the near-goal region. Conversely, the heuristic composite reward slowly increases to a maximum of 60% for near-goal arm selections. These findings validate that the reachability reward provides a more effective and efficient mechanism for embodiment selection.

title: Bridging Domain Gap and Preserving Privacy for Battery Diagnosis in Data Centers
authors: Yitong Shang, Thomas Tongxin Li, Peijin Li, Junqing Tang, Man-Fai Leung, Duo Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: Data centers, the cornerstone of the digital economy, depend on Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS) systems, where battery health is critical for operational continuity. However, the practical application of data-driven anomaly detection is severely hampered by two interconnected challenges: distribution shifts between different battery batches degrade model generalization, while data silos arising from commercial and privacy constraints prevent cross-institutional data aggregation. To overcome these barriers, we systematically formulate this industrial challenge as a Federated Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (FUDA) task, which enables collaborative model training without sharing raw data. The core contribution of this paper is the proposal of a novel scenario-driven adaptation strategy, which posits that no single algorithm is universally optimal. We demonstrate that for homogeneous scenarios with small domain gaps, a lightweight discrepancy-based method (CORAL) paired with a powerful feature extractor (Transformer) is most effective. Conversely, for heterogeneous scenarios with large domain gaps, a stable, decoupled adversarial approach (ADDA) combined with a capacity-constrained feature extractor (DeeperMLP) is superior as it ensures convergence and promotes generalization. Experiments on a real-world data center dataset validate our FUDA approach (F1-score of 0.872) not only significantly outperforms local non-adapted models but also achieves performance highly comparable to, and in some cases exceeding, that of privacy-violating centralized models (0.908).

title: Resilient Tube-Based Model Predictive Control With Polytopic Constraints Subject to DoS Attacks
authors: Shuang Shen, Junhong Liu, Runqi Chai, Yuanqing Xia, Senchun Chai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: Tube-based model predictive control (TMPC), a well-performed control algorithm in disturbed control scenarios, is widely applied in networked control systems (NCSs). Due to the inherent nature of communication networks, NCSs always are at risk of being attacked. Consequently, the pristine TMPC-based systems may undergo severe destruction. However, the field about resilience of this control method against cyber-attacks in communication links is still in its infancy. In this article, we consider a resilience scheme based on an actuator buffer to eliminate the harmful effects caused by denial-of-service attacks. In addition, we assess the inherent attack tolerance of TMPC by calculating the maximal permissible open-loop steps, the maximal steps with no feedback control while provably maintaining recursive feasibility and input-to-state stability, for arbitrary states. The effectiveness and merits of the proposed resilience scheme and algorithm are demonstrated through numerical comparisons, furthermore, implemented on a three-wheeled omnidirectional robot platform.

title: Traffic Flow Forecasting Based on Complex Gray Relational Degree Network and Multi-Period Exattention-GRU Model
authors: Zhuoxuan Li, Jinde Cao, Yifan Chen, Mahmoud Abdel-Aty, Leszek Rutkowski
publish: IEEE Transactions on Big Data
abstract: Traffic flow forecasting is vital but challenging in traffic management. This study proposes a complex gray relational degree-based network to cluster traffic flow observation nodes using the Louvain algorithm. The method effectively distinguishes the nodes, providing accurate clustering for equivalent training sets and traffic flow prediction. To capture the characteristics of time series data in traffic flow data, the gated recurring unit (GRU) is the backbone network. For improved accuracy, a multi-period Exattention-GRU model is developed. GRU enhances network performance as the main component with an external attention mechanism and a residual structure. Stacking and fusing the improved GRU network captures various traffic information, which contributes to improving accuracy. The model, trained on an equivalent set using the RAdam algorithm, demonstrates effectiveness with favorable prediction results across various datasets and traffic conditions. Taking into account 36 data sets, the average metrics for RMSE, MAPE and MAE are recorded as 60.6363, 7.40%, and 41.4897.

title: Centroid-Guided Continuous Adaptation for Class-Incremental Source-Free Unsupervised Domain Adaptation of Ground Source Heat Pump Units
authors: Shouqi Wang, Xuejin Gao, Huayun Han, Hongyan Yang, Yue Liu, Huihui Gao, Yongsheng Qi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: During long-term service, the fault types of ground source heat pump (GSHP) units gradually evolve with system aging. At the same time, constrained by engineering conditions, source-device data are usually unavailable at the model deployment stage, which makes cross-device diagnostic models prone to negative transfer and catastrophic forgetting when adapting to incremental faults. To address the above issues, this article proposes a class-incremental source-free unsupervised domain adaptation method based on centroid-guided continual adaptation. First, a gramian angular summation field is adopted to transform 1-D data into 2-D images, and the initial model training is completed using source-device data, after which the source data are inaccessible. Subsequently, a centroid-guided shared class mining method is designed to identify the shared classes at each incremental stage, and a collaborative discriminative pseudolabel assignment method is used to generate reliable pseudolabels for target domain data to drive model training while constructing a memory bank. Furthermore, a prototype topology distillation mechanism is introduced to maintain the topological consistency between new and old classes at the prototype level, effectively mitigating the impact of new class knowledge and thereby alleviating catastrophic forgetting. Validation on real-world GSHP industrial data indicates that, under the condition that source data are inaccessible, the proposed method demonstrates good cross-device incremental fault diagnosis capability, achieving an average accuracy of 80.20%.

title: Residual-Augmented Dual-Layer Koopman Modeling for Distributed Parameter Systems
authors: Yaru Zhao, Han-Xiong Li, Yun Feng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: Given that many industrial processes are governed by distributed parameter systems (DPSs), it is crucial to develop online models capable of robust spatiotemporal estimation under nonlinear, uncertain, or even entirely unknown conditions. To address this need, this article proposes a residual-augmented dual-layer Koopman modeling framework for DPSs, enabling real-time state estimation. First, an offline dual-layer Koopman model is constructed using Koopman randomized orthogonal decomposition and the lifting function to avoid nonlinear approximation. An adaptive term is then added to enable online coupling with a residual extended state observer. This allows the observer to estimate the mismatch between the process’s temporal dynamics in the lifting function space and the adaptive dual-layer Koopman model. The full spatiotemporal dynamics are then reconstructed through an inversion and time-space synthesis module. Additionally, a rigorous convergence guarantee is provided for the framework. The feasibility and effectiveness of the method are validated through two benchmark case studies with different boundary conditions, and its practical utility is demonstrated in real-time temperature monitoring of battery thermal processes.

title: Enhancing the Operational Efficiency of Immigration Inspection: An Empirical Study
authors: Chun-Ting You, Liang-Bi Chen, Szu-Yu Kuo
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Airport immigration inspection is crucial for national security. this study explores and recommends the deployment of an intelligent language assistance system at the arrival immigration counter, enabling travelers to communicate with immigration officers without efficiency loss due to language barriers. Finally, the study's results show that immigration officers are very willing to support and use this system.

title: A Boundary-Aware Surrogate Model for Predicting Feature-Scale TiN Etching Profiles
authors: Quanzhi Long, Fuqiang Zheng, Ting Yao, Xiaoming Shi, Xuqing Zhang, Dawei Gao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Semiconductor Manufacturing
abstract: In semiconductor manufacturing research, AI efforts mainly concentrate on virtual metrology and defect inspection, outputting scalars or labels rather than full cross-sectional morphology. Feature-scale plasma etch simulators remain the primary way to obtain the morphology by numerically evolving the etched front, but they require meticulous physical modeling and take tens of minutes per recipe. This cost and sensitivity make systematic process-window exploration and design–technology cooptimization (DTCO)-style iteration impractical. We propose a boundary-aware, time-conditioned surrogate model that maps six process parameters and absolute etch time to a 256×256 TiN cross-section. The model is a lightweight decoder trained with an edge-weighted composite loss and evaluated through a single unified boundary pipeline. We report recipe-level boundary-F1 and HD95 with right-continuous P90 tails, and treat pixel MSE/SSIM as secondary checks. On 80 TiN etch recipes (2.4 k frames) generated by Latin hypercube sampling, compared with a capacity-matched U-Net decoder, the surrogate model has reduced the mean HD95 value by about 10%, tightened the tail distribution (HD95 P90 from 1.047 px to 0.986 px), and lowered MSE by about 12%. Generating an entire about 30-frame trajectory for one recipe takes about 53 ms on a laptop-class GPU, which is about 3×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sup> times faster than a commercial topography solver that takes 28 min per recipe. These improvements can be attributed to absolute time conditioning, CoordConv-based spatial localization, and deep-supervised edge-weighted loss, offering the promise of near-interactive exploration of etching evolution hypotheses.

title: Design of Reliable PUF Cells at Subthreshold
authors: Li-Kai Lin, Hongchin Lin, Fang-Hsing Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Scalable PUF (Physical Unclonable Function) circuits biased at subthreshold regions with low bit error rates (BER) were designed and characterized. The proposed SRAM-like PUF cells were fabricated using the 90nm CMOS process and occupied an area of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1100 \boldsymbol{F}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex> /bit. By controlling the gate bias voltage of the PUF cells in subthreshold, the measurement results demonstrate good properties of randomness, stability, low bit error rate (BER), and reliability of the PUF cells. Thus, it is a good candidate for high-reliability and low-cost PUF key generation circuits.

title: Advanced Machine Learning-Based Framework for Short-Term Renewable Energy Power Forecasting
authors: Nishant Rajput, Quish Raj, Omit Singh, Prabhat Kumar, Shailesh Pratap Singh, Pankaj Kumar Das, Kanhaiya Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Reliable short-term forecasting of renewable energy output is critical both for maintaining grid stability and for effective participation in energy markets. Both wind and solar energy are highly intermittent phenomena, making it extremely challenging for traditional forecasting models to accurately forecast these energy sources. This paper presents a critical comparison of three different machine learning models: LSTM, XGBoost, and SVR, with regard to predicting short-term outputs for both wind and solar energy. Weather patterns for these predictions are obtained from NASA’s POWER database. The performance of these models is analyzed using Mean Absolute Error, RMSE, and Coefficient of Determination on varied training periods from historical data. The findings of this paper clearly establish XGBoost as the best compromise between forecast accuracy and processing speeds, followed by SVR with relatively lower processing overheads, while LSTM models are constrained by data granularity as well as lack of foresight regarding weather patterns.

title: Efficient Frequency-Domain Modeling for Lossless Recompression of Variable-Rate JPEG Images
authors: Hui Xiang, Yifan Bian, Yuqi Li, Haotian Zhang, Li Li, Dong Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: JPEG images are ubiquitous in various digital platforms, yet their compression efficiency is limited. Several lossless recompression methods have been proposed for JPEG images to further reduce storage and transmission costs. However, existing lossless recompression methods often neglect the frequency-specific characteristics, incurring suboptimal compression performance. These methods also struggle to support JPEG images with different bitrates efficiently. To address these limitations, we propose an efficient learned method that leverages frequency characteristics while addressing variable-rate challenges. We present a frequency-based masking model, which captures the frequency-specific dependencies across different discrete cosine transform coefficients. We suggest a frequency-specific probabilistic model that tailors entropy coding to the unique distributions of different frequency bands. We also incorporate the quantization table into the entropy model using a conditional convolution to support variable-rate images efficiently. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed method surpasses the previous state-of-the-art single-rate method by an average of 5.24% bitrate saving over multiple JPEG quality factors, while achieving faster encoding and decoding speeds.

title: A Machine Learning-Assisted High-Precision Fiber Optic FPI Pressure Sensor
authors: Jiaxin Liu, Yizhou Jiao, Yuxiang Zhao, Jing Yang, Peng Ye, Xinjie Li, Jinhui Shi, Chunying Guan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: A high-precision optic fiber Fabry-Perot interferometer (FPI) pressure sensor is proposed and experimentally demonstrated. The Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) model is employed to demodulate the pressure signal and suppress the temperature crosstalk. The sensing FP cavity is designed as a bowl shape and incorporates the Vernier effect to amplify the differences in the reflection spectra of the pressure sensor under different gas pressures, which can improve the prediction accuracy performance of the GPR model. Comparing with the traditional wavelength demodulation method, the root mean square error (RMSE) of the pressure measurement can be reduced from 21.64 kPa to 0.77 kPa (a 96.4% reduction) in the temperature range of 25 – 75 °C by using GPR model. Due to the high precision and resistant to temperature interference, this sensor has great potential for application in high-precision air pressure measurement.

title: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
authors: N/A
publish: N/A

title: Botnet Elimination via C&C-Based Collective Control of White-Hat Worms in Immune-Based Botnet Defense Systems
authors: Toshimi Nakano, Shingo Yamaguchi
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: With the explosive proliferation of Internet-ofThings (IoT) devices, botnet threats typified by Mirai have become increasingly serious. As a countermeasure, an Immunebased Botnet Defense System (iBDS) has been proposed, in which white-hat worms (antibody and phagocyte) are used to suppress malicious worms. However, in conventional iBDS, each worm relies on random exploration, which results in low efficiency in discovering and eliminating malicious worms. To overcome this limitation, this paper proposes a collective control method based on a Command and Control (C&C) server. The C&C server maintains the global network state as an infection ledger and computes binary Go/NoGo scores that control the movement of white-hat worms so that defensive resources can be concentrated on important nodes. Simulation results show that while the conventional method achieved a complete elimination success rate of 0%, the proposed method achieved 64%, demonstrating that C&C-based collective control is effective for botnet elimination.

title: Comparative Performance Analysis of Machine Learning Methods on Depression Dataset
authors: Ketaki Vinayak Gokhale, Rajeshri Shelke
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Early detection of depression can help mitigate serious outcomes in mental health. In this work, we propose a machine learning framework for classifying depression status from survey or social media datasets. The contributions are (i) designing a general preprocessing and feature-engineering pipeline for depression datasets, (ii) applying and comparing multiple classical machine learning classifiers (Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, Random Forest, KNN, SVM, XGBoost), and (iii) reporting detailed comparative metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, ROC-AUC) and analysis of results. We conduct experiments on a public depression dataset (e.g. PHQ-9 dataset) and perform 10-fold cross validation. Among the classifiers, Random Forest and XGBoost deliver the highest performance, with an average accuracy exceeding 88 % and ROC-AUC above 0.91. We also analyze feature importance, misclassifications, and tradeoffs across metrics. The results suggest that ensemble methods are promising for depression classification, and that balanced sampling and careful feature engineering are vital. Future work may extend this to multimodal data (text, EEG) and deep learning architectures.

title: Efficient Nonlocal Tensor Function Representation for 3D Seismic Random Noise Attenuation
authors: Shengrui Wang, Yisi Luo, Bangyu Wu, Jiangjun Peng, Yueming Ye, Junxiong Jia
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: High-dimensional 3D seismic data is inevitably contaminated by random noise due to environmental interference and acquisition system limitations. While many deep learning methods have been proposed for random noise attenuation, they often suffer from various limitations. For instance, some convolutional neural network (CNN)-based methods tend to be computationally inefficient when processing 3D seismic volumes, while implicit neural representation (INR)-based methods frequently fail to recover complex structural details owing to the spectral bias. To address these challenges, this study introduces an efficient nonlocal tensor function representation (NLTFR) for unsupervised 3D seismic denoising. Specifically, we cluster similar cubes within 3D seismic data into nonlocal similar groups. To efficiently and effectively exploit the nonlocal self-similarity inherent in seismic data, we employ tensor function factorization parameterized by INRs to model these clustered nonlocal similar groups. Furthermore, we propose a partially shared transfer learning strategy for NLTFR to accelerate model convergence when applied to large-scale seismic datasets. Multiple-frequency sinusoidal functions are incorporated into the INRs to mitigate spectral bias, thereby enhancing the model’s representation ability. Additionally, a weighted total variation and a second-order 3D total variation are introduced to improve denoising performance and robustness of the NLTFR model. Comprehensive experiments conducted on synthetic and field 3D seismic datasets demonstrate that the proposed NLTFR achieves a superior balance between efficiency and effectiveness compared with conventional deep learning methods. Overall, our method attains an average performance gain of 1.5dB while significantly reducing execution time relative to traditional deep learning methods for 3D seismic volumes, underscoring its practical efficiency.

title: Transmission Gate Logic Based Counter for Biomedical Applications
authors: Varun Chaurasia, Vibhuti Baldva, Mansi Jain, Tarni Joshi, Kirti Bhargava, Disha Sharma
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper presents a compact and power efficient auto-reloadable, negative-edge triggered 8-Bit Binary Up Counter implemented using Transmission Gate Logic based D-Flip Flop using 2:1 MUX-based latch which finds its application especially in biomedical field. The proposed design follows standard binary counting principles and supports cascading for higher bit-width applications. It is optimized at transistor-level having all its blocks full-custom designed and implemented in Cadence Virtuoso using SCL-180nm Technology and simulated under 1V and 1.2V because of the low voltage input requirements in the healthcare field, wherein the measurement results show that the power dissipated is 13.47µw and 19.71µw respectively, making the design suitable for biomedical and low-power digital systems.

title: Smart Energy Saving Socket with Automatic Power Cutoff
authors: Pasala Surya Akhil, Rayabarapu Siva Teja, Puppala Koushik Sai Venkat, Sunkara Teena Mrudula, Shaik Rezwaan, Alla Bhargav
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: In recent years, safety (i.e., electrical safety) and conservation (i.e., energy conservation) have been of great increased interest with regard to both automated systems and "smart home" technology. This paper presents a design and implementation of a Smart Energy Saving Socket with Automatic Power Cutoff based on monitoring of the load current with the intention of automatically disconnecting the electrical supply anytime the measured current falls below or equal to a predefined threshold of 0.035 A.The system employs an Arduino UNO microcontroller, an ACS712 Hall-effect current sensor, and a relay module, and operates as a standalone unit without reliance on wireless or cloud-based communication technologies to create a Smart Socket that automatically redirects electricity away from the Smart Socket when a connected device draws little to no current – thereby preventing wasteful use of energy and eliminating so-called "phantom load" or "vampire" loads during standby periods [10], [5]. This design represents an efficient and cost-effective way to manage energy consumption and promote "smart home" applications.

title: Evolution of ML and AI Techniques for DDoS Detection and Mitigation in SDN: A Comprehensive Review
authors: Nilesh Kumar, Amrit Lal Sangal
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Software-defined networking (SDN) is a significant technology for building network infrastructures that can be centrally controlled and programmed. The flow table, on the other hand, is very easy to attack with Denial-of-Service (DoS) and Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) assaults because it doesn’t have much space and its control plane is logically centralized. A lot of research was done on machine learning (ML), deep learning (DL), hybrid, and fully in-network AI-driven solutions for finding and stopping these kinds of attacks from 2017 to 2025. This analysis looks at some of the most important studies that came out between 2020 and 2025. There are papers about supervised learning models, online ensemble methods, graph-based architectures, generative frameworks, programmable data-plane strategies, and flow-table defenses that are based on learning to rank. The studies that were looked at are all put into one taxonomy and compared based on response characteristics, deployment layers, performance measures, and datasets. This shows that AI frameworks that work in real time, on two planes, and in the network are taking the place of offline systems that are based on controllers. The study finds three main problems with current research methods: they don’t protect well against zero-day and low-rate assaults, they rely too much on lab datasets, and they don’t work well with production-grade SDN systems. The paper finishes by saying that more research is needed on explainable and adaptive detection, graph- and GAN-based anomaly modeling, cross-domain benchmarking, and making AI pipelines with programmable data planes to make SDN protection that can grow and is reliable.

title: One Disease, Two Worlds: An Explainable AI (XAI) Implementation for Interpretation of Risk Factors of Heart Failure Outcomes
authors: Qisthi Alhazmi Hidayaturrohman, Eisuke Hanada
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Machine learning can play an essential role in building a decision-making support system for use in clinical settings that helps quicken diagnosis and that can predict the risk of disease, in this case for heart failure. Unfortunately, a lack of trust from clinicians arises when the decision-making process of the models is not well explained, especially when the model applies to different demographics but is able to make a similar disease diagnosis. This study uses the SHAP method to explore the use of explainable artificial intelligence for model building through transfer learning with two different resources. We built a LightGBM predictive model that employs a novel cross-domain approach to improve a poor, low resource dataset by leveraging a rich, high resource dataset. This study revealed that this crossdomain approach enhanced the predictive performance of the model, with an absolute improvement of 12.86% in F1-score. Furthermore, the proposed approach still allowed the model to be interpreted with the XAI approach: 80% of the top 20 features of both cohorts that appeared in the feature importance analysis were overlap.

title: Bridging Social Science with Artificial Intelligence Fuzzy Framework Smart Pedagogy for bright minds in Decision Making Process
authors: Amit Kumar Mishra, Savithri H., Firuza Khayitova, Moxira Kasimova, Asror Sabirov, Sarvarbek Matniyazov
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The growing sophistication of social, economical, and behavioral problems requires pedagogical frameworks with the capacity to strengthen analytical thinking and decision-making skills. Conventional social science education costs frequently depend on a linear and deterministic approach to evaluation which is not responsive to uncertainty, subjectivity and cognitive diversity of human learning. In this research, it is proposed to use empirical machine learning-based fuzzy artificial intelligence structure to assist smart pedagogy in social science teaching. The architecture combines monitored ML algorithms with the fuzzy logic to capture learner thinking, interaction, and reasoning behaviors, therefore, facilitating instructive decision support. Data collected among the students of undergraduate social sciences were experimentally evaluated based on classification and prediction models with a fuzzy inference system. Findings show that the suggested ML-fuzzy smart pedagogy provides much more efficient solutions in decision making, critical thinking, and application of concepts when compared to traditional pedagogical methods. The research also initiates a new field of interdisciplinary research between social science and artificial intelligence, which is explainable and human-based learning systems.

title: SketchOGD: Memory-Efficient Continual Learning
authors: Youngjae Min, Benjamin Wright, Jeremy Bernstein, Navid Azizan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Signal Processing
abstract: When machine learning models are trained continually on a sequence of tasks, they are often liable to forget what they learned on previous tasks—a phenomenon known as catastrophic forgetting. Proposed solutions to catastrophic forgetting tend to involve storing information about past tasks, meaning that memory usage is a chief consideration in determining their practicality. This paper develops a memory-efficient solution to catastrophic forgetting using the idea of matrix sketching, in the context of a simple continual learning algorithm known as orthogonal gradient descent (OGD). OGD finds weight updates that aim to preserve performance on prior datapoints, using gradients of the model on those datapoints. However, since the memory cost of storing prior model gradients grows with the runtime of the algorithm, OGD is ill-suited to continual learning over long time horizons. To address this problem, we propose SketchOGD. SketchOGD employs an online sketching algorithm to compress model gradients as they are encountered into a matrix of a fixed, user-determined size. In contrast to existing memory-efficient variants of OGD, SketchOGD runs online without the need for advance knowledge of the total number of tasks, is simple to implement, and is more amenable to analysis. We provide theoretical guarantees on the approximation error of the relevant sketches under a novel metric suited to the downstream task of OGD. Experimentally, we find that SketchOGD tends to outperform current state-of-the-art variants of OGD given a fixed memory budget.1

title: FaaSAdapter: An Adaptive Resource Configuration Framework for Serverless Workflows at the Edge
authors: Haoyu Luo, Ming Liu, Shaojian Qiu, Xiao Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: Serverless computing has emerged as a promising deployment paradigm for edge scenarios, owing to its efficient resource utilization and flexible provisioning enabled by Function-as-a-Service (FaaS). In Serverless environment, developers are required to configure resources for functions to balance cost efficiency and performance. However, determining appropriate resource allocations for the functions running at the edge is a challenge due to the dynamic nature of the environment. This challenge is further compounded when managing serverless workflows composed of multiple interconnected functions with complex dependencies. To address such an challenge, we present FaaSAdapter, an efficient runtime resource configuration framework for workflow functions, aiming at conserving computational resources at the edge while ensuring timely response to user requests. Different from existing dynamic resource configuration methods that incrementally determine resource schemes for only the immediate subsequent workflow function, FaaSAdapter predicts the execution times of all the unexecuted functions across various resource configurations and determines an optimal configuration schema for the function instances based on the current execution progress. Then, it updates the configuration schema as needed during runtime. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that FaaSAdapter ensures satisfactory response time of user requests with lowest resource consumption.

title: Terra-NTN-6G: A Multi-Layer Terrestrial–Aerial–Satellite Channel Dataset for AI-Driven 6G Networks
authors: Amandeep Kaur, Sujal Gupta
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Non-Terrestrial Networks (NTNs), including low-Earth-orbit (LEO) satellites and unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), are envisioned as an essential component of sixth-generation (6G) wireless systems to enable global connectivity, high mobility support, and resilient communication services. However, due to the limited availability of AI-channel datasets, jointly modelling terrestrial, aerial, and satellite links significantly limits data-driven research in NTN-enabled 6G networks. In this paper, we introduce Terra-NTN-6G, a comprehensive multi-layer channel dataset that captures terrestrial–aerial–satellite propagation characteristics under realistic mobility, Doppler, and blockage conditions. The dataset provides configurable uplink and downlink channel state information, path-level parameters, mobility trajectories, and service-level performance labels across sub-6 GHz and mm Wave bands. Terra-NTN-6G is designed to support a wide range of AI-driven applications, including beam tracking, handover prediction, resource orchestration, and NTN-aware RAN optimization. Experimental results demonstrate that the dataset effectively supports robust AI-driven beam and link selection under high mobility and heterogeneous propagation conditions.

title: Estimating Series Battery Pack Capacity From Charge–Discharge Behavior at Unilateral Boundaries
authors: Songtao Ye, Dou An, Kunlei Yu, Xiaoyu Shu, Chun Wang, Tao Zhang, Zhe Shan, Huan Xi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: Battery pack capacity estimation is crucial for improving the performance, safety, and reliability of energy storage systems. However, the inherent cell inconsistency and unobservable internal electrochemical reactions make this task challenging, especially when the battery behavior is only partially observed. To address this challenge, this article proposes a novel framework, UniCap, to estimate the capacity of series battery packs by utilizing partial behavior observed near the lower voltage boundary. Within this framework, the traditional estimation process is reformulated into two tractable subtasks. First, a hybrid K-nearest neighbors method is developed to nondestructively quantify unreleased capacity caused by cell inconsistency. Second, a cross-stream cell-wise transformer is proposed to predict the cell behavior and corresponding capacity at the unknown upper voltage boundary. Based on the above, the lower bound of the pack capacity can be inferred. Without requiring full charge or discharge cycles, the proposed framework provides an efficient solution for battery pack capacity estimation. Validated on a large-scale industrial dataset comprising 1757 battery packs, the framework achieves state-of-the-art performance with a mean absolute error of 0.0968 Ah. Notably, compared with existing industrial workflows, the proposed method significantly reduces test duration with only marginal accuracy degradation, thereby improving automation and production efficiency.

title: Decoupling Planning and Control for Resilient AGV Navigation: A Two-Tier Edge-MPC Approach
authors: Rômulo A. L. V. de Omena, Danilo F. S. Santos, Angelo Perkusich
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Industrial Electronics
abstract: The flexibility of Automated Guided Vehicle (AGV) fleets in Industry 4.0 environments is compromised by network degradation, which impacts navigational resilience. Building upon the conceptual framework introduced in previous research, this paper provides a substantive advancement through a two-tier, edge-based architecture that utilizes a predictive buffering mechanism. The core innovation lies in the systematic validation of this architecture via multi-scenario simulations—ranging from production lines to complex logistics centers—and the quantitative revelation of the intrinsic relationship between edge computational load and scene complexity. Furthermore, we provide empirical evidence of the effectiveness of a limited nearest-neighbor constraint strategy as a scalable optimization approach. Results demonstrate that the framework maintains operational persistence under network outages while enabling the use of low-cost, offering a high-fidelity blueprint for scalable and resilient smart manufacturing.

title: A Trajectory Generation Method for Image-Based Unmanned Mobile Vehicles Using Bézier Curves
authors: Sakuya Yamaguchi, Takumi Obayashi Tatsunosuke Shiota, Yukito Taguchi, Ryuki Tsuruda, Ryosuke Oshima, Shota Koga, Nozomi Tsutsui, Tan Jin Hung, Hiromu Matsuzaki, Masahiro Kiyota, Ryuto Matsunaga, Nagisa Yoshikawa, Kento Nakai, Masahiro Nishimura, Taito Manabe, Yuichiro Shibata
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: This paper discusses a trajectory generation method for image-based autonomous driving robots using SoC FPGAs. The UMV robot developed by the authors detects white lines on the road using a single camera and performs trajectory planning based on that information. In situations such as turning at intersections or avoiding obstacles, high-speed trajectory computation and stable navigation along an appropriate route are required. To achieve smooth trajectory generation, the system uses a Bézier curve algorithm to interpolate the feature points extracted from the images. The smoothness of the trajectory can be controlled by changing the number of computation points on the curve. In order to determine the optimal design that satisfies the real-time requirements of vehicle control under an embedded environment with limitations on performance and power consumption, we conducted a comparative evaluation from both execution time and accuracy perspectives. The results clarified that using 100 computation points is optimal for this robot.

title: Performance Enhancement of Cooperative NOMA in Satellite Communication through Combining Techniques
authors: K. Ashwini, Ashish Singh, V. K. Jagadeesh, Rachana Shenoy, Dushantha Nalin K. Jayakody
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This article studies the analysis of the key performance parameters in the proposed cooperative Non-Orthogonal Multiple Access (NOMA) system considering the satellite network. NOMA can be coupled with standard relaying procedures to increase the overall capabilities of the wireless communication system. The system under consideration consists of two users, one near and a far user, and a satellite. The paper explores by using a Decode and Forward (DF) relaying mechanism at the user closer to the satellite, which will forward the signal received from the satellite user that is distant from it. In separate case studies, the far user combines the direct and indirect transmission signals using Maximal Ratio Combining (MRC) and Selection Combining (SC) techniques. The expressions for outage, average bit error rate (BER), and capacity are derived for MRC and SC methods. It is also derived and approximated, and closed-form expressions are obtained. The simulation results of these analytical expressions reveal that the MRC method of combining outperforms the SC technique in terms of outage, BER, and ergodic capacity.

title: Learning-Based Airflow Templates for Real-Time Hand–Wind Interaction in Web-Based Cloth Simulation
authors: Jong-Hyun Kim
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper presents a learning-based hand–wind interaction framework that enables real-time airflow generation and cloth simulation in a web-based environment using only a standard webcam. Conventional gesture-driven wind interfaces typically map hand motion to a single force vector, which limits their ability to represent the spatial structure and temporal continuity of airflow induced by natural hand gestures. As a result, abrupt wind transitions and visually implausible cloth responses often occur, especially during gesture changes. To address these limitations, we propose a data-driven airflow representation based on learned airflow templates. Instead of directly solving physically accurate fluid dynamics, our approach focuses on visual plausibility by learning representative airflow patterns from paired hand-motion and material-response data. We construct a dedicated dataset by simultaneously recording hand gestures and airflow-induced paper motion using a dual-camera setup. The resulting airflow vector fields are clustered via unsupervised learning to extract a small set of representative airflow templates. At runtime, a temporal gesture classification model based on Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks analyzes recent hand-motion sequences and predicts a probability distribution over the airflow templates. The inferred airflow is obtained through probability-weighted template blending, which ensures smooth transitions between different wind patterns. This airflow field is then injected as an external force into a lightweight fluid solver to generate divergence-free velocity fields, which are subsequently coupled with a Position-Based Dynamics (PBD) cloth simulation. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework produces stable, continuous, and visually coherent hand–wind interactions in real time within a web browser. The system successfully translates natural hand gestures into spatial airflow patterns that interact intuitively with deformable cloth, without requiring specialized sensors or plugins. Our work highlights the potential of learning-based airflow modeling as a practical alternative to heuristic wind control for immersive, web-based interactive simulations.

title: Achieving Sustainable Connectivity in Agriculture through AI-Powered Beamforming
authors: Mohamed Naeem
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: Sustainable connectivity within agricultural systems ensures continuous monitoring and effective management. Disruptions in this connectivity can cause significant delays in field operations. Various factors threaten the stability of these connections, prompting many scholarly efforts to address these disruptions. However, a comprehensive solution for managing connectivity interruptions has yet to be developed. This paper proposes a novel hybrid dynamic algorithm that combines AI with classical MUSIC and least mean squares (LMS) for agricultural applications.
