The proposed solution utilizes the Feedforward Neural Network (FNN) to adjust the step size of the LMS adaptive beamforming algorithm, in conjunction with MUSIC direction-of-arrival (DoA) estimation. It considers realistic wireless propagation models and a heterogeneous wireless network infrastructure, along with optimized beamforming management. A comparative analysis reveals that this approach outperforms prior methods, resulting in a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 1 5 \%}$</tex> reduction in power consumption.

title: COMSOL–MATLAB Comparative Analysis of ISFETs
authors: Ball Mukund Mani Tripathi, Kodali Umesh Manikanta, Borugadda Jagan, Duriseti Chandrasekhar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper presents a detailed comparative analysis of planar and nanowire ion-sensitive field-effect transistors (ISFETs) using high-κ gate dielectrics, namely SiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>, Al<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>, and HfO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>. The study evaluates the influence of dielectric permittivity and device geometry on electrical performance parameters such as threshold voltage (V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</inf>), transconductance (g<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">m</inf>), subthreshold slope (SS), and gate capacitance (C<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">gate</inf>). Device simulations were carried out using COMSOL Multiphysics to model the electrostatic potential and charge distribution, followed by MATLAB-based extraction of electrical characteristics.The results shows a clear improvement in the nanowire ISFET setup when you compare it to the usual planar version. Things get interesting under the same biasing conditions and with an oxide thickness of just (t<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ox</inf> = 2 nm).The nanowire design brings down the threshold voltage by about 10 to 15 percent on average. It also boosts transconductance by close to 40 percent. Looking at the different dielectrics we checked out, HfO2 stood among all. It has delivered the top gate capacitance at 0.064 F/m2. It has the smallest subthreshold slope around 49.8 mV/dec. All this points to better control from the gate and way less leakage current. The pH sensitivity improved from 52 mV/pH for SiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> to 61 mV/pH for HfO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>, approaching the theoretical Nernst limit. These results demonstrate that combining a nanowire geometry with a high-κ dielectric provides stronger electrostatic coupling, improved charge modulation, and enhanced sensing performance. The proposed approach establishes a practical pathway for designing high-sensitivity, low-power ISFET sensors for biochemical and environmental monitoring applications.

title: Low Power Testing: A Study of Power Aware Test Insertion Techniques
authors: Atharva Yawalkar, Vrinda Gupta
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Research on low-power in semiconductor design has always been fascinating. The chip density has significantly increased as fabrication technology has developed and moved towards smaller nano meter (nm) technologies. Testing them presents an additional constraint for the design for test (DFT) design. DFT adds a 5-20% area overhead in comparison to the chip's core functional circuitry. In digital circuits, testability logic is just as significant as functional logic. Thus, optimizing DFT circuitry can help solve the issue of controlling power consumption in digital circuits. This paper discusses various advances in power-aware DFT insertion methods like unified power format (UPF), based designs, efficient scan mapping, and various tradeoffs between compression ratio, chain length, and test time affecting Power-Performance- Area (PPA) of the integrated circuit.

title: GTAssure: Ground Truth Quality Assurance Framework for Semantic Segmentation in Precision Agriculture
authors: Udaybhan Rathore, Amit Shakya, Rupesh Kumar, Shruti Phutke, Lalit Sharma
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: With the growing demand for precision agriculture to minimize food waste, optimize resource and crop management, semantic segmentation plays a critical role in enabling intelligent decision-making. However, achieving high segmentation accuracy on agricultural fields requires real, field-captured datasets. Manual annotation of these datasets is not only time-consuming and labor-intensive but also prone to errors such as missed regions, mislabels, and coarse boundaries, resulting in biased learning and unreliable model performance during training and testing. To address this, we propose GTAssure, an automated framework for ground truth quality inspection that detects and corrects annotation errors, thereby assuring dataset reliability for precision agriculture. The framework employs two correction approaches: the first addresses missed regions and misclassifications errors, while the second targets coarse annotations commonly found in complex agricultural scenes. GTAssure is evaluated across diverse agricultural tasks, including leaf disease, fruit, and crop–weed segmentation, using five challenging real-world datasets: NLB, LDSD, MinneApple, WGISD, and CWFID. The proposed framework significantly enhances models reliability and accuracy using expert-verified, refined ground truth, demonstrating consistent IoU improvements ranging from 0.68% to 5.13% across all experimental datasets, establishing a new benchmark for high-quality annotations in precision agriculture. Code will be made publicly available once the paper is accepted.

title: Rod-Pipe-Climbing Soft Robot With Transitioning Locomotion
authors: Jiaxi Wu, Mingxin Wu, Chen Wang, Guangming Xie
publish: IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics
abstract: Inspired by nature, researchers have created climbing robots with impressive performances. However, most existing soft climbing robots remain limited by their single functionality: they can either move along rods or within pipes, but typically cannot operate in both environments. Achieving smooth transitions between rods and pipes is even more challenging, restricting their deployment in practical scenarios. In this work, we propose a soft climbing robot using fabric-stacking pneumatic actuators, capable of climbing on rods, crawling inside pipes, and transitioning between rods and pipes. The robot’s body consists of two parallel fabric-stacking stretching actuators, which can stretch from an initial length of 8.8 mm to 166.4 mm, with a stretching ratio of 17.9. The robot’s foot, comprised of two zig-zag-shaped bending actuators, can grasp rods and support the inner walls of pipes by regulating air pressures. By coordinating the body with the front and hind feet, the robot can climb both vertical and curved rods and pipes. Furthermore, we demonstrate the robot’s ability to transition between two rods, two pipes, and between a pole and a tube—to the best of our knowledge, a capability not yet achieved by existing soft climbing robots.

title: Kernel Reboot: Breaking the Boundaries of Neural Tangent Kernels for Neural Fields_supp1-3692624.pdf
authors: Amir Moner Mallak
publish: N/A
abstract: Neural fields (NFs) map continuous coordinates to signals such as color or density, but fast high-quality reconstruction from sparse observations remains difficult. Classical Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) regression gives closed-form fits, yet it is fundamentally linear and cannot accumulate reusable task priors. We develop three algorithms that address these gaps. NTK-KIP learns a distilled support set of coordinates (and optional labels) so that a finite NTK can inpaint large missing regions from little observed data, yielding a compact non-linear representation instead of a raw kernel solve. MetaQuill meta-learns a shared initialization for an INR so that new scenes can be adapted by updating only a small task-specific weight offset, which provides true feature learning and a reusable prior. Finally, MetaQuill-KIP fuses both ideas: it seeds the task with a KIP-style non-linear warm start, then refines only that small offset around the meta-learned initialization. MetaQuill-KIP achieves high-PSNR reconstructions and semantically plausible inpainting under very sparse observations, while requiring only lightweight per-instance adaptation, whereas diffusion-style baselines typically depend on large pretrained generative priors and costly per-image tuning. This shows that NTK-driven neural fields can be made both non-linear and meta-learnable, narrowing the gap between analytic kernels and practical few-shot reconstruction.

title: Optimizing Leaky Private Information Retrieval Codes to Achieve O (log K ) Leakage Ratio Exponent
authors: Wenyuan Zhao, Yu-Shin Huang, Chao Tian, Alex Sprintson
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: We study the problem of leaky private information retrieval (L-PIR), where the amount of privacy leakage is measured by the pure differential privacy parameter, referred to as the leakage ratio exponent. Unlike the previous L-PIR proposed by Samy et al., which is merely a re-allocation of the clean (low-cost) retrieval pattern within the generalized TSC family, we show that the active pure-DP constraints couple adjacent Hamming-weight layers of the random key, which reduces the optimization to a layered problem whose optimum is geometric across these layers. As a result, only cyclic permutations are needed without loss of optimality, and lower-Hamming weight keys should be assigned higher probabilities. This new scheme provides a significant improvement, leading to an O(logK) leakage ratio exponent with fixed download cost D, in contrast to the previous art that only achieves a Θ(K) exponent, where K is the number of messages.

title: Inverse Design of Microring Resonator-Based Glucose Biosensor Using AI Diffusion Models
authors: Yasaman Torabi, Amirali Ekhteraei, Bardia Baraeinejad
publish: 2026 IEEE Silicon Photonics Conference (SiPhotonics)
abstract: Glucose detection is crucial for diabetes diagnostics. Microring resonator biosensors detect refractive index changes. We use artificial intelligence (AI) diffusion models for inverse design of the sensor geometries consistent with target spectral responses, offering potential for intelligent biosensor development.

title: A 6.78-MHz 15-cm-Air-Gap 2-kW Optimally Soft-Switched Modular Capacitive Wireless Power Transfer System for EV Charging
authors: Ayush Dixit, Asif Mushtaq Bhat, Sreyam Sinha
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This paper presents an optimally soft-switched modular capacitive wireless power transfer (CWPT) system for electric vehicle (EV) charging. Multi-MHz CWPT systems must operate with zero-voltage switching (ZVS) of their inverter and rectifier transistors to avoid excessive losses and thermal instability. Two different techniques to achieve ZVS, one with an additional series inductor and the other with an additional shunt inductor, are considered. For both these techniques, the required additional inductance and dead time between switching transitions are determined optimally so as to achieve ZVS of the inverter transistors with minimized reactive power in all the modules. The efficiency implications of both the approaches are formulated, and it is shown that while efficiency remains nearly constant with varying number of modules, the shunt inductor approach consistently provides a higher efficiency. A 6.78-MHz, 2-kW, 15-cm air-gap two-module CWPT prototype is designed using the shunt inductor approach, and the desired soft switching is shown to be achieved.

title: LLM-Powered SSH Honeypot with Behavioral Analysis and Real-Time ML-Based Attack Classification
authors: S. Metilda Florence, Abhinav U, Rashmika S
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: Hackers still use Secure Shell (SSH) services to get into systems a lot since they are common and may be located on public networks. A lot of how classic intrusion detection systems work is by looking at signatures or the network as a whole. But these strategies don't usually work after an interactive session is put up to figure out what the attacker wants to do. This paper describes a behavior-driven strategy for looking at SSH assaults using honeypot log data, with an emphasis on extracting features at the session level and showing them in real time. We gather logs of interactions from a Cowrie SSH honeypot and turn them into sessions for hackers. After that, we turn them into high-dimensional behavioral feature vectors that alter based on time, language, and purpose. A real-time dashboard has these capabilities and can tell the difference between attacks that are done by bots and attacks that are done by people. The suggested approach puts a lot of attention on being modular, easy to comprehend, and easy to see how it operates. The article also discusses a proposed machine learning enhancement that would utilize labeled attack datasets to autonomously categorize attacker behavior based on identified characteristics.

title: High-Quality Synthetic Annotated Tissue Data Using Conditional Generative Adversarial Networks
authors: Siddharth Srivastava, Cornelis Jan Weijer, Till Bretschneider
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: We present a deep-learning based pipeline for generating high-quality synthetic instance-segmentation datasets of tissues undergoing early gastrulation in chick embryo. We create point-clouds using the Lennard-Jones potential and learn an image-to-image translation from these point-clouds to create synthetic tissue images and auxiliary flow tensors using a Generative Adversarial Network, from which the segmentation masks are derived. We evaluate the downstream utility of our synthetic data by training Cellpose and Stardist models from scratch under data replacement and data augmentation scenarios, show that the synthetic datasets effectively capture the statistical properties of the real dataset, and show that our synthetic data improves segmentation performance on a held-out test set. This approach substantially reduces expert annotation time, as late-stage gastrulation data are challenging to acquire and manually label, while similar synthetic examples can be flexibly generated from easily obtainable and annotated early-stage data. Code: https://gitlab.com/siddharthsrivastava/synthetic-tissue-data

title: IoT Based Water Monitoring and Automatic Tank Filling System
authors: Sweta Bokade, Sumit Jaisinghani, Anish Mankani, Hitesh Motwani, Devansh Motwani, Juhi Sawlani
publish: 2026 International Conference on Multidisciplinary Innovations For Smart &amp; Sustainable Future (MISSF)
abstract: A vital natural resource, water's availability and quality are critical to industry, agriculture, and human health. However, in many areas, there are common issues due to water contamination and wash and wastage and manual tank management. To solve these problems, the project presents a system of monitoring an IOT-based water monitoring and automatic tanks that add smart sensing, automation and wireless communication to improve the efficiency of water use and ensure water safety. The system appoints various sensors for monitoring major water quality indicators such as <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{p H}$</tex>, TDS, turbidity, and temperature, provides a comprehensive view of water purity in real time. In addition to quality monitoring, a water level sensor is used to track the water level of the tank. When the level falls below a set threshold and water is considered safe depending on the sensor reading, the system automatically activates a solenoid valve to recreate the tank. Once reaching the desired level, the valve is closed to prevent overflow and water dissipation. A microcontroller, such as ESP32, collects and processes the sensor data, which is then wirelessly transmitted to a firebase or blynk like a cloud platform. Users can access real -time data, historical logs and alerts through mobile or desktop applications, which can enable remote monitoring and control. This system enhances the facility, promotes water conservation, and improves health protection only by storing and using clean water to ensure that. Overall, the project provides a smart, low cost and scalable solution for water management in houses, schools, hospitals and industrial facilities. Automation and IOT integration minimizes human labor, conserves water, and promotes sustainable water management techniques.

title: DERMAE: Improving Skin Lesion Classification Through Conditioned Latent Diffusion and MAE Distillation
authors: Francisco Filho, Kelvin Cunha, Fábio Papais, Emanoel dos Santos, Rodrigo Mota, Thales Bezerra, Erico Medeiros, Paulo Borba, Tsang Ing Ren
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Skin lesion classification datasets often suffer from severe class imbalance, with malignant cases significantly underrepresented, leading to biased decision boundaries during deep learning training. We address this challenge using classconditioned diffusion models to generate synthetic dermatological images, followed by self-supervised MAE pretraining to enable huge ViT models to learn robust, domain-relevant features. To support deployment in practical clinical settings, where lightweight models are required, we apply knowledge distillation to transfer these representations to a smaller ViT student suitable for mobile devices. Our results show that MAE pretraining on synthetic data, combined with distillation, improves classification performance while enabling efficient on-device inference for practical clinical use.

title: Advanced Multi-Layer Framework for Distinguishing Human-Written and AI-Generated Arabic Text: A Transformer-Based Approach with Linguistic Feature Engineering
authors: Yazeed Alsarhan, Farah Abo Asba, Ayoub Alsarhan, Suhaila Abuowaida
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Engineering Systems (ICETES)
abstract: The emergence of a number of large Arabic language models, such as AraGPT2, Jais, CAMeLBERT, and several other versions of Arabic-optimized GPTs, has revolutionized the way in which machine-generated text is created in Arabic and has achieved new benchmarks in terms of fluency, coherence, and semantic richness. However, this represents a significant challenge for the verification of academic integrity, journalistic authenticity, and cybersecurity applications because the differences between machine-generated Arabic text and human-authored Arabic text are becoming increasingly difficult to discern. This study proposes an integrated multi-layer detection system using a combination of stylistic, statistical, morpho-syntactic and transformer-based methods to reliably identify whether Arabic text was written by humans or machines. Using a carefully compiled dataset of 26,998 samples consisting of authentic human-written texts from a variety of sources, combined with the output of state-of-theart Arabic language models, our research team identified more than <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3 5}$</tex> linguistic features, including various lexical diversity metrics, entropy measures, perplexity estimates and parts-of-speech (POS) distributions. The results of our experiments demonstrate that fine-tuned transformer architectures, specifically AraBERT, were able to identify and distinguish between human and AIgenerated Arabic texts at an average rate of 94.8% and therefore significantly outperformed traditional machine learning baseline algorithms by approximately 10%.

title: Residual-Aware Reinforcement Learning with Bayesian Transformer for Probabilistic Anomaly Detection in PV Systems
authors: Apoorva Choumal, M. Rizwan, Shatakshi Jha
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Smart Grid, and Renewable Energy (PESGRE)
abstract: Reliable detection of operational anomalies in large-scale photovoltaic plants is constrained by unavailability of labeled data data, non-stationary ramp-rate dynamics and the need for well-calibrated uncertainty estimates. This research proposes an end-to-end framework that couples residual-driven anomaly exploration with uncertainty-aware prediction to deliver probabilistic anomaly score. A ten-year, 5-min resolution dataset from 1058 kW Dessert garden, Australia solar farm is used to access the proposed framework. A deterministic gradient-boosting model is trained to estimate the photovoltaic power output using meteorological parameters. The normalized prediction residual is employed as an unsupervised anomaly indicator. Further, the anomaly identification is formulated as a sequential decision-making problem which is addressed using reinforcement learning agent. At each step the agent selects the time window and sensor subset expected to maximize residual deviation, thereby concentrating computational effort on high-risk segments. The selected sequences are processed by a Transformer encode whose decoder is trained as a Bayesian neural network via Monte-Carlo dropout. The model outputs both point prediction and epistemic variance, enabling closed-form computation of anomaly probability from prediction errors. The proposed framework attains a CRPS of 0.041 with 70% faster inference and apporx. 33% lower false alarm rate hence providing an accurate and operational viable methodology for real-time PV anomaly detection.

title: Hybrid CNN-SVM Framework for Automated Depression Detection Using Speech Analysis
authors: Vishnu Nair, Khushal Rathi, Aakash Sarkar, Vikram Kulkarni, Raminder Kaur Nagra
publish: 2026 International Conference on Multidisciplinary Innovations For Smart &amp; Sustainable Future (MISSF)
abstract: This paper presents a hybrid CNN-SVM model that is used to detect depression based on speech using the DAIC-WOZ dataset (Opensource). All audio files were processed using Librosa, SciPy and Parselmouth to remove noise and normalize audio and extract acoustic features (including some MFCCs, spectral, prosodic and energy-based acoustic features). After data preprocessing, the CNN was trained to learn depth spectral representations and SVM was trained to classify the embeddings to improve the performance of generalization. The model achieved a rate of accuracy of 69.09%, which is a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$6 \%-8 \%$</tex> better result than the current methods. In general, the analysis finds a value in deep learning with machine learning in the creation of noninvasive, reliable, and scalable methods of detecting depression early.

title: TAPE: A Two-Stage Parameter-Efficient Adaptation Framework for Foundation Models in OCT-OCTa Analysis
authors: Xiaofei Su, Zengshuo Wang, Minghe Sun, Xin Zhao, Mingzhu Sun
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Automated analysis of optical coherence tomography (OCT) and OCT angiography (OCTA) images is critical for robust ophthalmic diagnosis. Existing mainstream methods trained from scratch rely heavily on massive data and model scale, thereby hindering their practical deployment in resourceconstrained clinical settings. Although transfer learning based on foundation models (FMs) is promising, it still faces significant challenges: domain shift and task misalignment. To address these, we propose TAPE: A Two-stage Adaptation Framework via Parameter-E fficient Fine-tuning, which strategically decouples adaptation into domain alignment and task fitting for downstream segmentation. The domain adaptation stage notably applies parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) in the context of masked image modeling for medical image domain adaptation, a novel approach to the best of our knowledge. Applying TAPE to retinal layer segmentation on both universal (masked auto-encoder, MAE) and specialized (RETFound) FMs, it demonstrates superior parameter efficiency and achieves state-of-the-art generalization performance across diverse pathologies.

title: Electrically Injected 1.55 μm InAs/InP QD Lasers on On-Axis (001) Si Using a Ge-Based Buffer
authors: Zhao Yan, Shangfeng Liu, Ka Ming Wong, Haotian Zeng, Yangqian Wang, Jae-Seong Park, Hui Jia, Zhongming Cao, Curtis Hentschel, Mickael Martin, Mingchu Tang, Samuel Shutts, Thierry Baron, Huiyun Liu, Peter M. Smowton, Qiang Li
publish: 2026 IEEE Silicon Photonics Conference (SiPhotonics)
abstract: We report <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.5 \mu \mathrm{m}$</tex>-band InAs/InP quantumdot lasers grown on CMOS-compatible on-axis (001) Si using a Ge buffer and InAsP/InP dislocation filters. Deepetched ridge waveguide lasers show threshold current of 301 mA, an optical output power <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$>10 \text{mW}$</tex> per-facet, and operation up to 80° C.

title: Design of a 6.5 kV SiC MOSFET Full-bridge Module
authors: Max Rogers, Arthur Boutry, Andrew N. Lemmon, Nicholas Turner, Nick Baker
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This article details the design of a 6.5 kV Silicon Carbide (SiC) MOSFET module in the full-bridge topology. The increased industrial use of SiC modules in industrial and transportation applications is driving intensified interest in module topologies beyond the conventional half-bridge. Advanced module topologies unlock greater levels of physical integration and power density for power electronic systems such as DC-to-DC converters and electric motor drives. The prototype module described in this article adopts an industry-standard footprint and represents a significant advancement of a previous prototype design performed by the authors. This article discusses the design of the prototype module, along with fabrication details, simulation results, and preliminary experimental results.

title: Generative AI for Overcoming Rf Data Inaccessibility in Quantitative Ultrasound Imaging
authors: Nasrin Sheibani-Asl, Gregory Czarnota, Ali Sadeghi-Naini
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Quantitative ultrasound (QUS) imaging enables noninvasive tissue characterization using frequency-dependent parameters derived from raw radiofrequency (RF) data. However, the limited accessibility to RF data, particularly in commercial scanners, restricts its clinical use. This study adapts a conditional generative adversarial network (cGAN) to synthesize RF data from conventional B-mode images, addressing this limitation. A large paired dataset of B-mode and RF signals acquired from 152 patients with benign and malignant breast lesions was used for training and evaluation. Quantitative pixel-level metrics were employed to assess reconstruction fidelity, while histograms of QUS parametric map mean-values as well as the attenuation coefficient estimates derived from the original and synthetic RF data were analyzed for distributional agreement. The synthesized RF data showed close similarity to the originals (SSIM <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.81 \pm 0.06$</tex> for RF and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.87 \pm 0.04$</tex> for B-mode). These findings demonstrate the feasibility of translating B-mode images into RF-equivalent representations for QUS analysis, which support wider clinical adoption of QUS imaging.

title: Segmentation and Diagnosis of Fetal Heart Ultrasound Based on Anatomical Structure Priors and Dual-Threshold Guided Semi-Supervised Learning
authors: Bo Zhai, Changqin Yan, Zhiyuan Zhao, Fangjin Deng, Feng Wei, Chenzi Wang, Kundong Mo
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Aiming at the problems of scarce labeled data, complex anatomical structures and insufficient boundary accuracy in intelligent diagnosis of fetal congenital heart disease, this paper proposes a fetal heart ultrasound segmentation and diagnosis algorithm fusing anatomical structure prior and semi-supervised learning. Its innovations include three aspects: designing an Anatomical Structure Query-based Segmentation Decoder to embed medical prior into global feature learning; proposing a View-Aware Connected Component Loss to dynamically constrain the connected domain morphology of anatomical structures and suppress false positives and structural fragmentation; putting forward a Dual-Threshold Guided Semi-Supervised Classification Learning strategy to maximize the value of unlabeled data and alleviate class imbalance and pseudo-label noise. Ablation studies on the FETUS 2026 dataset reveal that our modular framework achieves state-of-the-art performance by specializing components: the Anatomical Structure Query Decoder drives segmentation accuracy with a DSC of 74.43 and an NSD of 49.16, whereas the View-Aware Connected Component Loss optimizes the comprehensive diagnostic metric with an overall Score of 48.17, collectively demonstrating superior generalization and robustness compared to the baseline.

title: AMLSBF: Adaptive Multi-Layer Semantic–Behavior Fusion Framework for Intelligent Cloud Security
authors: Mande Sandeep, Shaik Kareemulla
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: The growing complexity of cloud infrastructures has brought about major problems in the management and security of environments that are highly dynamic and very large-scale. The conventional defense models based on rules and regulations are not effective in solving the emerging environments of cyber threats that breed on contextual reliance and behavioural-patterns. In this paper, I am going to introduce a framework of the adaptive multi-layer semantic behavior fusion which provides stronger intelligent cloud security by integrating semantic reasoning and behavior analytics seamlessly. The given framework builds on the contextual semantics -based on the ontological structures, data interrelationships, and the intent of the user and utilizes real-time behavioral models of user and system interactions. These layers together can be used to identify anomalies in advance, predict possible threats, and automatically optimize its defense mechanisms with the help of constant learning. The paper offers an extensive literature review of the published research on semantic-based threat intelligence, behavioral detection models, and adaptable fusion practices. Moreover, it constructs a taxonomy which classifies existing models based on their design structures, computing performance, and scalability to various cloud eco systems. The current challenges covered in the survey are also lack of interoperability between heterogeneous systems, explainability in AI-based decision-making, and trade-off between adaptability and computational performance. The work forms a solid foundation on how to merge the aspect of semantic depth, and behavioral flexibility in the development of the future-generation, intelligent and self-evolving cloud security structures that can support complex and dynamic threat environments.

title: Performance Analysis of LSTM-Based Radar Scanning Pattern Recognition Under Quantum-Noise-Limited Sensing Conditions
authors: Sourav Kumar Mitra, Md. Waquib Bin Awal, Md Hossam-E-Haider
publish: 2026 22nd IEEE International Colloquium on Signal Processing &amp; Its Applications (CSPA)
abstract: Radar antenna scanning pattern recognition is an important task in electronic intelligence and electronic warfare, as it helps identify the scanning behavior of unknown radar emitters for threat evaluation. Recent studies have shown that long short-term memory (LSTM) networks can effectively recognize radar scanning patterns using pulse amplitude sequences under normal measurement conditions [6]. However, the performance of such methods under extremely noisy sensing environments has not been clearly studied. This work analyzes the limitations of amplitude-based LSTM recognition under quantum-noiselimited conditions. A realistic simulation framework is developed that models photon shot noise using Poisson statistics and includes practical effects such as amplitude-frequency response distortion, pulse amplitude measurement errors, missing pulses, and outliers. Mechanical scanning, one-dimensional phased array scanning, and two-dimensional phased array scanning patterns are represented as time-series amplitude sequences. Robust least absolute deviation (LAD) interpolation within coherent processing intervals is applied before classification using a lightweight LSTM network. Simulation results under different noise levels and measurement error conditions show that, in quantum-noiselimited scenarios, the recognition accuracy of amplitude-only LSTM models quickly saturates near random-guess performance. This demonstrates that the accuracy loss is caused by fundamental sensing noise rather than model design or training issues. The results highlight the need for additional signal information beyond amplitude to achieve reliable radar scanning pattern recognition in extreme noise conditions.

title: Integration of Cardiac Histopathological and Imaging Data to Improve Understanding of Stereotactic Arrhythmia Radioablation
authors: Juan A. Cisneros Jacome, Louis Rigal, Raphaël Martins, Solène-Florence Kammerer-Jacquet, Annegret E. Werner, Melvyn Dezecot, Loig Duverge, Renaud De Crevoisier, Julien Bellec, Antoine Simon
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Ventricular tachycardia (VT) refractory to conventional therapies can be treated with stereotactic arrhythmia radioablation (STAR), yet its biological mechanisms remain unclear. To locally analyze the myocardial response to irradiation, we propose an full workflow integrating in-vivo treatment planning, ex-vivo imaging, and histopathology from an explanted human heart post-STAR. The method establishes a landmarkbased registration framework aligning ex-vivo and in-vivo data within a common coordinate system using anatomical landmarks (base, apex, and a rotation point). Twenty-one anatomical landmarks enabled consistent registration of CT, radiation dose maps and histopathology descriptors. By quantifying the fibrosis fraction in each histological sample, the framework expanded the dataset from 21 to 210 localized measurements. Correlation analysis revealed heterogeneous dose-effect relationships, with higher fibrosis density in regions exposed to 27-31 Gy and minimal changes in apical areas (< 2 Gy). This framework demonstrates the feasibility of direct dose-histopathology correlation in human cardiac tissue and provides a reproducible approach bridging radiotherapy, cardiology, and pathology for STAR evaluation.

title: Multi-Objective Optimization of Multi-Level DC-AC Converter for Single-Phase PV Microinverters
authors: Abhishek Awasthi, Majid Pahlevani, Praveen Jain
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: A flying capacitor (FC)-based multilevel structure is proposed as a viable solution for voltage-source inverter (VSI) implementation in a two-stage, single-phase, grid-connected photovoltaic (PV) microinverter application. The compact size and high-density design requirements for micro-inverter applications necessitate the optimal design of passive components, specifically the flying capacitors (FCs). Existing studies lack a systematic methodology for designing FCs of individual voltage levels and are based on a single objective of satisfying energy storage requirements, leading to suboptimal designs. The lack of generalized analytical expressions for FC-multilevel inverter in the grid-connected mode further inhibits study of critical performance characteristics, impeding optimal design. This study presents a practical multi-objective design optimization framework for FC-based VSI structure. The results show that the proposed capacitor-aware optimization procedure reduces the resulting DC-link capacitor design volume by 7% compared to a pure film-capacitor DC-link for a 2-level VSI with 10% lower cost. Additionally, a 17% volume reduction compared to the heuristic ratiometric FC design strategy with no efficiency degradation is achieved with the proposed optimization method.

title: Design of Hybrid Micromixer for Biomedical Applications
authors: Halah Basim Al-Mudhafar, Qais Al-Gayem
publish: 2026 22nd IEEE International Colloquium on Signal Processing &amp; Its Applications (CSPA)
abstract: Microfluidic mixing is one of the core unit operations for miniaturized analysis devices. Achieving high mixing of conventional microfluidic micromixers are inherently difficult to obtain as the fluid flow is typically in the laminar flow range where molecular diffusion predominates. In this work, a numerical investigation of a microfluidic micromixer with a hybrid actuation scheme and micropillar shape and gap variation is presented. COMSOL Multiphysics v.6.1. is used for the purpose laminar physics to compute fluid flow governing equations, and diluted transport species to compute mixing governing equations. The simulations were carried out at different Reynolds numbers like 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 40, 80, and 100 with an oscillation frequency of 10 MHz. Three micromixer designs are investigated in this study: T-mixer, a square micromixer, a hexagonal passive micromixer. With a mixing index of almost $86.7 \%$, the passive hexagonal design demonstrated the highest efficiency of all passive buildings. The hexagonal micromixer was then equipped with acoustic actuation to improve the mixing process even more. Under the same operating conditions, the active arrangement showed a notable improvement, achieving a mixing index of $99.73 \%$. The findings show that a highly effective method for microfluidic mixing in biomedical applications can be achieved by combining optimal geometry and acoustic excitation.

title: Normal-Mode Helical Antenna with Tap-Fed Impedance Step-Up for Near-Metal UHF RFID Systems
authors: Nur Syazani Bin Norman, Badrul Amin Bin Azahari, Nazihah binti Ahmad, Norsiha Zainuddin, Noorlindawaty Md Jizat, Yoshihide Yamada
publish: 2026 22nd IEEE International Colloquium on Signal Processing &amp; Its Applications (CSPA)
abstract: Normal-mode helical antennas (NMHAs) are unique for UHF RFID applications due to their compact size and the distinctive advantage in its simultaneous electric and magnetic current radiation characteristics. In comparison with the conventional dipole antennas, whose performance degrades severely in the presence of metal due to electric-field suppression, NMHAs have a dominant magnetic near field that is less sensitive to conductive surfaces. However, electrically short NMHAs exhibit very low input resistance, which made practical impedance is challenging, particularly for the RFID applications which require conjugate chip matching. This paper presents a compact, center tap-fed NMHA designed to achieve a controlled impedance transformation using a direct tap-feed structure by only controlling the tap dimensions. Unlike lumped matching networks or geometric modification of the helical radiator, the tap-feed approach enables systematic adjustment of the antenna input impedance while preserving the intrinsic electromagnetic behavior of the NMHA. By appropriately selecting the tap position along the helical element, the antenna input impedance can be matched to a $50 \Omega$ reference, allowing stable characterization and evaluation of near-metal performance. Full-wave simulation results demonstrate stable impedance behaviour and maintain magnetic near-field characteristics when the NMHA is placed near metal surfaces. The proposed tap-fed NMHA establishes a robust and scalable baseline for subsequent RFID near-metal studies, where the tap matching impedance can be adjusted to match with the RFID chip impedance in the future work.

title: A Hybrid Image Encryption Scheme with Wavelet Transform and Chaotic Permutation For Enhanced Security
authors: Ahwar Khan, Malik Fuad Wali Khan
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Emerging Computational Intelligence (ICECI)
abstract: With the emergence of quantum computing, conventional cryptographic mechanisms are under serious threat. Therefore, there is a need to design more secure alternatives. We propose a hybrid image encryption scheme that is resistant to quantum attacks. Our design combines the Kyber512 key-encapsulation mechanism for securely exchanging keys, and Ascon-128 authenticated encryption for efficient symmetric encryption. For stronger security, we used the discrete wavelet transform (DWT) to decompose the image into frequency subbands and a 4D coupled logistic map to shuffle the approximation sub-band, achieving strong, quantitatively validated diffusion and confusion properties. We analyzed the scheme using extensive security evaluations, including histogram analysis, pixel correlation, information entropy, resistance against differential attacks (NPCR and UACI), structural similarity index (SSIM), and key sensitivity tests. Experimental results show that our approach successfully randomizes pixel values, decorrelates, attains near-ideal entropy ($\approx 7.999$), demonstrates high robustness against differential (NPCR $\approx 99.6 \%$ and UACI $\approx 33.5$, statistical and brute-force attacks, maintains key sensitivity, and provides strong SSIM recovery upon decryption ($\gt0.997$). Compared with recent methods, the proposed scheme achieves higher or competitive entropy and lower pixel correlation. Formal analysis confirms security under established cryptographic assumptions, and the overall complexity is linear (O(MN))) in terms of image size. The proposed design offers a practical and scalable solution for postquantum secure image transmission in sensitive domains, such as healthcare, defense, and surveillance.

title: Resilient Neural Control for Grid-Forming Microgrids: An Integrated AI-Based Approach for Virtual-Impedance Scheduling and Cyber-Attack Mitigation
authors: S. M. Talha Zaidi, Uzair Asif, Arslan Munir, Mohammad B. Shadmand, Sudip K Mazumder
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Microgrids are playing an increasingly important role in the shift toward decentralized energy systems, yet they continue to face stability and reliability challenges under fast-changing operating conditions and rising cyber threats. In this work, we develop an integrated neural control framework for grid-forming inverters (GFMIs) that enables rapid, inverter-level detection and mitigation of cyber-attacks. Each GFMI is equipped with a lightweight bidirectional long short-term memory (Bi-LSTM) network that learns the typical time-based patterns of its local power and voltage signals, enabling accurate prediction of expected virtual impedance (Z<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">v</inf>) values. A centralized residual network (ResNet) is included to generate Z<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">v</inf> references under nominal conditions, providing coordinated power-sharing support. The locally predicted Z<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">v</inf> values are then continuously monitored against the incoming Z<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">v</inf> reference commands. If the difference exceeds the threshold, the primary controller immediately switches to its locally predicted value to maintain system stability and mitigate the impact of the attack. Testing on a modified IEEE 9-bus microgrid shows that the proposed method maintains critical parameters within operational bounds and delivers fast recovery from fixed, random varying, and gradually increasing Z<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">v</inf>-targeted attacks, while supporting decentralized decision making to enhance cyber-resilience and scalability for future smart grids.

title: Investigating Label Bias and Representational Sources of Age-Related Disparities in Medical Segmentation
authors: Aditya Parikh, Sneha Das, Aasa Feragen
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Algorithmic bias in medical imaging can perpetuate health disparities, yet its causes remain poorly understood in segmentation tasks. While fairness has been extensively studied in classification, segmentation remains underexplored despite its clinical importance. In breast cancer segmentation, models exhibit significant performance disparities against younger patients, commonly attributed to physiological differences in breast density. We audit the MAMA-MIA dataset, establishing a quantitative baseline of age-related bias in its automated labels, and reveal a critical Biased Ruler effect where systematically flawed labels for validation misrepresent a model's actual bias. However, whether this bias originates from lower-quality annotations (label bias) or from fundamentally more challenging image characteristics remains unclear. Through controlled experiments, we systematically refute hypotheses that the bias stems from label quality sensitivity or quantitative case difficulty imbalance. Balancing training data by difficulty fails to mitigate the disparity, revealing that younger patient cases are intrinsically harder to learn. We provide direct evidence that systemic bias is learned and amplified when training on biased, machine-generated labels, a critical finding for automated annotation pipelines. This work introduces a systematic framework for diagnosing algorithmic bias in medical segmentation and demonstrates that achieving fairness requires addressing qualitative distributional differences rather than merely balancing case counts.<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup><sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>Code for the experimental framework and evaluation is made available at https://github.com/ADE-17/bias-audit-isbi2026

title: Accelerated Reconstruction for Sparse-View CT with Angular Sinogram Blurring Arising in Fast Continuous Rotation CT Scanners Using Dykstra-Like Splitting
authors: Donghui Li, Hiroyuki Kudo, Wataru Yashiro
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Continuous-exposure X-ray imaging accelerates data acquisition by avoiding rapid source or detector switching, but both continuous exposure and fast rotation introduce angular-integration effects due to exposure characteristics and detector response limits. These effects cause severe blurring in reconstructed images or videos, and similar artifacts also arise in modern CT systems when scan speed increases for dynamic or real-time imaging. Conventional least-squares-based reconstruction methods struggle to handle sparse-view data and angular blurring simultaneously. To address this, we propose a reconstruction model within a Dykstra-like splitting framework, incorporating total variation (TV) regularization to suppress blur from continuous exposure, rapid rotation, and sparse-view conditions while accelerating convergence. By embedding a blur-compensation mechanism into the system matrix, the method effectively reduces angular-integration artifacts and preserves resolution and contrast at high scan speeds. It shows strong potential for high-speed, irreversible imaging in physics, chemistry, and materials science.

title: Digital Twin Approach for the Stator Winding Interturn fault Detection of Alternator
authors: Makarand Sudhakar Ballal, Bhimrao Sitaram Umare, Hiralal Murlidhar Suryawanshi, Rashmi Singh, Tushar G Vilhekar, Kanchan P. Ghule
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Smart Grid, and Renewable Energy (PESGRE)
abstract: This paper concentrates on the approach of digital twins in alternator stator winding Interturn fault (SWIF) diagnosis. Initially, a mathematical relationship was established linking alternator dynamics to stator winding interturn faults (SWIF). A real-time 10 kW, 400 V, 50 Hz alternator model was developed in MATLAB Simulink, with a corresponding Digital Twin in ANSYS Maxwell finite element tool is considered for analysis. The models were dynamically coupled via co-simulation. SWIF detection was achieved through negative sequence current analysis, with ANSYS simulations confirming change in magnetic flux distribution proportional to fault severity. This integrated Digital Twin approach demonstrated accurate, real-time fault detection and severity assessment, validating its effectiveness for predictive maintenance and operational reliability enhancement in alternator systems under varying conditions.

title: A Multimodal Cross-Attention Pathotranscriptome Integration for Enhanced Survival Prediction of Oral Squamous Cell Carcinoma
authors: Kountay Dwivedi, Amirreza Mahbod, Rupert C. Ecker, Klara Janjić
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC) accounts for a major part of cancer mortality, with survival outcomes highly dependent on early diagnosis. While many approaches have been proposed for OSCC survival prediction, they often rely on unimodal data, which may be suboptimal. In this study, we introduced a unified cross-attention-based deep learning framework that integrates whole-slide histopathology images (WSIs) and transcriptomic data from OSCC patients for survival prediction. The framework employed an autoencoder for transcriptomic feature extraction and a state-of-the-art pathology foundation model-evaluated across five alternatives-to derive WSI embeddings. These embeddings were subsequently integrated using cross-attention and concatenation within a Cox proportional hazards model. The multimodal approach outperformed nearly all unimodal counterparts, achieving a maximum concordance index of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.780 \pm 0.059$</tex> with cross-attention and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.766 \pm 0.050$</tex> with concatenation. The results indicate that pathotranscriptomic integration could improve survival prediction for OSCC patients. The implementation is available on GitHub at: https://github.com/kountaydwivedi/multimodal_fusion.git.

title: Extended Zero Turn-Off Loss (ZTL) Region for SiC MOSFETs by Gate Voltage Boosting
authors: Morten Rahr Nielsen, Luke Shillaber, Ameer Janabi, Stig Munk-Nielsen, Teng Long
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Zero turn-off loss (ZTL) or near-ZTL has gained increasing interest in zero voltage switching (ZVS) topologies to minimize switching losses, as both turn-on and turn-off switching losses approach zero. ZTL can be obtained at low switching currents under ZTL switching conditions as the switching current solely charges the SiC MOSFET output capacitance, C<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">oss</inf>, and does not generate any joule heating in the SiC MOSFET during the turn-off switching event. Extending the ZTL switching conditions to higher switching currents can be achieved by gate voltage boosting during the turn-off switching event to obtain faster switching speeds by increasing the SiC MOSFET gate current. This paper validates, through LTspice simulations and preliminary experimental results, an extended ZTL region for SiC MOSFETs with the use of gate voltage boosting. The preliminary experimental results obtained through double pulse testing show a reduction of 10-15% in the turn-off energy under non-ideal boosting voltage.

title: Machine Learning–Based Surrogate Models for Knowledge Discovery in Multi-Stage Engineering Simulations
authors: Nischal Kumar. S, S. Elangovan, Subashini. N.J
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: Analytical and empirical models are commonly employed to describe multi-stage engineering processes involving thermal, kinetic, and contact phenomena. In earlier studies, such processes were analyzed exclusively using physics-based formulations, where repeated analytical or numerical evaluations were required for parametric analysis and design exploration. No machine learning models were previously used to approximate or accelerate the simulator outputs. The present work introduces machine learning-based surrogate models developed as a continuation of a validated multi-stage engineering simulator. The underlying mathematical formulations and computational structure of the simulator are retained without modification and are treated as a deterministic knowledge source. Synthetic datasets are generated by systematically varying physically meaningful input parameters across three sequential stages: transient thermal response, diffusion-controlled coating thickness growth, and empirical contact time prediction. Stage-wise regression models are independently trained for each output variable. Multiple learning algorithms are evaluated, among which Random Forest regression consistently demonstrates superior performance across all stages. For the transient thermal stage, coefficients of determination of 0.9979 and 0.9984 are achieved for the series and lumped temperature predictions, respectively. The coating thickness surrogate attains an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex> value of 0.9836, while the contact time prediction achieves an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex> value of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 9 7 9 4}$</tex>, with low associated prediction errors. The results confirm that machine learning surrogates can accurately emulate physics-based simulator behavior while significantly reducing computational effort. The proposed framework enables rapid knowledge discovery and efficient analysis of multi-stage engineering systems, supporting effective integration of mechanical modelling with data-driven intelligence.

title: Design and Implementation of a KNX-Based Smart Home Training and Educational System
authors: Aya Alsharif, Mohammad Kouali, Jenan Abu Dayyah, Ahmed Abdou, Mohanad Rezeq, Mohammad Omar Sabri
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Engineering Systems (ICETES)
abstract: KNX is one of the most commonly used and accepted home/building automation standards. However, it is not widely used in practical engineering and vocational training. This paper introduces the design, implementation, and assessment of a KNX-based smart home training and educational system that aims to bridge the gap between academic education and professional commissioning practices. The platform proposed unites KNX-certified hardware, professional configuration of ETS, structured addressing, and group communication plans, modern humanmachine interfaces, such as touch panels and voice control via a 1 Home server and Amazon Alexa. An apartment-scale prototype of the module was created to simulate real-life automation (residential) conditions, such as lighting, dimming, shading, socket control, and sensor-based automation. The system was implemented in a one-week training program and tested using a quasi-experimental pre-post design, with 18 participants. The experiments' outcomes indicate significant improvements in theoretical knowledge and real-world KNX commissioning skills. The suggested system offers a training platform that is reproducible, curriculum-ready, aligned with industry standards, and applicable to engineering education.

title: Progidiff: Prompt-Guided Diffusion-Based Medical Image Segmentation
authors: Yuan Lin, Murong Xu, Marc Hölle, Chinmay Prabhakar, Andreas Maier, Vasileios Belagiannis, Bjoern Menze, Suprosanna Shit
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Widely adopted medical image segmentation methods, although efficient, are primarily deterministic and remain poorly amenable to natural language prompts. Thus, they lack the capability to estimate multiple proposals, human interaction, and cross-modality adaption. Recently, text-toimage diffusion models have shown potential to bridge the gap. However, training them from scratch requires a large dataset-a limitation for medical image segmentation. Furthermore, they are often limited to binary segmentation and cannot be conditioned on a natural language prompt. To this end, we propose a novel framework called ProGiDiff that leverages existing image generation models for medical image segmentation purposes. Specifically, we propose a ControlNet-style conditioning mechanism with a custom encoder, suitable for image conditioning, to steer a pre-trained diffusion model to output segmentation masks. It naturally extends to a multi-class setting simply by prompting the target organ. Our experiment on organ segmentation from CT images demonstrates strong performance compared to previous methods and could greatly benefit from an expert-in-theloop setting to leverage multiple proposals. Importantly, we demonstrate that the learned conditioning mechanism can be easily transferred through low-rank, few-shot adaptation to segment MR images.

title: Intelligent Optimal Design of Community Public Interaction Space for Aging Groups
authors: Zhicheng Zhang, Kyoung Kook Chang
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: In view of the trend of population aging, it is necessary to construct an efficient and interactive community service network to meet the needs of the aging group. In order to improve the quality of daily life of the aging population and ensure efficient allocation and utilization of resources, the study focuses on the design of community public interaction space for the aging population, aiming to enhance the efficiency and quality of the community service network through intelligent optimal design. The study is based on genetic simulated annealing algorithm for intelligent optimal design of community public interaction space for aging groups, combining the global search efficiency of genetic algorithm and the local search advantage of simulated annealing algorithm in order to seek the optimal solution. The results show that the genetic simulated annealing algorithm outperforms other algorithms in terms of the convergence speed and accuracy of the loss function, especially after 100 iterations, the value of the loss function is close to 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-5</sup>, and the accuracy rate is close to 1.0. The separation degree of the genetic simulated annealing algorithm after optimization is improved from 0.57 to 0.62 in the range of 10km, showing the effectiveness of the optimization measures. The study can significantly improve the quality of life of the elderly by improving the separation degree and service quality of community public interaction space, and provide a sustainable community development model for the aging society.

title: Cross Regulation Characteristics by Current Dip in Fly-buck Converter
authors: Younghoon Cho, Paul Jang, Jong-Won Shin
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: A Fly-buck converter is one of the multiple-output converters which has non-isolated and isolated outputs. The isolated output voltage suffers from poor voltage regulation characteristic since it is controlled indirectly. The parasitic capacitances and leakage inductance induce current dip at the non-isolated output during dead time between the high- and low-side switches, and the isolated output voltage is affected by this phenomenon. In this paper, the cross regulation of isolated voltage is analyzed. The 20-W prototype to power gate driver was built and tested to verify the analysis.

title: Overvoltage Mitigation via Variable Switching Frequency Discontinuous PWM in SiC Inverter Systems
authors: Min-Gi Kim, Kyo-Beum Lee
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This paper proposes an overvoltage mitigation via variable switching frequency discontinuous PWM in SiC inverter systems. Variable switching frequency secures zero-vector voltage in each DPWM interval to suppress the double pulsing effect. The variable switching frequency is classified into modulation for double pulsing suppression and compensation for switching. The variable switching frequency is formulated with consideration of the voltage reference in DPWM. A flowchart illustrating the proposed algorithm is presented. The validity of the proposed method is verified through simulations.

title: A Novel Dual-Stream Framework for dMRI Tractography Streamline Classification with Joint dMRI and fMRI Data
authors: Haotian Yan, Bocheng Guo, Jianzhong He, Nir A. Sochen, Ofer Pasternak, Lauren J O'Donnell, Fan Zhang
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Streamline classification is essential to identify anatomically meaningful white matter tracts from diffusion MRI (dMRI) tractography. However, current streamline classification methods rely primarily on the geometric features of the streamline trajectory, failing to distinguish between functionally distinct fiber tracts with similar pathways. To address this, we introduce a novel dual-stream streamline classification framework that jointly analyzes dMRI and functional MRI (fMRI) data to enhance the functional coherence of tract parcellation. We design a novel network that performs streamline classification using a pretrained backbone model for full streamline trajectories, while augmenting with an auxiliary network that processes fMRI signals from fiber endpoint regions. We demonstrate our method by parcellating the corticospinal tract (CST) into its four somatotopic subdivisions. Experimental results from ablation studies and comparisons with state-of-the-art methods demonstrate our approach's superior performance. Code: https://github.com/Hotel-Y/DF-NET_ISBI.git

title: Joint User Grouping and Power Allocation in RIS-Aided MISO-NOMA System under Rician Fading Channel
authors: Karima Ait Bouslam, Jamal Amadid, Fatim-Zahra Bennioui, Radouane Iqdour, Abdelouhab Zeroual
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Global Aeronautical Engineering and Satellite Technology (GAST)
abstract: For 6G and beyond, the combination of reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RIS) and non-orthogonal multiple access has shown to be a successful strategy. offering a number of benefits, such as excellent spectral efficiency (SE) and extensive connectivity. Nevertheless, the RIS-aided NOMA system’s effectiveness was still hampered by interference. In order to address this, we use a joint dynamic user grouping and power allocation-based particle swarm optimization technique (PSO) for the RIS-assisted MISO-NOMA system in this research. The SE optimization problem is then posed under stringent constraints. The chordal distance serves as the foundation for the dynamic user grouping technique. The results of the simulation evaluate how well the suggested grouping performs in comparison to current benchmark algorithms.

title: Unsupervised Domain Adaptation with Target-Only Margin Disparity Discrepancy
authors: Gauthier Miralles, Loïc Le Folgoc, Vincent Jugnon, Pietro Gori
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: In interventional radiology, Cone-Beam Computed Tomography (CBCT) is a helpful imaging modality that provides guidance to practicians during minimally invasive procedures. CBCT differs from traditional Computed Tomography (CT) due to its limited reconstructed field of view, specific artefacts, and the intra-arterial administration of contrast medium. While CT benefits from abundant publicly available annotated datasets, interventional CBCT data remain scarce and largely unannotated, with existing datasets focused primarily on radiotherapy applications. To address this limitation, we leverage a proprietary collection of unannotated interventional CBCT scans in conjunction with annotated CT data, employing domain adaptation techniques to bridge the modality gap and enhance liver segmentation performance on CBCT. We propose a novel unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) framework based on the formalism of Margin Disparity Discrepancy (MDD), which improves target domain performance through a reformulation of the original MDD optimization framework. Experimental results on CT and CBCT datasets for liver segmentation demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in UDA, as well as in the few-shot setting.

title: Sample-Based Training Data for Effective 3D Cell Segmentation
authors: Adam Smith, Till Bretschneider
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Deep learning remains the leading choice for cell segmentation, a critical step in bioimaging analysis. Whilst deep learning models provide excellent segmentation accuracy, a large number of hand-annotated samples are required which are scarce in 3D. We propose a method for generating synthetic data for training deep learning models to augment or replace such datasets. By preserving key features in real data, we show that a standard UNet trained on synthetic data can segment single motile cells with branching filopodia with high accuracy. We also demonstrate how low-effort slice annotations can sufficiently replace volume annotations in our data generation pipeline. Overall, we provide a simple alternative to annotating large 3D datasets for training neural networks to segment cell imaging data.

title: Wirelessly Powering a Cardiac Pacemaker with a Body-Coupled TENG for Self-Powered Operation and Sensing
authors: Bowang Zhang, Xin Xia, Yunlong Zi, Ping Wang, Wei Han
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This paper presents a self-powered cardiac pacemaker platform that combines body-coupled radiative triboelectric nanogenerator (TENG) wireless power transfer with passive transient parity–time (PT) symmetric sensing. A sliding freestanding triboelectric-layer (SFT) mode TENG with an integrated breakdown discharger harvests biomechanical motion as high-voltage corona discharges, modeled as an initially energized RLC resonator that acts as an antenna-like source. By optimizing the carrier to 40 MHz and leveraging human-body coupling, the radiative link achieves higher efficiency than traditional near-field wireless links. Experiments show that body-coupled transfer provides 4.3 times more energy than air-path transmission at the same distance. The harvested energy is rectified and stored, charging 4.7µF and 10µF capacitors to 17V and 7V within 13 seconds, supporting pacemaker-level power. For monitoring, the pacemaker electrodes and intracardiac blood form a variable capacitor to create an internal sensor resonator, which is inductively coupled to an external reader resonator. This coupled tank forms a transient PT-symmetric system near an exceptional point (EP). FFT analysis of the reader voltage tracks eigenfrequency coalescence and splitting, enabling battery-free detection of small capacitance changes linked to rhythm abnormalities. The proposed architecture integrates body-coupled wireless power with exceptional-point-enhanced passive sensing in a single implantable platform.

title: Identification and Assessment of Buildings Approaching the River Canals: An Integrated Geospatial Method for Conservation of Waterway Preservation
authors: Hany A. Elsalamony, Ayman Mohamed, Saeed Hamouda, Nevien Moawad, Ayman Elshenawy, Amr A. Hassanain
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Engineering Systems (ICETES)
abstract: The growing international problem of illegal construction and urban encroachment on central water infrastructure, such as river canals, has significant environmental and structural implications. This paper presents a reliable and integrated Deep Learning (DL) geospatial approach to accurately identify waterway borders in terms of detection, monitoring, and prioritization, as well as in addressing their potential negative environmental impacts, such as disrupting natural flow, increasing flood vulnerability, and harming aquatic ecosystems. The proposed approach integrates high-resolution remote sensing imagery, GIS, and a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for precise building identification. Successive proximity and risk-based evaluation systems are used via a projected Coordinate Reference System (CRS) to profile the encroachments. A more detailed case study of one such important waterway network in Egypt the CNN achieved strong detection accuracy (F1-score: 0.92, IoU: 0.855), enabling reliable identification of using our method revealed many buildings located inside the crucial 30 meter buffer area. Concentrated analysis of 500 detected structures whose risk was based on a close distance (10 -meter radius) to a household identified as high-risk, in which actions needed to be taken immediately. Quantitative risk classification of buildings into High-Risk <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\leqslant 5 \mathrm{m})$</tex>, Medium-Risk <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(5-10 \mathrm{m})$</tex>, and Low-Risk <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(10-30 \mathrm{m})$</tex> allows for prioritization in low-cost enhanced mitigation options, e.g., removal or planned relocation. This paper provides a transferable, evidence-based tool for municipal authorities and water boards to further boost their operational capacity in the maintenance of the integrity of waterscapes, which should evoke sustainable development along canal corridors.

title: Adaptive Clinical-Aware Latent Diffusion for Multimodal Brain Image Generation and Missing Modality Imputation
authors: Rong Zhou, Houliang Zhou, Yao Su, Brian Y. Chen, Yu Zhang, Lifang He
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Multimodal neuroimaging provides complementary insights for Alzheimer's disease diagnosis, yet clinical datasets frequently suffer from missing modalities. We propose ACADiff, a framework that synthesizes missing brain imaging modalities through adaptive clinical-aware diffusion. ACADiff learns mappings between incomplete multimodal observations and target modalities by progressively denoising latent representations while attending to available imaging data and clinical metadata. The framework employs adaptive fusion that dynamically reconfigures based on input availability, coupled with semantic clinical guidance via GPT-4o-encoded prompts. Three specialized generators enable bidirectional synthesis among sMRI, FDG-PET, and AV45-PET. Evaluated on ADNI subjects, ACADiff achieves superior generation quality and maintains robust diagnostic performance even under extreme 80% missing scenarios, outperforming all existing baselines. To promote reproducibility, code is available at https://github.com/rongzhou7/ACADiff.

title: Multimodal Oncology Agent for Idh1 Mutation Prediction in Low-Grade Glioma
authors: Hafsa Akebli, Adam Shephard, Vincenzo Della Mea, Nasir Rajpoot
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Low-grade gliomas frequently present IDH1 mutations that define clinically distinct subgroups with specific prognostic and therapeutic implications. This work introduces a Multimodal Oncology Agent (MOA) integrating a histology tool based on the TITAN foundation model for IDH1 mutation prediction in low-grade glioma, combined with reasoning over structured clinical and genomic inputs through PubMed, Google Search, and OncoKB. MOA reports were quantitatively evaluated on 488 patients from the TCGA-LGG cohort against clinical and histology baselines. MOA without the histology tool outperformed the clinical baseline, achieving an F1-score of 0.826 compared to 0.798. When fused with histology features, MOA reached the highest performance with an F1-score of 0.912, exceeding both the histology baseline at 0.894 and the fused histology-clinical baseline at 0.897. These results demonstrate that the proposed agent captures complementary mutation-relevant information enriched through external biomedical sources, enabling accurate IDH1 mutation prediction.

title: Gaze2Report: Radiology Report Generation via Visual-Gaze Prompt Tuning of LLMs
authors: Aishik Konwer, Moinak Bhattacharya, Prateek Prasanna
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Existing deep learning methods for radiology report generation enhance diagnostic efficiency but often overlook physician-informed medical priors. This leads to a suboptimal alignment between the structured explanations and disease manifestations. Eye gaze data provides critical insights into a radiologist's visual attention, enhancing the relevance and interpretability of extracted features while aligning with human decision-making processes. However, despite its promising potential, the integration of eye gaze information into AI-driven medical imaging workflows is impeded by challenges such as the complexity of multimodal data fusion and the high cost of gaze acquisition, particularly its absence during inference, limiting its practical applicability in real-world clinical settings. To address these issues, we introduce Gaze2Report, a framework which leverages a scanpath prediction module and Graph Neural Network (GNN) to generate joint visual-gaze tokens. Combined with instruction and report tokens, these form a multimodal prompt used to fine-tune LoRA layers of large language models (LLMs) for autoregressive report generation. Gaze2Report enhances report quality through eye-gaze-guided visual learning and incorporates on-the-fly scanpath prediction, enabling the model to operate without gaze input during inference.

title: Entropy-Guided Agreement-Diversity: A Semi-Supervised Active Learning Framework for Fetal Head Segmentation in Ultrasound
authors: Fangyijie Wang, Siteng Ma, Guénolé Silvestre, Kathleen M. Curran
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Fetal ultrasound (US) data is often limited due to privacy and regulatory restrictions, posing challenges for training deep learning (DL) models. While semi-supervised learning (SSL) is commonly used for fetal US image analysis, existing SSL methods typically rely on random limited selection, which can lead to suboptimal model performance by overfitting to homogeneous labeled data. To address this, we propose a two-stage Active Learning (AL) sampler, Entropy-Guided Agreement-Diversity (EGAD), for fetal head segmentation. Our method first selects the most uncertain samples using predictive entropy, and then refines the final selection using the agreement-diversity score combining cosine similarity and mutual information. Additionally, our SSL framework employs a consistency learning strategy with feature downsampling to further enhance segmentation performance. In experiments, SSL-EGAD achieves an average Dice score of 94.57% and 96.32% on two public datasets for fetal head segmentation, using 5% and 10% labeled data for training, respectively. Our method outperforms current SSL models and showcases consistent robustness across diverse pregnancy stage data. The code is available on GitHub.

title: End-to-End Mandibular Reconstruction with Integrated Repositioning and Completion Networks
authors: Feng Lu, Chenfan Xu, Jiacheng Sun, Chi Zhang, Min Zhu, Zhiming Cui, Dinggang Shen
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Patients with mandibular defects require mandibular reconstruction surgery to restore both physiological function and facial morphology. Accurate preoperative planning relies on a complete mandible in its correct anatomical position. However, existing methods primarily focus on morphological completion, without considering anatomical repositioning, which can result in significant issues if the defective mandible is left in a compensatory malposition. This paper presents a novel end-to-end framework that jointly performs mandible repositioning and morphological completion. In the repositioning stage, we predict rigid transformations to restore the displaced mandible to its anatomically correct location. The completion stage then restores the repositioned mandible to its complete morphology. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method surpasses state-of-the-art approaches, showing strong potential for clinical applications in computer-assisted craniofacial reconstruction.

title: Monolithic C-L-C Hybrid DC-DC Converter for High efficiency High-Density Direct 48V-Sub-1.8V Point-of-load Applications
authors: Guozhen Chang, Wing-Hung Ki
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: There is high demand for power converters with ultra-low voltage conversion ratio (VCR) for high-performance computing (HPC) and data centers.
